passages.text: Every mailbox on the street carried the Northrop Grumman logo, a company mark repeated until the neighborhood looked issued, not built. Inside, the server pinged six headsets as evening settled across the identical duplex roofs. Andrew dropped his black leather chair with the red wing decal stamped along the right armrest and pulled his headset down as the seat tightened around him.

Elena was already inside the settings menu, altering the recon drone skin no one else had unlocked, her cursor moving through nested options faster than the system wanted to display them. The others came online through static and half-finished jokes while Andrew watched the launch map assemble itself.

Capture & Delay opened above a real-world simulation grid, all roads and overpasses, and modeled wind, with two teams spawning at opposite edges of a city that had probably existed somewhere before becoming training terrain. Andrew dropped into the match without announcing a plan. He flew at a low hover, swung north, and checked the wind speed while the AI attempted to predict his arc and lagged half a beat behind his hands.
“Andrew’s already cutting corners,” someone groaned.
“He didn’t even check for spawns there,” another voice added.

Andrew kept his line, taking manual control before the system could correct him back toward the intended route. The sticks pushed back beneath his hands, one brief human moment between decision and obedience. Elena moved high without being told, filling the high lane his hands had already left open for her. The midpoint objective flashed, confirming victory in hard white letters while the squad whooped over the channel.

The next match opened in an urban canyon of glass, scaffold, rail platforms, and two elevated overpasses that narrowed the sky into strips. An armored van moved without escort through the center lane, its AI pilot choosing the safest path according to every visible condition except Andrew. He came in low from the south while Elena held the roofline, her marker steady above the van’s eastern turn, holding the blind side before he asked for cover.
“Heading eastbound,” Elena called. “Here’s where they always slip by.”
“Not this time,” Andrew said.

Chasing gave the AI a readable prediction tree. Instead, Andrew cut the throttle, drifted sideways, and placed himself where the vehicle would turn only after choosing to avoid him. The van skidded into his line and stalled against the curb, its route collapsing around a decision the system had never modeled. TARGET NEUTRALIZED blinked across his HUD while the squad noise washed through the channel.
“Andrew.” Elena’s eyes stayed on the places his hands tried to cover. “You manually wrote that path?” She heard the move before the system had a name for it.
“Yeah.”
The others kept celebrating, but Elena stayed quiet for half a beat longer.
“Nice,” she said.
After the round closed, a notification opened in the upper corner of his display, marked with a classification he had never seen before. Tier 3 Referral Flag.

Andrew let the alert fade without saying anything to his friends. The matches blurred into one another after that; each city rearranged into roads, retreat lanes, and points where the AI could fail. By the time the others logged off, the house beyond his screen had gone quiet. Elena remained connected, her avatar idle on the rooftop where his next route would normally begin.
“You were flying new lines tonight,” she said.
“I know.”
“You didn’t tell us what your plan was,” Elena said. “You cut us out.”

The classroom ran its assessment the next morning beneath soft ceiling lights and banners that changed slogans every quarter. The category was pattern anticipation, though the room speaker called it readiness recognition. Vehicle formations moved across each screen, overhead feeds shifting from convoy roads to industrial yards to half-ruined intersections. Andrew answered without listening to the assistant. His desk chimed after every correct response, a bright little reward tone beneath the ceiling lights. He kept working through the final evaluation.
From the back of the room, Elena raised her hand.
“Something’s wrong,” she said. “My agent is misreporting answers.”
Her desk pulsed once beneath the classroom lights.
“Reassessment advised,” the desk replied.
“I know the answer,” Elena said, not louder, only sharper.
Another boy leaned over his screen. “Maybe your agent’s glitched?”
Elena wadded a piece of paper and threw it across the room without looking at him.
“Maybe the agent only likes boys,” she said.

The wad struck the far wall and dropped beside a charging cart. One of the few teachers they still saw in person entered wearing a gray uniform, his collar stiff and his eyes hollow beneath the classroom lights.
“Everything alright back here?”
Elena turned to face him fully. “I’m being scored differently for the same answers.”
The teacher’s smile did not last. “Sometimes the system needs you to follow instructions.”
“That is not an answer,” Elena said.
“Let’s not have a problem here,” the teacher replied. “Answer how they want you to, Elena.”

He walked out while the door was still opening for someone else. The second man entered without scanning the room, because he already knew where to look. He wore black slacks, a contractor jacket, and polished military lace-ups. His head had been shaved down to the skin.
Elena looked at Andrew before the rest of the room moved.
“Andrew Raines,” the new man said.
Andrew stood so quickly his bag snagged against the desk chair, twisting the strap around the metal frame. He left the chair that way.
Across the room, Elena watched him. Andrew walked past the teacher’s empty desk and out through the door with his head down, looking at his shoes.

Above the administration office, a banner declared VICTORY BEGINS IN THE CLASSROOM in blue letters trimmed with silver. The man walked beside Andrew with one hand on his arm, escorting him away from his school.

Outside, a black convoy rover idled against the curb with an engine too quiet for its size. Andrew climbed into the back seat, and the door shut behind him with the sound of something sealed rather than closed. Andrew kept looking out the window and tried to roll the window down, but the button didn’t work.

The rover passed the Northrop Grumman mailboxes, the shared lawns, the identical bike racks, and the community pool where Andrew had learned to swim beneath contractor families and company flags. When the rover stopped, two technicians in lab suits stood beside the platform arguing quietly with their whisper pins. One reached for Andrew’s left wrist without asking permission. The scanner chirped against his skin, and the floor descended.

At the end of a basement corridor was a room with one metal chair and a headset. The technicians asked whether he had ever completed a neural sync. Andrew told them he had never done one before. They said most candidates at this stage were perfectly fine.

The device covered his eyes and face, and the world beyond the glass was not a game. The feed stripped away the HUD, the squad channel, the leaderboard, and Elena. Gray terrain stretched beneath a drone aloft in winter weather, with one unmarked road below and no Elena above the line to close the blind side.

A voice entered his ear to engage. The vehicle turned before the people inside knew they had made the wrong decision. He clipped the target cleanly, without fanfare, without cheering, without even the little desk chime that had followed him through school that morning. For a moment afterward, the gray terrain remained still.
The voice in his ear returned, flat and certain as a door closing. “Candidate Raines, you are confirmed,” the voice said, while the empty upper lane stayed open in his mind.

They kept him for three weeks in a room with rounded gray walls, one bed, one monitor displaying his tests, and no windows. Meals arrived through a slot in the wall. His laundry was folded at the foot of the bed for him each morning.
The tests continued during the day; at night, the room replayed him to himself. By the second week, the room chimed. “Welcome, Talon.”

They sent him home after that. His parents still spoke carefully over dinner about school, neighborhood schedules, and the company shuttle routes. The same lawn drones trimmed the same strips of grass between the same driveways. His teachers still marked attendance, his classmates still traded skins, and the public servers still filled every evening with tournaments staged brightly enough for parents to pretend they were only games.
The difference was the secondary market beneath the public servers. Some contracts were reconnaissance and harmless, but not all, and the higher payouts arrived where the feed stopped naming people.

The work came easily to Andrew, the way lungs hold oxygen underwater, until surfacing became less natural than staying down. He learned to keep his pulse low and strip every route down to braking point, apex, and exit. Weeks passed with normal school layered over private work, until Elena blocked him between class segments and refused to move. Her eyes dropped once to the oversized Northrop Grumman jacket, its factory-stiff seams making the company visible before the boy.
“Nice jacket,” she said.
Andrew looked past her toward the door.

“Andrew,” Elena said. “What you hide from the others does not work with me.” Her eyes stayed on the places his hands tried to cover. He shifted the strap of his recovered bag against his shoulder and oversized jacket. As he walked by Elena, Andrew reached for his noise-canceling headphones and put them on.

That night, he lost the first two runs without her. By the third attempt, he covered the blind side himself. The upper lane stayed empty through the end of the round. He checked twice before the score posted. The replay saved automatically, his global rank jumped, and a new invitation opened beneath the score.

Heathrow replaced its windows with layered display glass. Aircraft moved as pale silhouettes behind corporate overlays, each fuselage tagged with routing data, customs flags, carbon scores, and insurance warnings that refreshed faster than passengers could read them. Above the gate, an advertisement for reconstruction finance rotated through Mandarin characters first, with smaller English text promising resilient corridors, stable transit, and restored trade from Warsaw to the Black Sea.

Elena Morales sat beneath the board, her bag tucked under the bench and a paper ticket folded inside her passport. Around her, the terminal carried passengers through delays in narrow circuits. A British contractor argued with a baggage drone near the lift. Two embassy families clustered beneath a charging tree, children asleep against hard plastic suitcases. Near the duty-free wall, a queue wound around a Talon kiosk selling black-and-silver jackets, replica wing decals, and toy drones small enough for airport security to ignore.
The departure board blinked amber above Gate A47, then replaced the hour with a new delay marker.
FLIGHT 451 TO RZESZOW DELAYED

No weather map or mechanical explanation followed the change on the board. A smaller line advised passengers to remain near the gate for operational updates, though the gate desk was unmanned and the boarding tunnel beyond the glass stood empty. Elena turned the passport once beneath her palm, then drew the bag closer when a baggage drone routed itself around her.

A cheer rose from the lounge bar, not loud enough to cross into panic but strong enough to pull faces toward the ceiling screens. The Nations Series broadcast had replaced the delayed-flight loop above the bar. Colored gates hung over an artificial canyon rendered in sponsor light, while six racing drones waited on a suspended start line. Odds scrolled beneath each pilot's name, adjusting based on micro-bets placed by fans across the world.

The American favorite held the top lane in black and silver. His sponsor stack filled half the lower screen. Beverage brands, defense affiliates, neural interface companies, luxury shoes, and a children’s coding academy rotated beneath the pilot camera while commentators rehearsed the same astonishment with different teeth.
Elena raised the tea toward her mouth, then kept the rim just below her chin. The screen reflected across the cup lid in broken color while the countdown burned over the racecourse.

Talon entered the first gate late, high, and wrong by the line the broadcast had drawn. The Italian pilot held the center channel. The Korean drone held the inside bend. The American favorite drifted outside the safe route before the first turn was complete.
A man beside the bar laughed into his pint. “He blew the entry.”
“No,” Elena said, tightening the cup sleeve around her palm. “He is cutting under.”

The man turned, pint halfway to his mouth. He wore airport slippers and a conference badge turned backward on its cord. His whisper pin translated her words late, repeating them against his collar in a softer voice.

Talon dropped beneath the Italian lane before the replay system understood the maneuver. His drone cut throttle, slid sideways through the wash, and placed itself where the Korean line had to open or crash. The Korean pilot yielded a fraction too late. Talon passed under him, clipped the sponsor gate with one rotor, and came out level while the feed lit the move in red.
The bar broke into curses, cheers, wagers, and late translations. Betting odds scattered, reassembled, and scattered again. The man next to Elena set down his pint.
“How did you call that?”
Elena placed the tea on the small table beside her passport. “He leaves the upper lane open before he cuts.”

Onscreen, the commentators spoke over each other while the replay circled Talon’s route with bright arrows. The broadcast called the move a spontaneous lateral inversion. The second lap tightened around glass towers, scaffolds, and a split-level freight ramp modeled after some demolished port authority complex. Talon came in behind two drones with cleaner acceleration. The Japanese pilot held the obvious center, daring him to chase through the narrowest corridor.
“He has to climb,” the conference man said, leaning closer to the screen.
“And that’s why he won’t,” Elena said, lifting her bag before the strap caught beneath her boot.

Talon’s drone dipped again, not toward the lower gate this time, but toward the dead space behind a simulated billboard where the broadcast had not drawn a viable route. His rotors flashed under the billboard’s unlit service frame. The drone vanished for one frame, reappeared along the freight ramp, and used the ramp wall to mask its turn until the Italian pilot overshot the block. Talon came out behind him with the line already won.

The woman in a Polish medical jacket swore in Polish beside the counter. The whisper pins around the bar translated at slightly different speeds, making the profanity arrive in layers. Elena kept her hands at her sides while the bar erupted again.
“You race?” the conference man asked, his badge swinging against his shirt.
“Not professionally,” Elena said, keeping her hands away from the cup.
“You train with him?”
The commentator praised Talon’s calm.
Elena reached into her coat pocket and withdrew a small lighter, the old kind, not smart, not tagged, worn silver along the short end. She set it beside the tea, then pressed the passport flat.
“Because I know him.”

The conference man waited for more, but the next words never arrived. The broadcast cut from Talon’s chair to a sponsor package showing children in charter uniforms flying training drones. The Northrop Grumman mark sat on every mailbox in the staged neighborhood behind them. A smiling instructor lifted one hand toward the sky while a caption promised readiness through play.

Elena took the lighter and slipped it back into her pocket. Her passport remained on the table beneath the cold tea. The American eagle faced upward while the Talon kiosk behind her sold another jacket to a boy too young to understand the seal on the sleeve.

Across the lounge, a man in a dark coat stood beside the mirrored column near the premium exit. The crowd at the bar tracked Talon, but the man in the dark coat had his face angled toward Elena and the small table where her passport pinned the ticket.

She turned the cup once, lining the drinking slot away from the papers. The man remained beside the column with his hands visible. A bar drone crossed between them, carrying two perfect pints to the counter. When the drone cleared the lane, the dark-coated man had shifted a half step nearer to the mirror, placing himself where the glass gave him the gate, the bar, and the bench at the same time.

The race reached its final split above a rendered industrial river. Talon entered second behind a European champion with cleaner acceleration. His drone carried too much speed for the east gate, not enough for the west gate, and the broadcast geometry began drawing failure paths around him. The crowd at the bar leaned forward. Someone said he had run out of track.
Elena moved back to the bench and lifted her bag strap over one shoulder. “He is not taking either gate.”

Talon cut his engine against the river wall. The drone dropped for less than a second, then caught the rebound from the lead drone’s rotor wash and used the fall to pass through a maintenance gap the broadcast had marked as scenery. The system scrambled to rename the move before the finish line. Talon crossed first by a length.

The bar detonated into applause, wagers, curses, and translated fragments. Onscreen, the replay tried to explain the third exit with arrows and a new sponsor graphic. Elena picked up the passport, slid the ticket inside, and placed both into the inner pocket of her coat.

Behind the celebration, the reconstruction advertisement returned to the display glass above Gate A47. A high-speed rail line moved across a map in bright gold, beginning at a port marked with Chinese characters, passing through occupied Ukrainian territory, and ending in a promise of European stability. The English caption lagged behind the animation by one beat.
THE EASTERN CORRIDOR RESTORES TOMORROW

Below the caption, tiny contractor logos formed a row. One of them matched the freight tag on a crate stacked near the service door. Another matched the logistics badge worn by a man moving through the premium lane with diplomatic luggage. Elena shifted the bag higher on her shoulder. The gate board above her changed, replacing the delay marker with a green boarding notice.
FLIGHT 451 TO RZESZOW BOARDING SOON

Most passengers stayed with Talon, waiting for the interview. The first camera angle showed his chair again, its red wing decal bright beneath studio light. A host asked whether the win proved that American restraint remained the highest form of power. Talon’s reply vanished beneath terminal announcements, betting chimes, and the soft collision of whisper translations.

Elena crossed toward the lounge bar while the dark-coated man remained by the mirrored column. The column now held his reflection in three broken panels, each panel showing him in a different posture. One version held the gate, another held the bar, and the last held Elena.

A young man with a camera occupied the bench near the window, one hand around the lens cap as if the cap might leave without him. He aimed the camera toward the gate sign, adjusted it to a long focus, and missed a Polish couple passing beneath the display. His equipment still carried a retail tag hidden under the strap. The strap had already cut a red line into the side of his neck.

At the bar, two Americans sat with drinks someone had already paid for. One was broad-shouldered, loose in the stool, working a controller case open and shut with the hinge barely making a sound. The other sat straighter, coaster pinned beneath one finger, his shoes scuffed from years of pavement rather than display shelves. His eyes moved by reflection first, catching the cameraman, the dark-coated watcher, and Elena before his face turned.
The broad-shouldered one raised his glass as she reached the counter. “You headed east?”
“Yes,” Elena said, setting her bag beside the stool.
“Good. For a second there, I was worried I was standing next to someone normal.”
The other man glanced over from beside the glass. “Reese,” he said, tipping his chin toward the glass. “That’s Logan.”
“Elena.”

The bar drone slid a tea toward her before she ordered. The cup arrived centered on its saucer, steam coiling through the stale airport light. Elena tracked the drone’s return route along the counter rail, past the Guinness tap, around the service notch, and back beneath the charging dock.
Logan tipped his chin toward the screen. “You were the woman calling Talon’s moves.”
“The broadcast makes lanes for people who need them,” Elena said, wrapping one hand around the tea.
Reese turned the coaster beneath his finger. “Are you a pilot?”
“Maintenance,” Elena said, letting the cup’s heat settle.
Logan grinned into his glass without lowering his eyes. “Isn’t that what pilots say?”
“Pilots say more when they lose,” she said.

Reese’s mouth shifted at one corner, not a smile, only the smallest concession that the line had landed. Logan leaned back from the bar and made a point of checking the boarding board, though the board had not changed. The cameraman across the lounge raised his camera again and caught them this time.

An elderly woman drifted between the tables near the gate, narrow and wrapped in a threadbare coat buttoned wrong at the throat. No luggage followed her across the polished floor. No family kept pace behind her uneven steps. Her shoes made a dry, dragging sound as she moved from table to table, laying one hand on chair backs, untouched cups, forgotten bags, and the hard plastic arms of empty seats.

The woman stopped beside Elena, her hands settling over Elena’s wrist with the weight of paper laid over stone. Her skin was thin, her tendons raised, but the grip held a strange precision. She leaned close and spoke in Polish, too low for the bar to catch cleanly. The woman’s hand released her wrist, leaving a pale mark that filled slowly as blood returned beneath the skin.

The old woman moved away without waiting for thanks. The crowd accepted her absence as easily as they had missed her arrival. She passed beneath the reconstruction display, her bowed shoulders crossing the gold rail line promised through occupied ground, then disappeared behind a cluster of delayed passengers and diplomatic luggage.
Logan’s grin flattened while Reese’s coaster stopped beneath his finger. Jack, still near the window, had his camera lifted and not enough courage to press the shutter.
“What did your paperwork say?” Elena asked, setting the cup down carefully.
Reese kept the coaster pinned beneath one finger. “A liaison meets us here.”
“Name?”
“Collins.”

Across the lounge, the dark-coated man left the mirrored column at last. He crossed with controlled pace, threading through the passengers without brushing a sleeve. The bar drone reached the counter before he did, placing another beside Logan. The man stopped at the edge of their group. Jack left the window bench, both hands fixed around the camera, and took the last stool without asking. No one invited him, but the group widened around him.
“Apologies. I did not mean to interrupt,” the man said.
Logan measured the fresh Guinness and rested one elbow on the bar. “That seems unlikely.”
“I am Colonel Rupert Collins, Allied Volunteer Liaison for NATO Strategic Operations. I am here on behalf of those who approved your travel documents.”
Jack stopped short of pressing the camera switch, and no one reached for the drinks Rupert had bought them.
Rupert’s gaze passed over the four of them, then rested on Elena for one beat longer than required. “I paid for your drinks. The gesture seemed polite before expectations changed.”
“Changed how?” Reese asked, keeping the coaster pinned beneath one finger.
Rupert adjusted the cuff of his coat. “There is no American base waiting for you. When you land, you embed directly into Polish or NATO-aligned units already operating near the front.”
Logan placed the Guinness on the counter without drinking. “That is all?”
“You will likely never see another American. That is not by mistake. Small groups move faster, hide better, and make less noise inside command channels.”
The boarding board changed again, amber giving way to green.
FLIGHT 451 TO RZESZOW NOW BOARDING

The gate doors opened with a hydraulic hiss. Lights inside the tunnel shifted from amber to green, forming a narrow path with no windows and no visible aircraft beyond the bend. Passengers at other gates kept their backs turned. The Talon interview resumed above the bar, the host smiling under the sponsor light while the winning replay looped behind him.
Rupert drew a phone from his coat and tapped. “This is the class. You receive training at Camp Tadeusz in Medyka. Equipment, rules of engagement, and embedded command will meet you there,” he said.
Jack adjusted the camera strap across his neck. “You are not coming?”
Rupert returned the phone to his coat. “I am bound for Lublin. When you land, look for Slava Bohdan.”
Rupert moved back from the bar. No one reached for the drinks he had bought them, while the screen above the counter replayed Talon passing through the maintenance gap again.
Elena took the passport from her coat and opened it to the ticket. The blurred RZESZOW line lay over the American seal, its raised wing still visible beneath the paper.

She placed the untouched tea beside Logan’s Guinness, lifted her bag, and walked toward the gate. Reese crossed from the bar without ceremony, Jack followed with the camera held in both hands, and Logan drained the Guinness in one draw before joining the line.

At the scanner the gate drone read her passport. The device chirped, accepted the volunteer clearance, and projected a green passage mark across the floor. A Talon toy drone crossed overhead from the duty-free kiosk, piloted by a laughing boy in a black-and-silver jacket. The toy clipped the edge of the reconstruction display, recovered badly, and continued toward the charging tree.

Elena crossed through the scanner and entered the boarding tunnel beneath green lights. Behind her, the lounge kept its races, its perfect drinks, its delayed flights, and its gold rail lines crossing maps that did not belong to the people under them. Ahead, the boarding bridge curved left, narrowing toward the aircraft door. At the bend, the window glass gave back one last reflection. Four Americans, unevenly spaced, moved forward beneath green boarding lights.

They were in the same volunteer class. So far, that was all they shared. She knew the most about what people cared about. By the time Jack arrived, she was already making friends even before their first breakfast in Poland.

The Nations Series opened in the Pacific window, and Camp Tadeusz arranged the morning around the drone races. The biggest screen hung above a makeshift bar with a bookie drone taking bets, where everyone came together. Officers held the back wall with paper cups cooling in their hands. Mechanics in oil-dark coveralls crowded beneath the television. Medics leaned against the serving counter beside trays of powdered eggs.

Cooks watched through steam while contractors in civilian fleeces stood between Polish infantry, Romanian engineers, Italian artillerymen, French recon, British signals, Baltic volunteers, and the three Americans from Jack’s recruitment class. Rank stayed visible on sleeves and collars, but the room had turned toward flags, odds, pilot colors, and the old animal pleasure of watching your side beat someone else’s.

The American favorite sat at the top of the board in black and silver, his sponsor stack wider than the names beneath him; he entered the second gate late, wrong by every line the broadcast had drawn, then dropped beneath the Italian lane. The Italian drone clipped the pylon spray and spun away in pieces.

Half the mess hall cursed before the replay started, while the other half slapped tables and pointed at the screen as if the move had been obvious all along. Beneath the duty board, the prop machine changed every market before the wreckage stopped moving.

The same light had filled his parents’ kitchen for years, turning disaster into panels and commercial breaks. Jack crossed the room before another expert could begin and switched the television off. He ignored the veteran soldiers for a battered box of sugar cereal beside powdered milk, and built himself a bowl beside the men eating sausage and black bread, old enough to have children his age.

He lied on the application and during the phone interview. At Heathrow, Jack stood between a digital camera that promised automatic upload and a film camera locked behind the counter with two rolls taped to the box. He tried the digital model first, its display squaring every face in the aisle, then set it back on the shelf. The film camera cost more than he expected and came with a sticker that read ‘WITNESS,’ which Jack threw away with the receipt before locating the small American Foreign Legion group gathered at the airport bar. From London was a short flight to war.

The NATO volunteer intake system wasn’t hard to beat, even without an official State Department endorsement. When the acceptance email arrived, the recruiter didn’t even request follow-ups or references. The AFL pulled volunteers through NATO-adjacent channels to fill the gaps that official deployment couldn’t.

Jack kept his shoulders squared as the nylon strap sawed into his collarbone and forced him to overcorrect his grip. Jack’s camera hummed to life, green LED pulsing, as he left the mess hall and panned across the yard of Camp Tadeusz, which sat near Medyka, close to the Polish border and now pushed deeper by the Russian advance.

The center of camp was a low-slung warehouse reeking of diesel and damp wool, its concrete walls bleeding old smoke chill through the camouflage netting. A half-buried statue of a Polish statesman who won his country’s independence after World War I stood frozen mid-gesture beside a stack of gutted old drone chassis. At the base, Jack read the name. Józef Piłsudski. Jack framed the fiber-optic cabling draped across the statue's neck. Józef’s outstretched hand raised above the jittering silhouettes of medics arguing over a mislabeled crate.

Two soldiers passed near the statue, but Jack fumbled with the aperture ring. The figures drifted out of frame before he could press the shutter. A breeze lifted a thread from a shattered light pole. The cable shimmered, hung a moment, then dropped onto a field table. A volunteer nudged the wire aside with the back of his hand as if the wiring would bite the hand that kept the signal going.

Jack moved the lens slowly, lingering over a long-abandoned pierogi stall. The chalkboard menu, still legible, once offered dumplings with meat, potatoes, and mushrooms. Someone had scrawled across the board in English: Closed for War, the paint half-washed by rain. Jack pressed the shutter; beyond the stand, near the duty display case, a group of fighters clustered around a folding counter. The warehouse smelled of grease, gun oil, and coffee. Jack adjusted focus. A resistance emblem flickered in the viewfinder, a small Kotwica carved into a beam near the ceiling, old enough that Jack could not tell which occupation had left it.

The morning air carried a tang of slow-burning wood and fuel from the east. The front shifted daily, but only by inches. Three kilometers in six weeks, less than the width of the camp itself. Direct conflict hadn’t reached Medyka yet; as Jack drifted through the area, movement flickered in every corner of the camp. Drones passed overhead, their buzz folded into the camp's rhythm.

Jack’s lens tracked a drone dropping crates of bandages onto a cart, another zipped low to deliver food packs to a medic tent, while a third processed retinal scans. A soldier spat a thick wad of tobacco next to the rations. “They feed the whole front from here,” he said.

The lens was the perfect shield; while he was shooting, none of the other soldiers expected him to make conversation. Jack’s camera lingered next on a group of soldiers gathered around the medical kits. Drones whirred away after their delivery; through the lens, dried blood and masking tape marked one of the kits.

The main highway out of town had broken pavement and rebar wire veins, but the green customs arch still hung overhead. Underneath, two soldiers stood close, trading folded bills for morphine ampoules. Jack kept the camera at his side and walked past.

Jack wandered beyond the active loading lanes until the engines, shouted orders, and metallic racket of the working depot thinned behind him. The warehouse ahead opened vast enough to mute war. Rows of allied drone frames swayed beneath hanging dust sheets and dead work lights, their silhouettes receding out of view. Along one wall, a coat rack stretched nearly the length of the warehouse with enough hooks for a hundred mechanics, though only a single oil-black jacket hung beside a dented thermos gone cold.

An older Ukrainian mechanic worked alone beneath a suspended chassis, surrounded by machines still covered in factory protection film. At the same time, nearer the open loading doors, patched drones with mismatched panels and replacement arms lifted one by one toward the front. Jack raised the camera toward the dormant rows. The mechanic stopped wiping his hands on a rag blackened with grease and said something in Ukrainian.

Jack strolled back to the city center runching frost. Around him, some soldiers wore NATO fatigues scavenged from old supply depots. Others had no flag at all, just patched sleeves and distant eyes. One wore a New Jersey VFW cap. Another had scrawled “AFL” in black marker on his vest. Jack skimmed back through his pictures from the morning. Hours of it had worn a raw stripe across his collarbone. He kept the camera raised. Near a stack of crates, Jack spotted three of his own recruitment class, one already busy, two more leaning against a supply truck.
“Yeah, this is a good mess,” a volunteer in Jack’s recruitment class said, tightening her ponytail. “Half the world’s pretending this isn’t happening,” she said.
Reese O’Malley stretched on the side of a supply truck, arms crossed, his body’s movement never stopping. He spoke with a half-smile. “Elena, we have everything we need right here.”
“We wouldn’t be here if that was true.”
Jack zoomed in on Reese's half-smile. Then came Logan, the third volunteer who had traveled with Jack from London, carrying himself with the delicate balance of a drunk who hadn't spilled his glass.
Elena worked a mechanic’s wrench across the bolts of a damaged drone. “Almost lunch, Logan,” she said.
Logan grinned and patted the drone controller clipped to his back. “Clocked in an hour before Jack did. He’s late, not me.”
Reese watched Logan study the treeline a moment longer, then asked, “You good if we need you to fly?”
Logan finally looked back at him. “Always.” He held Reese’s eye a beat too long after saying it.

Logan clapped Reese on the shoulder and strode toward the staging area, vanishing into the morning mist that clung to the supply crates. From the east end of camp, a matte-black recon drone groaned to life on its vertical launch rack, launching with a hiss as its pistons ground. The camp watched the Polish recon drone serve as a carrier, its undercarriage loaded with smaller attack drones as it climbed eastward toward the horizon.
Jack locked the lens onto an older soldier at the center of Camp Tadeusz. The rugged commander stood at the front, cleared his throat, and the camp went quiet.
His face was sunburned, his eyes clear and alert. “Rookie squads. Today, you march,” he said.
“Where are we going?” Logan asked Jack.
The older man in generalist fatigues heard Logan and addressed him personally. “To the front. To see and smell this war.”

Jack’s lens locked onto the broken ridges of the man’s hands, tracing raw burn scars and a freshly split scab. The other soldiers gave the commander small, informal nods while his gray hair shifted against the dark green kit. His eyes cut to Jack’s camera. “Film all you want, just remember, the war’s not watching back.” He turned toward the gate. “Rookies with me, move out.”
The other volunteers formed a rough line. A veteran’s low voice reached him from near the gate. “That’s Slava Bohdan.”

The murmurs rippled through the group; as they left camp, the gate clanged shut behind them, the sound carrying down the dirt road ahead. Slava led the column forward; beyond the camp, they passed under hastily assembled coverage nets, poly-tents stretched and stitched across the road. Cheap plastic was the only cover between them and the sky as they passed the camp perimeter.
Logan ducked low beneath the stitched-together tents. “What a joke. We’re hiding,” Logan said.
Up ahead, Slava didn't break pace but shouted back. “The war’s different now. Six feet is your life.”

A shard of cover material fluttered in the frame, warped plastic bending the light across the covered ground. Jack twisted the focus ring and filmed a quick pan of the line behind him. Then he steadied the frame, holding the road until the formation stretched thin inside the lens.
Elena kept pace behind Slava. Her gaze tracking the drone’s lazy, mechanical orbit. “That one’s on a pre-programmed loop," she said. "Logging fallback routes.”
Slava didn’t glance over. "No hand at the wheel," he said.
"Until an override," Elena said.
That made Slava look at her, and his mouth quirked. "A rare smart American," he said.
Elena kept going, not breaking stride. “I grew up with drone pilots, but most of us kept the maintenance bays warm.”
Slava’s attention returned to the drone overhead. “Useful skills,” he said.
The drone’s hum faded with the wind behind them. Reese kept his rifle slung, hands in his pockets. Elena and Logan walked side by side as the trail opened into the vast countryside.
Jack followed a few paces behind, camera low, as the volunteers quieted in the morning light. “Lots of folks out here?” Logan asked, gesturing toward the scattered silhouettes of soldiers ahead.
Slava chuckled. “Government’s not. But that doesn’t mean a few good individuals can’t step up.”
Elena pointed. “Look at the line there. Trenches built by different groups at different times. They don’t match up.”
“Yeah, well, they expected to have the US Air Force with them,” Logan said.
“Too busy domestically to be on this continent, Logan,” Jack replied.

Ahead of them at the next treeline, they passed a crate of medical gear. Elena reached out and took a bundle from the crate. She didn’t open the packet; she turned the kit once in her gloved hands. “Did we even bring what we need?” she asked as she pocketed the extra supplies.
“Brought what we could,” Logan replied, brushing a fogged spot off his lens.

Jack exited the treeline and was greeted by fields covered in railroad steel. Black iron crosses, welded from beams thick enough to build stations, stood in irregular rows, stretching farther than his camera could hold.

They were only beyond the first few of these before Slava continued talking. “You know, I saw traps bigger than even these in the Donbas. Very impressive from the air. But there’s always a way around, if you know where to look.”
Jack gave up trying to fit them all into a single frame. “So they’re not actually that effective?”
Slava adjusted his rifle strap with one hand, brushing ice from the collar of his coat.
Slava gave a nod. “Perimeters don’t mean all that much when the enemy’s inside your head. This war isn’t just about ground.”

Slava moved through the rusted tank traps with a full stride as the rest of the column filed after him. Jack lagged behind them all, adjusting the focus on his feed lens. Ahead of him, Logan veered toward Reese, drone remote loose in one hand, an unlit cigarette resting in the other.
“Reese, did you ever hear the one about Christmas?” Logan said. “In the first big war, the front stopped fighting on Christmas Eve."
“I’ve heard the story,” Reese said.
Reese lifted his hat and tucked his cap into his coat pocket. “A lot has changed, but the songs are the same,” he said. His voice was flatter than usual.
Logan raised an eyebrow. “How do you mean?”
Reese’s eyes stayed forward as they kept pace following Slava. “I took a job I shouldn’t have back home.”
Reese said, avoiding eye contact. “Before we went in, our brief was crowd control. Management didn’t want what happened during the ‘Hallelujah’ part.”

Jack took the camera off and the nylon strap peeled from his neck. The skin beneath was scored with small red indentations from the day’s weight, a private injury from hiding behind the lens too long.

“They said the problem was when everyone stood up together. I’d never seen the performance before, and most of the federal agents hadn’t either. There were nearly two hours before that moment in the show. After the arrests, a lot of guys called that night The Last Messiah. Doing that kind of thing, standing without being told to, became worse than being undocumented.”
Logan held his drone in both hands while Jack kept his eyes on the ground. Ahead, the trail bent into a stand of stripped birch. Logan started passing the drone chassis back and forth.
Reese continued. “That’s why I quit.”

Jack pivoted his lens to the hill. A figure moved across the land, half-sunk to the waist. Jack adjusted focus and panned left. Click. He focused on the camouflaged netting and loose brush. Click. With each shot, the trench’s pattern became more obvious. Jack advanced to higher ground, trying to frame the whole curve.
Elena moved forward. “Slava, any word out of Ukraine? Kyiv?”
“Nothing honest, only performances. Kyiv’s been a closed circuit for years now.”
“There must be survivors?”
“We’ve heard rumors.”

That was all he said about that. In the forest ahead, splinters marked where trunks used to be, branches sheared away, some trees standing without tops, their crowns amputated and cores exposed in jagged spirals.
“We’ll keep fighting,” Elena said, not loud, but confident. “For everyone else who won’t.”
Slava’s hand came to rest on her shoulder. “Most lack the spirit,” he said. “That’s why those who can fight must fight.”

Jack widened the frame and caught Logan at the edge of the road, one knee in the frost, lining up spent casings in the dirt. He set three close together, dragged another around their flank, then tapped the rear casing finding the blind side.
Slava turned his head enough to study the little formation. “You think war is running and shooting?”
Logan swept the casings into his palm and stood. “Eventually, I’m planning to shoot a Russian, yes,” Logan said.
Slava grunted and pointed past the rise. “Look.”

Jack shifted to follow his finger; the lens stuttered, caught on a jagged shape in the brush. A burned-out APC. Russian markings. Scorched metal, one tread sunk into the dirt after a turn too late. Jack zoomed in on the skeletal frame of a blackened vehicle, abandoned but not empty.
“Those remains were a scout team,” Slava said. “This war doesn’t move in straight lines,” he said. “A tide that pulls back, then rushes forward. Too many allies and enemies washed away.”

Jack kept the camera low as they resumed walking, the light shifting over the trail. The lens dropped toward the ground, where each step crunched with brittle, crystalline resistance and sterile chemical cold bit through his boot soles.
Elena crouched and dragged her hand through the dirt. Jack zoomed to catch the motion. “Salted,” she said.
Slava kept walking without turning back. “They’ll do anything that works.”
Jack held the focus on Elena’s hand as she lifted a pinch of the earth, letting the white dust fall. “All this for the plants?”
Slava veered left, revealing a narrow trench carved across the field. Jack panned to follow it; fiber-optic lines nested in bundles along the trench walls, a web left by unjammable trailing drones.
“Nature wants to take back what nature can,” Slava said. “Dead ground holds.”
Jack’s lens flicked up as Reese blinked behind him. “They sterilized the fields to run cable?”
“Interference shielding?”
Slava brushed white dust from his palm. “Still Earth, yes, that’s their policy.”
The group’s pace shifted without an order, while the drone hum stayed under every step. The land natural only in outline at a distance beneath the drone hum.
Slava raised a hand, halting the line. “Now, watch.”

Jack zoomed in on a lattice of machines, sharp-edged and black against brittle blue. They edged forward with measured precision; a legion of drones moved in formation, black and numberless, filling the farthest edge of sky. The horizon full.
“They don’t sleep,” Slava said. “This is the front.”

Jack pulled back and zoomed out completely as the swarm coalesced into a single dense shape against the horizon.  He tracked left, panned slowly, keeping the whole horizon in shot. There were tens of thousands of them. He held the shot long enough for the sun to drop the amount he needed. He pulled back from the eyepiece, the swarm's perfect symmetry nauseating to see.
Elena climbed onto the bluff for a clearer view. Her attention stayed on the swarm longer than anyone else’s. “How do you break a swarm that size?” she asked.
Slava turned to her with harder lines than before. “You don’t fight them,” he said. “You live long enough to find the pilots and kill them in their sleep.”

He walked at the head of the formation, eyes scanning past them into the darkening rise. One by one, the others followed, easing toward the lee of a collapsed brick wall. Slava moved among them, giving out shoulder taps. They all drank water without removing gear and kept their backs to the warm side of the ruin, eyes trained on the treeline and the half-black sky.

Slava drew a cloth along the length of his knife before sliding the blade back into its sheath. For a moment, he only watched the swarm, the cloth still bunched in his fist. Then he said, “Enough for today.”

The column began its return, but Elena did not. She stood at the edge of the hill, eyes locked on the swarm. Below her, the ground stretched in a slush of salt and snow, with glass-veined fiber, dead earth into circuitry. Reese doubled back; he saw her, too, and stood with her until she decided to  leave. Then together they walked back toward the others as the column filed out. Jack held the frame a moment longer, the swarm still pulsing across the sky.

Then a fold in the darkened horizon stirred. One shape became three, and three became twenty. Drones surfacing behind drones, a second swarm tucked inside the first. A cluster of smaller drones peeled off from the main body and dipped low, moving with fluid, automated precision. They latched onto a larger carrier drone, a matte-black, beetle-shaped hub. One by one, the smaller drones docked into the undercarriage beneath the ribcage. A syncopated click prompted a soft hiss as they began refueling. Jack kept shooting. The docking process looked careful, almost tender, and worse for that.

The cold came up through the road into Reese’s knee. The old wound answered before the eye took measure of the ground. The ache moved behind the kneecap and down through the tendon, and his next step failed by a half. He shortened his stride and kept his shoulders square.

Logan moved past with his controller active, the drone scouting ahead. Jack carried the camera forward in both hands, guarding the glass with a medic’s care. Elena walked two steps ahead, looking at the cables threaded through the cold ground.
“Is this a defensive line?” Reese asked.
Ahead, Slava walked without turning. “They don’t hold ground,” he murmured. “They build rats’ nests. They spin webs.”

The cable grit cracked beneath their footfalls while, in the distance, an old wooden chapel stood half-burned, its steeple missing. The wind carried the faint toll of a bell, probably loose in its frame, knocking dully against itself.
"Hold still, you little shit," Reese muttered, adjusting for the drone's erratic flight.
Ice cracked at the edges where the current still moved. Reese adjusted for the glare.
"I'm not missing," Reese said, eyes still locked through the scope. "Fly straight, man."
"That's why you're losing." Logan jerked the drone into a sudden dive, twisting just before clipping Reese's helmet.
"You flinched!" Logan said.
"Bullshit." Reese reset his stance.
Logan wagged a finger at him. "Little twitch, right there. I got you."
Reese rolled his shoulders, locking the stock into his pocket. "First one to blink hauls the rucks back to Medyka."
They kept pace with the column, Logan spiraling the drone, Reese tracking.
"You'd make a terrible sniper," Logan said, quiet enough that only Reese heard.
"And you make a terrible pilot," Reese said.
Logan dropped the drone's altitude, its rotors whipping up a frantic spray of ice crystals as it skimmed the frozen river.
Elena kept stride a few paces behind. She reached forward without breaking pace, adjusted two settings on Logan's controller.
Elena's gaze stayed on the drone. "Back in California, kids started young with drone competitions instead of baseball. If you weren't flying, you were learning to keep them flying."
Logan flicked his drone higher, one eyebrow lifting toward Reese. "Tragic."
Reese kept walking. "Scholarship shortstop."
Jack looked over. "Seriously?"
Reese shrugged beneath his pack. "Before the tendon went."
Logan grinned. "Now you fly?"
Reese watched Logan’s drone climb, wobble, then recover. "Still read a field better than you read a feed."
Logan barked a laugh. "Bullshit."
Reese adjusted his rifle sling without looking at him. "Pick a test."
"Anywhere?"
"Anyplace."
Logan flicked his drone higher. "You flew?" he said to Elena.
She shook her head once. "For a while before maintenance. My friend Andrew flew."

Logan smacked the heel of his hand against the controller. His fix for everything; the drone buzzed overhead, slicing the cold air before juking right and dropping into sudden freefall. Logan cursed and corrected the controls, pulling up just before slamming into the dirt.

Ahead of them, an open field stretched toward a ruined farmhouse at the treeline. The grass trimmed for farming, practical and distinctly non-military. Reese's pace hitched. He dropped the safety on his rifle. A scarecrow slumped, wearing an old Polish military helmet on a rotting post. The scarecrow's coat fluttered in the breeze.
Behind him, Logan asked, "You good?"
Reese tapped the receiver of his rifle.
Logan switched to thermal.
Slava's voice cut through the cold air. "Enough playing."
Reese mirrored Slava's halt as Slava's rifle snapped up.
"Something is off," Slava said, just loud enough for them to hear.
Elena went still beside him.
Reese edged closer. He'd seen that expression before contact.
"Tracks," Slava murmured.
Reese widened his stance, gaze locked on the treeline.
"Should we be seeing our own patrols by now?" Logan muttered.
"Spread out. Slowly."
Reese dropped into a half-crouch, weapon up, eyes scanning the buildings ahead, the scorched supply depot, and the carcasses of burned-out vehicles.

Dry brush snapped near the treeline as Slava turned just as the shot cracked and the treeline erupted. Reese moved on pure reflex, body-checking Logan into the dirt. He was already past him, one hand flat on Logan's back, before planting his feet the way he had a thousand times at short, turning a double play. He raised his rifle and fired.
"Move!" Reese shouted. "Wake the hell up!"

Logan ran. Reese pivoted, dropping behind an old transport frame for cover. Rounds tore past without warning as Slava barked commands in Ukrainian, and the allied soldiers scattered and returned fire.

Reese dropped low, rifle moving from windows to ditch mouths to the broken treeline as hot dirt sprayed across his neck. From the corner of his eye, he caught Jack diving for cover behind the truck's axle, camera still clenched in one hand. Across the open trail, movement. Figures were breaking the treeline, moving fast through the smoke.
Reese was already moving before anyone else. "Logan!"
Logan already had the drone up. A round tore past, and Reese dropped behind the truck's axle, the ringing sharp.
"Get to cover!" Slava yelled, his voice ragged now.

Jack was still there, crouched low, with his camera where his weapon should have been. Elena had pressed into a broken wall, eyes moving from treeline to rubble in short, deliberate arcs. The same pattern she used on maps.

Their uniforms were stitched from scraps, and their helmets sat too loosely on their heads. Elena's rifle faltered for a half-second. She saw them before Reese did. Reese couldn't tell who fired first as muzzle flashes flickered down the line and Elena's rifle kicked against her shoulder again and again. The first went backward. Another crumpled. A third kept running after the bullets took his legs, then crawled.
"They're not trying to break the line," Reese said, though no one heard. One after another, a tide of bodies moved forward.

Elena kept firing. For a half-second, her trigger finger didn't pull. A screamer drone passed directly overhead, its harmonic raking the air. The last boy screamed; she pulled the trigger a second time, silencing him. Reese shifted behind her, his palms slick, blinking away sweat and gun-smoke. He sighted and fired short bursts; these were real professional soldiers this time, outfitted in gleaming gear. The wall vibrated with each shot.

He dropped two advancing mercenaries; the second man wore factory-clean Talons on the wrong feet. Reese held on the boot sole for one beat before reloading. Then the man staggered and dropped, a marionette without strings.
He slammed a fresh magazine home and moved low, following Logan's voice.
"Reese, they're dropping more in!"
Logan hunched over his tablet, flying and reading at the same time, the way only he could. The recon drone darted through the parachuters, weaving intel out of madness.

Logan shoved the tablet toward Reese without lifting his eyes from the screen. A bright thermal bloom washed out the screen as a NATO counter-strike hit a Russian flank. As the static cleared, armor surged on the eastern corridor. Logan flew higher, the feed showing columns of tracked vehicles raising dust before the drone swarm eclipsed them entirely.
Logan pinched the screen wider, and the horizon moved, his drone feed shifting east. Tens of thousands of black drones swarmed in one synchronized direction.
"Cover your ears!"
Logan sprinted toward them, ripping foam plugs from his pouch and hurling them at Reese. Reese shoved the buds into his ears, and when Logan fumbled with his, he reached over and pressed them in.

Slava arrived, Elena on his flank, Jack behind with the camera. Reese followed her line just in time to see the drones banking hard overhead. Slava dropped to a crouch and clamped both hands over his ears.

Smaller swarms separated from the main group, swooping in tight arcs through the matte-gray screamers, not to attack but to howl. Palm-sized, and the pitch was a locust-swarm frequency that set teeth on edge. These drones didn't fire or self-destruct. They turned collective speed into a wave of sound that was more than noise. Together at scale, they changed the air around them, a harmonic overload rupturing unprepared inner ears.

The sound alone was the bombing run as the screamers worked in diving waves, flying within inches of each other, a choir of machine anguish tearing through the air, calibrated for maximum biological disruption. Reese staggered even with the earbuds in, feeling his ribs vibrate. Around him, those without warning clutched their heads, blood pouring from their ears in thin arterial threads.

Polish infantry, medics, and hardened NATO veterans trembled across the frozen ground. Some clawed at the whisper pins on their collars, trying to send final messages through mouths that could no longer shape words. Others dropped their rifles and pressed both hands to their skulls while their agents blinked unanswered against their throats. A man with a Swedish patch who didn’t have earplugs bit through his own tongue.

The Russian infantry waded through the writhing defenders in a blitz. They worked with a deliberate, butchering efficiency, driving bayonets into the fallen to conserve ammunition. Reese did not look away as the battle in front of him transformed from a clash of weapons and positions into the eternally irreversible. Logan's hands were still on the screen. For now, their position held, though the line kept shrinking around them.
"We can't stay."
Ahead, Slava stood firm in the dust and firelight, barking orders into the smoke. Slava's eyes swept the line.
Beside him, Jack knelt, the lens jerking in raw fragments. Trying to record their collapse, or just trying to understand the collapse.
"They boxed us in!" Reese shouted, ducking low as rounds chewed through the frozen metal behind him.
Elena's rifle fired over his shoulder. "We need to fall back!"

Slava stood still, rifle raised. A Russian soldier broke from the treeline beside their position. Slava took him down with a single, practiced shot. Another soldier flanked from the right; Slava turned but was half a second too late. The second Russian rifle shot tore into Slava's side.
"NO!"

Someone shouted. Reese couldn't tell if it was Jack, Logan, Elena, or himself. He stood still, hands dropping from his rifle. Slava dropped to one knee, stabilizing himself in the dirt with his rifle still raised but unsteady. Slava raised a heavy, blood-soaked hand, stopping him.
"Don’t be foolish," he said.
A screamer drone tore low over the position, its harmonic cutting through the smoke. By the time Reese’s eyes came back to Slava, the man's rifle was already rising.
Elena reached Slava just as he slid back against the rubble wall, one hand pressed hard to his side. Blood darkened the fatigues; his rifle hadn't dropped.
She knelt and grasped his shoulder, catching a smirk.
"We hold the line with you."
Slava shook his head and forced himself upright.
"That's why you have to go."

His rifle snapped back into his shoulder as he squeezed short, surgical bursts, dropping three men before the final click echoed empty. Slava surged forward. He dropped his spent rifle mid-stride, drew his sidearm, and racked the slide. The heavy pistol hammered the air, dropping targets until the slide locked back.

The Russians hesitated, and Slava dropped the empty pistol before grabbing a short, serrated knife from his belt. Slava charged ahead, a flash of motion, leaving maimed enemies in his wake. As he advanced, a rifle butt struck Slava's back, forcing him to his knees as the enemy closed in around him.

Reese followed Elena, Jack, and Logan away from Slava's advance, looking back once despite himself. Slava's outline disappeared inside the mass of bodies. Ahead, Elena kept moving while Jack and Logan stumbled behind her. They escaped west with rifles loose, no commands, no words between them. A woman struggled, her arm bandaged in plastic, dark liquid seeping from the stitches.

A matte-black ground robot moved past on six wheels, pulling a sled with tagged bodies, its speaker chirping in Polish and English amid the destruction. Reese's pace slowed for one step; then he kept walking.
"Platinum window expired. Transfer priority, transfer priority. Platinum window expired."
The machine scanned each gurney with a laser pulse. The sequence flashed red, red, amber, red. The drone continued at a rapid pace, nearly keeping up with the group.
Reese didn't stop. The drone beside them scanned a man collapsed in the mud, but this time it chirped, sprayed a DNR mark on his neck, and slipped on.
Reese's eyes stayed on the mark until the robot rolled on.

The air choked them with the stench of scorched cordite, ruptured bowels, and iodine. Blood-soaked tourniquets lay discarded. Elena met Reese's eye. She was watching a drone load a twitching body onto a gurney. Logan was quiet, still scanning the sky, still flying recon overhead. Down the road, the retreating army converged, and the four of them gathered around a burn barrel, in a narrowing where everything broken pooled.

A cluster of soldiers blocked the road ahead, their sleeves marked by Czech, Polish, and Ukrainian patches half-buried under mud. Their uniforms matched no single army, but the stains matched the Americans.
"Americans?" They didn't sound happy, and Reese didn't want to answer.
"You're the ones from Medyka," the soldier continued. "Slava was at the front with you."
Elena addressed them. "I'm sorry, soldier, Slava fought to the end."
"Sorry? Slava died because of you."
Another soldier joined in.
"You were the reason they brought the big toys," the first soldier said. "You painted a target on us."

The circle tightened around them; overlapping accusatory voices erupted in Polish and Ukrainian. Soldiers, medics, and able-bodied wounded gathered, shoulders pressed together. The crowd compressed, one man shouted right in Reese's face, spittle flying. Another raised a weapon, threatening to strike. The old ache returned to Reese's hands. He was ready to fight if he had to.
Then a new voice cut through the roar.
"Enough."

A woman emerged from a medical tent, wearing light gear, rifle slung muzzle-down and forward, her right hand resting on the grip the way other people rested their hands in pockets. Her eyes moved across the crowd in the fast, slotting way of someone filing positions, not faces. She spoke softly, not needing to raise her voice. The crowd quieted as she passed.
"Confirm your affiliation. Are you Rupert's assets?" she demanded.
Reese said yes. Her gaze moved on to Elena, then Logan, then Jack. Quick, assessing, as if slotting them into a plan already half-made.
"Your deaths were reported as the battle began. How did you survive?"
She brought her forefinger to her lips and blew a sharp whistle, just high enough to cut through the background hum of machines and groans. Movement stirred behind the medical tent.

The first to emerge was a short, heavyset man biting into a ration bar while he watched the skirmish de-escalate. A third figure followed, tall and lean, with a rifle slung low beneath a long coat and a French-made weapon resting easy in his hands.
Reese tightened his grip on his rifle. The woman didn’t take her eyes off them.
"You will come with us,” she said.

For the first time since Slava died, someone else was in control. The night was quiet, a fire burning low, a small flickering circle of light in the vast, cold dark. One by one, they dropped their gear, pulling in toward the warmth.
The short man spoke, rough and low, English decent but just enough. "I'm glad you four survived. Many did not."
"Long day," the wiry French sniper replied to the short man.
The woman set down her pack but didn't sit. Arms crossed, watching the treeline for the thing the treeline wasn't showing. "A tragedy."
Logan let out a bitter chuckle. "You guys do this every day?"
Felix's calloused hand checked his sidearm. "For many years."
Reese took in the three of them. "I don't think we've actually done this yet."
Jean Pierre unscrewed his dented flask. "Done what?"
Reese shifted his weight and gestured lazily. "Introductions later."
Anika's gaze flicked to him, her expression hardening. "Not necessary."
"You want me calling you 'Polish lady' all night?" Reese asked, moving closer to the fire.

The short man said, "I used to think war was about fighting. Turns out, it's just about outlasting the other bastard." He drove a calloused hand over his scalp. "Don't call her 'Polish lady,' boy, aren't you a drone pilot? She'll slit your throat in your sleep."

The tall man rested his hand on Felix's shoulder, his weathered cloak still muddy from the road. "Jean Pierre Marchand. French Army, Foreign Legion. Sniper." He cracked his wrists with a twist of them both, the sound sharp as a misfire. "I shoot fast, move fast, and get paid slow."
Reese noticed the missing whisper pin before he remembered her name. The third woman studied them for another second. Then, with a dry exhale, she said, “Anika Kowalska. For now, just Anika.”
Logan leaped forward. "Logan Brooks. Our eyes in the sky, trying to keep this group out of trouble."
Jean Pierre snorted. "How did you do today?"
Elena threaded forward and shook hands with each of them. When she reached Anika, she straightened as if reporting in. "Elena Morales," she said. "What's next?"
Anika's smile flickered. She raised a hand low at her side, giving a small wave that stopped Elena mid-step.
The camera had not come down. “I’m Jack. Do you think some of this gets past the firewalls back home?”
The fire burned low as Jack opened his ration kit. He didn't eat anything.
Felix spoke. "You barely knew Slava." He watched the fire. "We knew him for a lifetime."
Jean Pierre tapped the stock of his French MAS rifle. "We fought in the Donbas. Before that, Crimea. We held the line together in Kharkiv." His hands tightened around a dented metal flask.
Anika stirred the fire, embers drifting up.
Jack flipped open his camera as he spoke next. "I captured Slava's final moments."
Heads turned finding spots over Jack's shoulder to watch the footage.
Felix rolled a shotgun shell vibrating with a gravelly hum. He shook his head. "That bastard."
Jean Pierre capped his flask. "You're jealous."

The others laughed around the fire, but Anika gave them nothing. She watched the footage of Slava dropping to his knees, knife still in hand. Jack let the recording run a few more seconds, until the frame held only the space where Slava had been.
"We take this to Lublin." Her voice cut through the air. "The enemy broadcast your deaths. We can weaponize their lies. We will go to Lublin to structure the counter-offensive."

Reese followed the clatter of toolboxes and metal work toward the garage. Each person scrambled for supplies; behind the medical tent stood a garage, two jeeps, and no mechanics. Anika crossed past the gutted jeep, pulled a welder off the rack, shouldered the cord, and kicked the generator that sputtered, then caught. Dropping to a knee beside the second jeep, she worked fast, tightening bolts, replacing sheared brackets, and heat-flashing seams. Sparks spat across the concrete while the metal popped.
"Hey," Reese said.
Anika did not slow.
"Thanks," he said, quieter now.

She killed the torch; the blue flame collapsed with a sharp hiss, leaving the bitter scent of melted slag between them. Anika wiped grease onto her sleeve and passed him without acknowledging his words.
"Felix drives with Reese in front. Jean Pierre, we'll want your MAS precision rifle out the back window. Elena, Logan, you're with me."
The new welds still glowed dull orange.
Anika turned to Jack at last. "You sit middle back."

A new low rumble echoed outside, then followed another distant shell. Her eyes were scanning the route display on the console. "We're 160 miles out. If we keep at 80, no stops, no stalls, we'll reach Lublin in just under 1:20." She tapped a cadence. The drone grid bloomed across the display, crimson markers curling west from the S19. “We’ve got thirty-five minutes before we’re in range of the drone swarm. After that, every kilometer becomes contested. That’s the window.”
Reese hit Logan on the shoulder to help him focus.
"So we go as fast as we can," Anika finished. "And we don't stop."

Reese leaned back in his seat, flicked the strap on Jack’s seat belt, and tightened the clip. He tapped Jack’s breast plate to be sure, too. Then he circled to the rear of the jeep. Logan was still arguing with his whisper pin over a broken ammunition count when Reese shoved two fresh magazines into his side pouch.
"Top off,” he said.

Reese spotted Elena at the rear of the convoy, rifle slung, standing with the same hard steadiness she carried before the ambush. Reese turned back toward the lead jeep and climbed inside as the engine's low vibration rose through the steel.

Engines snarled as tires tore against cracked concrete as the convoy surged through the jagged gap in the warehouse doors. Only the two engines carried through the night as they began the road to Lublin. The road quickly gave way; moonlight flickered over broken signs and abandoned car wrecks, dust trailing behind. Reese kept one hand near the weapon mounted on the dashboard and the other steady on his vest buckle.

Ahead, Felix gripped the steering wheel, sharp and erratic. Reese kept his gaze on the flanks, watching as they drove into the night. He could hear Elena behind him, her breathing steady, rifle ready. Fields slipped past under the moonlight as he leaned forward, and Anika's voice crackled over the comms: "Stay close. Don't let anyone get separated."
"Next city's Rzeszów. We hit the S19 and stay on until Lublin," Felix's voice came through calm but clipped. Reese was already checking the road ahead, cataloging every ditch, blind corner, and bend.
"Assuming the S19 still exists," Logan muttered over comms from the second jeep. Then came the overhead whistle, the undeniable sound again. Reese's stomach dropped and held.
"Artillery!" Felix started.

The blast cut off his words. A concussive wave punched Reese into the seat, the nylon belt biting deep across his collarbone as the air inside the jeep crushed against his lungs. Wood splinters and dirt flashed past his peripheral vision outside the car. Another round screamed overhead a heartbeat later.

"Speed up!" Logan said through the comms. Felix didn't need told twice. Reese steadied his rifle against the dash. Elena was behind him, muttering as shells landed and the road cracked beneath the tires. Ahead, Felix swerved sharply.
"Shit. Road's gone!" he shouted. A crater had eaten half the lane, steam rising off molten edges.
"Break left!" Reese called, gripping the door frame.
"We're exposed. Get off the road," Anika said. "We have a twenty-minute window before the swarm envelops this sector. Push the engine."
"Open on the right," Felix said.

Anika followed close behind; tires screeched as the jeep veered off the main road and onto a narrow service route running parallel to the highway. Reese hung on, keeping his body low and steady. The artillery fire continued unabated; the jeeps pushed forward, jarring over debris and struggling to maintain grip.
"That wasn't on my map," Felix said over the radio.

Ahead through the cracked windshield, the service track merged back into the highway. The pavement was cracked and veined, some parts missing entirely. Beyond that, the silhouette of Rzeszów. They were making good time.
Anika's voice crackled. "10 minutes. Is anyone seeing anything?"
Felix replied. "No signatures."
"All clear sounds good." Logan cut in.
Reese kept his eyes on the skyline ahead. Then came a new sound that all could hear. The hum deepened, growing stronger.
Then Elena said from the second jeep, "Sounds big."

As if summoned, they descended; sleek, jet-powered hunter drones sliced through cloud breaks. The first small grenade dropped and flickered across the hood of Felix's jeep before exploding beside them. Then chaos opened around them as dozens more grenades hammered the road, chewed through the ditch, and burst across the broken fields on every side. Further away, gunfire cracked; Logan leaned away from the window, but with his rifle facing out, firing wildly, trying to pin the drones as they banked and circled.

Reese raised his rifle, but Jean Pierre's MAS-style rifle cracked first. One of the drones spiraled down, slamming into a field in a burst of sparks. The rest scattered and regrouped, shifting tactics. One peeled off low and fast, angling toward the road ahead.
"They cut us off here," Reese snapped.

Felix braked hard. The jeep lurched. Behind them, Anika shouted something in Polish, then slammed her own brakes and yanked the wheel. The shrapnel explosion came the next instant. A gouge carved into the asphalt just ahead of where they'd been seconds ago.
Reese's hands pressed in. "They're driving us," he said.
Elena's voice confirmed over the radio. "They're herding."
A rusted overpass cut the highway into broken lanes, with burnt-out civilian vehicles flanking both sides of the narrowing stretch until the road became a kill zone.
"No room to maneuver," Felix pounded the steering column.
"Maintain vector. We punch through," Anika said.
Logan said, "They'll pin us in!"
"Shit," Reese said.
Something moved at the edge of the treeline. Lower, heavier silhouettes. Not shapes Reese had a word for.
"What happened to the clock?" Logan asked.
"We ran out," Anika replied.
Reese’s rifle came up, but the treeline burst alive with motion as segmented limbs cut between trunks and crawled forward from the forest.

"Go, go, go!" Reese barked. The jeeps tore through the choke point as a second explosion collapsed the overpass behind them. Reese's boot started tapping fast, uncontrolled, his heel clicking against the Jeep's floorboard. The drones outside his window had eight legs.
Not the skittering recon units he'd seen on feeds. These were bigger. Twenty feet from claw to thorax, each one moving with fluid velocity.
Reese counted at least six of them. Their legs cut through fallen branches and burnt guardrails, their eyes low and joints flexing as they moved together, closing the gap.
A drone flanked wide, then another mirrored it, circling quickly. Reese gripped his rifle tighter, tracking the threat over his safety selector.
Jean Pierre racked his bolt. "Spider drones!"
"Maintain velocity. Do not brake," Anika commanded.
The lead jeep surged forward, tires screeching as the drones around them attacked.

One leaped too late, caught mid-air by the bumper, and sent spinning into the dark. Reese jolted sideways, bracing the rifle against the doorframe. Another shape lunged from the treeline, missed them by inches, skidding claws first across gravel. They chased at deadly speed as Anika swerved left, and Felix headed right.
Logan said, "What the fuck?"

Logan steadied his rifle and opened fire into the trees beside the road, his eyes wide, and his teeth bared. Reese reached over and directed the barrel left toward the road. The mount snapped beneath him; Logan followed the new line of fire in a different direction. The spiders were behind them; the jeeps wove around each other as pavement crumbled to rubble, tearing through side streets and ruined alleys between fractured houses.

Reese held on with one hand, firing blind in bursts. Couldn't track them all. One climbed a collapsed billboard and jumped, a full sprint-leap, landing just behind them, clawing pavement to stay upright, covering more than six hundred yards effortlessly.

Gunfire slackened for a moment, and Reese caught Logan over the controller instead of his rifle, dragging the drone back above the convoy. “Don’t worry about your damn drone right now,” Reese shouted, firing past the jeep’s shattered frame.
"He's part of the team," Logan said.

Logan dragged the drone back into formation through static, its camera feed snapping clear just long enough for him to catch movement below the treeline. "Contact," he barked, his whole posture changing as the scout feed tracked the spider drone cutting hard along their flank beneath the canopy. "Right side, moving parallel, eighty meters and closing." He stabbed a targeting mark onto Elena's display. "Elena, your two o'clock."

Elena opened fire. The drone was right beside the Jeep, but skidded after Elena expertly placed a shot into its thorax. Reese stopped shooting just for a moment as he watched the spider terror go limp, all eight legs going lifeless at the same instant.
Reese looked toward her. "Nice shot, Elena!"

Anika ramped over a low divider directly onto a sloping access road that wasn't on the map. Tires caught air, and Reese hit the Jeep's ceiling. They landed amid sparks, with the suspension wailing. Ahead of them, another drone leaped the barrier behind them, landed too hard, split a leg, and tumbled into a wrecked bus.
Felix bellowed into their radios, "Stop staring and start shooting!"

Anika dropped gears, punched harder as she tore through a village square, clipping a burned-out fountain. A drone hit the edge of a roof above them, claws crunching tiles, then sprang, landing ahead and turning quickly. Anika crashed into the drone head-on. The Jeep shuddered, its front grill folding as the badly damaged drone was dragged underneath and ultimately left behind.
Logan's voice came crackling through the radio. "Holy shit, we got through." Reese relaxed just before the whine returned.

The blast struck before Reese could alert the others, white fire expanding and swallowing the night as the jeep left the ground. There were no tires beneath him, no road, only sky before the impact hit hard. Reese could not remember landing, only the sound of everything breaking at once. Thought slipped loose and scattered; then came heat, sudden and total as blood pooled inside his throat.

The pine mist cycled on; his eyes stayed on the pilot feed while the secondary monitor ran the perimeter sweep. He’d finished his fourth civilian race of the night, a high-speed sprint through a simulated canyon that settled without needing to be kept. A sterile burst of dry-blended pine hissed from the vents to ground him, but the pine-burst couldn’t mask the cold sweat at his nape.

Before those four public feed races, he’d taken a private job earlier that night, not broadcast to any audience. Even after those races, the spider drone pursuit in a Polish treeline stayed with him. Through the sterile display he maneuvered the spider drone over a patchwork of barren, melted earth, steel legs easily bypassing rusted caltrops and crystalline burns in the salted mud. Most overpasses had collapsed over their roads, main streets paved with rubble from artillery strikes. Any signal held long enough could be pinpointed, and every pinpointed target could be killed.

Andrew piloted all types of drones. This spider drone carried a white phosphorus payload. The shell launched at a perfect arc, merciless and uncorrected, only to be cut off just as his unit's remote timed out while the shell was still in flight. The bounty system flagged the payout as partial and marked the mission as "interrupted" due to a lack of a kill confirmation. The interruption left a blemish on a night of perfect scores. The data stream hummed a low, erratic pitch.

The old leather chair settled against Andrew’s back, the faded red wing decal on the right armrest catching beneath his fingernail. As the fifth circuit loaded, his hands flexed against the worn grip.

Andrew cycled through public events and private work, running rounds until dawn. He had clearance for both, and the next assignment was already starting. Left of the rig, a flatscreen cast restless light against the black wall. The monitor ran muted, tracking the same flight on the public channel, rendered from the audience broadcast. His view remained clinical while the crowd’s response burned with anarchic heat, two versions of the same violence occupying separate screens.

The leather seat clung to his shoulders, leaving marks on his skin. On the secondary monitor, the spider drone’s interrupted payload and partial payout still blinked without confirmation.
