Outside his room, the pedestal waited with a tall bottle of water sweating from the ice, a crustless sandwich cut into quarters, and two tablets.

On the tablet beside his chair, driver profiles and course preferences crawled past while Andrew murmured into his whisper pin in bursts too fast for the delayed voice to follow cleanly. He would run the projections through the simulator twice before strapping back in. One more circuit to win, then maybe, if the window held, a few more private jobs.

The next morning, the house awakened before him, starting with the scent of eucalyptus-cedar rehydration mist, followed by the gradual uncurling of the blinds as the low, diffused, pale sun entered the room. The ceiling arc held blue across all panels, declaring him safe while his hands still twitched from the last route. Depending on the room’s lighting configuration, Andrew would hear from his team about health and performance if he took on too much work and his vitals began to lag. As long as the room was blue, he was fine.
Andrew entered the kitchen, and the house shifted the way prey reacts when a predator stops pretending to be asleep.
“Morning, champ,” Ridge said.
From the kitchen, Cam raised the volume in the house. “Lorna! I need to know, did he sleep? Vitals people, vitals.”
“Lorna,” Noor said. “Are we mixed in?”
“Standard micro,” Lorna replied.

Andrew tasted the mix; Lorna’s formulation changed every week depending on what Noor had on the calendar, with a core trio of Modafinil, Dexedrine, and Ketamine. The quantities were measured in micrograms, just enough to tilt him toward the feed and keep the body quiet.
"Where are we?" Andrew said.
Cam perked up. “We’ve got the big panel interview. Our pickup details are confirmed. Oh, and Noor, the station forwarded a note that we should expect a crowd out front today.”
Ridge moved closer to the center of the kitchen. "We’ve got a prime segment, mainstream media gig. Hosts are friendly. The military will have someone there, too, but this should be a softball setup.”
“What are they after?” Andrew asked.

“Right,” Ridge said. “Soft-line glory stuff, you know, Talon is the best of the best.” His tablet brightened in his hand. “There’s a question about how you're the symbol of restraint in an age of escalation.”
"We fed them that one," Noor said.
"You're gonna get asked about the civilian strike rumors," Ridge said, stepping forward. "We negotiated that it's off-limits, but just in case."
Lorna said, “We go with what we practiced before. That's a nasty rumor, and brand disparagement.”
“Fine,” Andrew said.
Ridge smiled. “That’s why this works.”

Andrew took the spiral stairs two at a time, brushing past the trophy vault’s tinted slabs and autographed killcams, commemorative microchips, and a one-off cast of his thumbprint labeled “one in a billion.” The landing opened into the west gallery, which featured a panoramic art display. The staff selected all the art in the house and placed the pieces for him.

The hallway curved into Andrew’s bedroom; he turned left and entered the six hundred square feet of his closet, the space pressure-sealed, air with controlled humidity, fabric-test lighting, and an automated shoe carousel. Suits never worn and sponsorship merch still sealed on hangers.

At the back hung a weathered jacket of rugged black leather and faded gray fabric, outside the carousel tags and sponsor inventory. The jacket carried no price tag, only a seal worn thin. The wardrobe pinged: DO YOU NEED SELECTION ASSISTANCE TODAY, ANDREW? The old jacket remained unpriced on the bar until Andrew lifted it from the rack. Old drone race awards patches stitched the intact lining, a few older than the Talon logo itself. The faded insignia caught against the fabric: NGCS-A / SECTOR A / CALIBRATOR RANK: 1.

The patch frayed at one corner, the thread pulled loose where a rank number had been hand-sewn. Andrew descended the stairs, the old charter jacket hanging loosely from his shoulders, zipper half-done. The insignia glinted in the atrium sunlight. Cam choked mid-sip. “What the fuck is that!”
Ridge hammered the tablet. “Do you know how many licensing groups blacklist that logo now?”
“He knows,” Lorna said.
“PR will say we need to escalate,” Noor said, watching the first sponsor hold turn amber.

Andrew turned away and walked toward the exit. His team cut the chatter and arranged itself behind him. Outside, the limo was lined with ad-suppressant tech to keep viral tags from catching their faces en route. The hover-dampened limo glided away from the compound. Andrew sat in the quiet cabin, his reflection still against the reinforced glass.
Cam tracked from the faded NGCS-A insignia to the valuation ticker brightening on Noor’s board. “I still don’t get the wardrobe choice.”
Ridge kicked off his shoes, balancing himself to search for the champagne chiller. “Nobody wanted to watch a kid get used unless the footage is for origin content.”
Andrew traced the frayed edge of the Calibrator patch where the thread had pulled loose. "We played capture and delay enough that they had all the data they wanted."
Noor stopped scrolling as the projection flipped from sentiment to risk, three red bars opening under the jacket image. “Then why is there only one Talon?”
“I don’t know,” Andrew said. The city moved past the glass.

Cam pressed his palms into his knees. “Right. Let’s not say that in the panel interview.” The limo turned onto the avenue of the recording studio. Crowds packed the barricades ahead, roaring beneath the white stutter of camera flashes.

The city blurred past; his reflection in the glass held steady. As the limo arrived outside the news channel headquarters, crowds of thousands filled the streets, waving flags and VancePAC banners while children raced toy Talon drone replicas overhead; when the door swung open, the volume of screams intensified, followed by a rush of press eager to capture the unfolding scene.
“Is the jacket a rebuke of the Olympic system?”
Andrew moved ahead as his team swarmed into protective formation, the crowd surging and the cameras buzzing.
One reporter, an older man with a wire-frame mic rig, tired but sharp, leaned in close and asked, “When was your last military agreement?”
Lorna’s index pressed her earpiece. Gray-clad contractors in bandanas broke from the edge of the crowd, folded the man to the pavement, and secured his wrists before his question had finished.

Andrew climbed the stairs one step at a time with thousands of people behind him. Around him, his fans’ whisper pins dropped into secure mode one after another, the ambient murmur of private prompts disappearing in a slow-moving circle. Veteran Talon fans expected the blackout. First-timers checked their collars before realizing why they were off.
As he strode out onto the stage, flags snapped in the wind while drone cameras flew overhead, illuminating the scene in a vibrant red, white, and blue lattice.

The applause, the anchors’ enthusiasm, and the manufactured warmth never reached him. They used up about half of the interview complimenting the old charter jacket. By the time the military rep asked about the Olympic team offering the flag-bearer role. The trap of the clip. He said no. The anchor on his left gasped. The other adjusted his chair; he’d answered enough rooms to know the weight that word carried before it landed.

One host invoked patriotism; the other tried to soften the moment before they lost the crowd. Andrew kept going, searching the audience until one woman broke the broadcast design, unbranded and attentive. When he pointed at her, the room turned all at once.

The anchors lost control of the segment, and the production cut away under the weight of the noise. Noor’s board lit green across sentiment while the sponsor-risk ticker kept one thin red line moving downward. The woman he’d pointed out stood at the door by building security.

Andrew didn’t acknowledge her as he left. A surging protestor clipped Ridge's shoulder, and Andrew had to violently shove past a swinging placard just to reach the reinforced doors. Outside the venue, the crowd intensified, security tightened, and opposing groups pressed against the barriers. The crowd was in a full-scale riot, with Talon supporters in direct conflict with everyone else. He watched long enough for the riot to settle into a pattern, then turned to his staff. “Find me a track.”
Lorna’s eye shifted to the side, catching Andrew’s. “We have something ready to go.”
Andrew lifted his head. “Local?”
“We have an alias driver slot on a Tier Five circuit. Southside in the sprawl, and local feeds only.”

“Do we have driver profiles?” Andrew asked. Ridge handed him a stack of rider profile dossiers, each filled with crash counts and weak spots. Andrew scanned the files, returning the tablet to Ridge as he finished.

They arrived at the southside track; the limo’s hover-dampening cut out on the gravel. Floodlights and the smell of grease and burnt corn oil. For a few breathless minutes, Talon vanished; only the scarred wheel and raw torque remained. Under the alias Phantom Three, he fused himself to the engine and won. After the race, the limo glided away from the southside track, the floodlights and burnt corn oil falling behind.
Ridge kicked off his shoes, balancing himself against the door. "Sponsors want to know why we’re suddenly looking backward."
"It isn't the alias racing," Cam said, leaning forward. "Is he concerned about the girl Ridge ran background sweeps on?"

Andrew opened his eyes, and the cabin stopped talking. The limo’s dampeners kept correcting beneath the floor, small mechanical taps arranging themselves beneath everyone else’s breathing. Andrew worked the frayed edge of the Calibrator Rank 1 patch sewn into the old jacket. “She was another good pilot. They just didn’t let her fly.”
Ridge dropped his tablet. "Why not?"
“They said she wasn’t a fit,” Andrew said, working the broken corner of the patch. “She was modding game modes before the rest of us had our training wheels off.”
His finger pressed the door console, and the reinforced glass rolled down without a sound.

The next morning, Andrew left the routine untouched and went straight to the chair. His whisper pinged him with a new opportunity, and Andrew accepted. The war map materialized, initially grainy but gradually coming into focus. Details of Ukraine unfolded before him, the topography stitched together from live satellite feeds.

As the loadout came online, his display cleared to the profile of a spider drone model, and blue light pulsed across the room’s walls. Andrew settled into the black leather seat flexing against the worn grips as the chair took his weight. The spider answered before his shoulders finished dropping. His feed confirmed south of Kyiv, twenty-seven meters beneath an abandoned tram terminal.

The spider drone slinked from the vault. Andrew ran a micro-diagnostic: two legs, four, all eight, locking the synchronization. Time was on his side; the drone’s cameras swept gently left, then right, panning over abandoned media kiosks in the gloom. Andrew kept low alongside a few old cars that were looted, gutted and covered in mold. Through the machine’s unfeeling lens, the urban decay of occupied Kyiv was just another arena. Pigeons scattered as the spider legs clicked against the asphalt. He scaled a fire escape, then onto the roof of a five-story apartment block, the drone’s cameras panning left, then right, mapping the geometry without rating it. Kyiv around him was still, calm, and grey.

Then his screen flashed a yellow alert. A second followed, then a third; a warning tone deepened in pitch as Andrew’s display bloomed with red pings across the city grid from tunnel mouths, sewer chutes, and ventilation breaks, all rupturing open across the map at once. The pulse of the blue light on the walls slowed as more figures poured into the streets. Andrew’s screen overlay confirmed they were all eligible targets; some wore formal gear, but most wore salvaged uniforms or black-market kits.

The drone plummeted from the scorched balcony, eight steel legs absorbing the impact with a heavy, hydraulic thud that kicked up a blinding spray of shattered ice. The drone’s strike claws activated mid-air, three limbs engaging nearby targets before the body hit stone. The drone threaded narrow alleys between shattered trolley cars and vaulted stairwells, carving deep gouges through the concrete with every landing.

The breaches were hand-cut paths crafted with a blowtorch and a shovel. Pivoting the drone left, Andrew moved away from the skirmish on the surface and navigated past a line of burning motorcycles toward the old square, known as the Maidan, according to his display. Locating the main route from beneath the city could trap enemies and incinerate the entire nest. He went to an opening, a broken sewer vent nestled at the base of a collapsed office wing, marking one of the primary tunnel entrances. The drone broke left, legs skittering across ice-slick stone, then launched upward in one bound to a rooftop edge. Two fighters crouched nearby the opening, guarding the entry. The spider drone dropped from the ledge, with claws striking first, one through a clavicle, the other across the lower back. Andrew primed the payload and fired.

The charge dove into the tunnel mouth, trailing sick white flame; the strike hit deep, echoing once, then the blast churned upward in a heat pulse, stone fractured, as dust rained skyward. Andrew leaned back in the rig and rerouted the display lower, toward the place where the blast had entered. The drone dropped back to ground level and scanned the remaining active enemies across the square.

Andrew tracked one fighter by rhythm, not speed, and his hands remembered the roofline before the feed identified her. He held the approach a half-beat past the optimal window. The system flagged the delay. He closed anyway. She wasn’t erratic; she held exits the way Elena used to hold them. While the other defenders scatter-gunned from cover, this fighter worked the square, forcing the spider to turn before Andrew wanted to turn.
"Smart,” Andrew said.

He changed positions, pivoting the spider mid-stride into a gallop. All eight limbs skittered wide, turning on a dime. The fighter still didn’t run; she just stood there watching his approach. He tilted the display again and turned one last time to close the last gap. In that moment, she raised her rifle and shot at Andrew’s spider drone head-on, and the shot hit him low, in the rear leg, with expert precision.

The spider’s interface flashed, and Andrew’s own hip tightened in answer. He checked the spider’s vitals, noting armor integrity above ninety percent while the square around the machine bled. The leg was fine. He counter-pivoted, dropping low and firing back where she had been standing a moment before, but she was gone.

Just as she repositioned, her second shot came from behind, hitting the same leg joint as the first. Sparks flashed across the ground below the spider in Kyiv. Andrew narrowed his stance, pulling the damaged leg inward under the rest, though the leg existed only on the screen. The fighter would try that again. He’d get her on the move.
Instead of running, she anchored her stance and fired a final third shot.

Andrew was back in the room with the chair, but the walls were cycling emergency crimson. Andrew ripped his headset off and threw it against the wall, the visor casing cracking with a sharp snap. The fracture ran directly down the center of the glass lens.

Andrew faced the broken visor on the floor, locked against the armrests. His visor split from crown to chin while alerts washed red across the room. He turned toward the door hinges as air left him in a hard, uneven pull.
"Elena," he whispered.
No answer came; instead, only the sound of the static hissing from the broken headset on the floor.

The jeep flipped, metal shards flashing around her, and the road disappeared beneath a rush of weightlessness and dark. Consciousness returned in pieces: copper on her tongue, ozone scorched through her nose, and fractured ribs grinding whenever she tried to draw air.
“You’re awake.”

The world swayed. A streak of light across concrete. A man sat near the cot, elbows on knees. Rupert Collins gave a short nod; Elena propped herself up; Rupert held a steel thermos and three cups nested against its side.
“How long?” she said.
He checked his watch. “Not long, you’ve been out less than a day.”
“Not enough.”
His mouth twitched. Rupert poured and slid the cup across.
“I know your preference is tea, but this will have to do. You’ll want to be sharp,” Rupert said. “Not everyone knows yet, but we retake Medyka today.”
She sat up straighter. “Today?”
“Today.”
“I’m not sure I can even lift a rifle,” she said as she explored the bandages wrapping her rib cage.
He shook his head. “We have another role in mind for you now. Because you lived.”
Elena held his gaze while the thermos cap ticked once against the cup, and Rupert looked away first.
“They don’t fight with munitions, they fight with perception.”

“For too long we let this pattern happen,” he said. “The world saw Medyka fall easily through their broadcasts; they aired locals cheering, allied forces shaking hands with Russian aggressors in a peaceful transfer of power. They even broadcast your death, Elena. We must expose the lie.”
Elena’s hands curled around the mug. She drank.
“Where are we?”
“You are in Lublin, Poland. This underground med-bay is secure for now and well defended.”
“What got us?” she asked.
“White phosphorus launched from a spider drone.”
Elena pulled the blanket flat across her legs. The road came back first. Then the brakes. Then the lift.
“Bad combination,” he added. “One landed a high-arcing shot that came down on top of your jeep.”

The field generators pressed a steady vibration through the bedframe while rubber soles crossed the corridor beyond her curtain, and a medcart motor complained somewhere near the nurses’ station. Iodine hung over everything; burnt plastic lingered beneath it, stubborn as smoke caught in fabric.

“Your team survived,” Rupert said. “All of them.” Rupert stood with hospital light flattening the lines beneath his eyes, one hand still wrapped around the visitor badge clipped to his coat. Elena closed her eyes for a moment. The monitor counted her back into the room while everything else held still.

She reached for the elastic around her wrist, gathered her hair off her neck, and pulled it tight into a ponytail with slow, practiced motions that made her ribs protest. “The other drones followed patterns. This one changed them.” Pain cinched around her ribs when she pushed herself higher against the mattress.
“Rupert,” she said. “That pilot wasn’t from Moscow; that drone pilot flew Western.”
He set the thermos down, one eyebrow lifting. “If anything else feels familiar,” he said. “You let me know.”

Outside the med bay, a news loop showed Slava’s final seconds, blurred, slowed, and re-cut. The chyron read WAR HERO KILLED AND NATO CONFIRMS LOSS, then changed. VOLUNTEERISM IN DOUBT. Jack’s mother sobbed into a stiff collar of his dad’s. The scrolling chyron and her parents' faces arrived at the same moment.
“You don’t know how much power this gives us.” Rupert’s hand on her shoulder. The bunker was a concrete tomb, narrow passages branching into war rooms and holding cells.
Rupert walked ahead. “This isn’t survival, people,” he said, clapping his hands. “We’re taking Medyka back.”

Elena followed over scuffed concrete while the air carried sweat, oil, and old stone. Lublin seemed deliberately buried, built quickly, and held together by steel, generators, and refusal. Generators hummed deep within the walls, a radio barked out orders, and somewhere a kettle boiled too long before Elena unscrewed the thermos cap, set out the nested cups, and pushed the first one toward the nearest soldier before taking her own. She passed Polish resistance fighters in salvaged gear, Bundeswehr tags, and old French insignia. No uniforms matched, but each face carried the same weary certainty.

Jack stood at the operations board with one hand against the scarred oak table and the other resting on his camera. When he looked at her, the soft expression she expected never arrived. Elena crossed to him and hugged him. Together, they followed Rupert into the corridor.
Jack walked beside Elena, his eyes tired. “We have that veteran look now, don’t we?”
“I saw your parents on the news, Jack. They’re on the TV now, giving interviews about your death.”
Jack let out a short laugh. “They got what they wanted. They’re probably going to dinner afterward to tell themselves how well they told their story on TV.”
“Jack, they looked devastated. The enemy reported our deaths with generated content and details how we each died,” Elena said.

Jack worked the camera’s aperture ring. Rupert gave them both a small nod as the door relay clicked and the guards moved aside. Inside, stale recycled oxygen hung over a massive map of the Eastern Front, spread across the sturdy oak table and stabbed with red and blue operational markers. The door hissed shut behind them.

Rupert coordinated between a South Korean general and two French officers from the center. The room vibrated with clipped German logistics and boot-falls. One of the French officers shared a route proposal. Rupert didn't look at him. He continued marking the eastern board as if the proposal had been a weather report, and after a moment, the officer turned back to his own screen.
The door swung open as Logan and Reese entered the room.
Elena smiled. “I’m in one piece. But, Reese, we seriously need to do something about those Talons. I’m sick of seeing them.”

Logan erupted in laughter and pulled her into a bear hug that lifted her off the ground. Rupert led them through a reinforced door beneath the operations floor. Concrete corridors, exposed conduits, brushed metal walls. Doors unlocked in sequence ahead of him, revealing screens that displayed shifting maps while analysts moved briskly between workstations. The corridor opened into a circular command room with layered displays, tactical projections, and Rupert was now positioned at the center behind a large table.

Rupert advanced with medals gleaming. “We have a window,” he said. “Russian units pulled back from Medyka. We'll catch them thin.” Elena watched Rupert. “The Russians say they already killed you. They flooded state networks,” Rupert continued. “AI composite wreckage, faked bodies, staged dog tags. You're supposed to be dead.”
Logan huffed a laugh. “And here we are.”
“Precisely,” Anika said. “The enemy’s primary theater is distortion. They gave us an opening, and we will weaponize the truth.”

One fighter drifted from the table toward the mess coffee pot. Another followed with an empty tin cup, and the two whispered beside the kettle’s hiss, far enough from Anika that whatever passed between them stayed there.
Another intelligence officer Elena didn’t know added a new mark to the board. “She’s right. We retake Medyka while we broadcast our counterstroke in real time.”
Jack joined in. “Are you proposing a livestream?”
“You four are the truth the world needs.”
Reese leaned forward, forearms braced against the edge of the table. “Alright. So what’s the play?”
The officer tapped the map again. “Medyka’s garrison is light. The Russians pulled back to reinforce the western corridor. We have forty-eight hours before they come back in full force.”
Reese narrowed his eyes. “That feels too easy.”
“This will not be,” Rupert said. “They left the pass vulnerable on purpose. Expect drone overwatch, indirect fire support, and signal jamming.”
The heavy doors swung inward. Jean Pierre and Felix entered next, eyes alert and unhurried, their uniforms sharp. Every soldier in the room snapped a fraction straighter.
Jack shifted his weight and spoke first. “This plan makes us targets.”

“You already are.” A NATO officer set down a reinforced case on the table and unlocked it. Inside, protected by custom-cut foam, were a compact camera, a small delivery drone, and an adaptive signal amplifier. Alongside them was a military-grade mobile broadcast rig.

Rupert said, “That’s your camera now. After you get the footage, you’ll place the file in the delivery unit, which will bring the data to us here in Lublin, where we will initiate the global broadcast of your survival.”

Then Anika stood, and the scrape of her chair echoed in the room. “I propose one modification to this plan. As the combined forces retake Medyka, I’ll lead these Americans into Ukraine. This broadcast should come from Kyiv.”
Rupert snorted, then clapped his hands together twice. Elena watched something flicker in his posture. She didn't perceive shock, but rather a quiet readiness.
“You mean split priorities away from Medyka?” Rupert asked.
“I mean for you to continue through Medyka, through Lviv, to Kyiv.” She placed her hand on the map table over the corridors leading east to the borderlands and conflict zones.

“We can go small and infiltrate,” she said. She turned to the squad. “I take point with the Americans. We take the route behind enemy lines, completely dark, and use the broadcast to draw their forces away from Medyka, clearing the path for your advance.”
Elena kept her gaze on Anika, who remained calm and focused on gaining Rupert’s approval.
Rupert said, “We do have a guide here who knows a safe route east. Although this will be dangerous, if you reach Kyiv, this will work. And you might find old allies along the road.”
Anika faced the room. “The resistance is gone, Rupert.”
“We cannot be certain of that. There is one route where you might find the resistence. Then, if you hold Kyiv, we’ll establish a new corridor, resupply, and save you before the end.”

Anika pressed on the map beside them, not on Kyiv, but beyond toward the rail lines carved into the occupied territory. Elena followed her gesture, tracing the vast, occupied expanse east of Kyiv. A few soldiers murmured at the edges of the room as they attempted to resume their quiet banter. Most of the eyes, however, stayed fixed on Anika. From the corner of the room, Jean Pierre anchored himself to the concrete and didn’t move. The others in the room discussed the overlays.
Felix stood over the old map, checked his radio, and rolled his shoulders. “Here! Hit them here, you see?” he gestured to the allied leaders while making a ragged line along the Polish border.
Jean Pierre’s hand rested on the dented metal flask at his belt, his arms swaying in a loose pendulum. “Pleine mobilisation,” he said in French. “If you can, you fight.”
The words ignited the room, the same as a spark in dry brush. Jack, Logan, and Reese exchanged a glance. Felix then tapped a marker onto the map.
“Medyka is the battle,” he said, tapping a second marker deep into the red-tagged chaos of Ukraine. “My friends get the battle of our time, and I’m chaperoning Americans?” he said.
Jean Pierre tapped the top of Felix’s head with his flask. “No small parts, only small Germans.”

Low murmurs echoed through the room while whisper pins translated, summarized, and captured hurried notes beneath jacket collars. Fighters turned aside for brief exchanges with their agents before returning toward the grease-marked maps spread across ammunition crates. Gear straps tightened across winter jackets while fresh route estimates passed quietly between overlapping translations. Eyes kept returning toward Anika despite the constant movement surrounding her.
“How long have you been planning this?” Elena asked.
Jean Pierre’s lips thinned into a flat line. “From the beginning.”
Jack spoke next, addressing Anika. “Why now?”
Felix shifted his bulk, covering Elena’s exposed side. Jean Pierre eclipsed her flank, dropping his volume while his gaze remained steady. “Après la pluie, le beau temps.”

“The Russians broadcast your deaths,” he continued. “They gave your government convincing autopsy reports, and your government believed them.” He let that sit for a moment. Then he said, “The sun will always rise, but the light on the truth is ours, and ours alone to cast.”
Felix struck the table. “Enough French. We show up inside Ukraine, alive and armed, and the enemy’s eye won’t be able to stay on Medyka.”
Logan let out a low whistle. “So we’re bait.”
Felix grinned. “Heroic bait.”
Jack didn’t smile. “And if we get killed or captured?”
“Then you’re dead again,” Felix said. He drove a heavy hand into Jack’s back.

A fresh throb of pain pulsed behind Elena’s eyes. She avoided eye contact with Jack, Logan, and Reese. Instead, she focused on the nature of the operation and the logic of using their apparent deaths against the enemy.
Jean Pierre and Felix returned to the yellowing map, layered in black paint, warped from use. Felix shook a metal can; the smell of oil curled into the room.
Felix dipped the brush. “Old tradition,” he said. “Before we begin, there is more to paint after Medyka.”

“We haven’t needed this paint for some time now,” Felix set the can before her. He returned to the map. “Then we lost Medyka.” Felix slashed a wet line across Medyka, the bristles scraping once before he stopped and held out the brush. “You were there.”
Around the room, Logan was mid-gesture in some animated exchange by the supply crates. Jack and Reese had migrated toward the map table, heads bent over comms manifests and fallback schematics.

Elena took the brush and turned to the map. She painted a crescent representing the breach. The curve spread slowly dragging layers of black across the lost territory. She set down the brush. Felix’s chin dipped toward Jean Pierre, who stood partially obscured by his weather cloak, before he unsheathed a field knife, driving it deep into the black line of paint.

Elena caught Rupert’s eye, pointed toward the outer ramparts, and crossed into the wind with her jacket pulled tight. The air slashed out, carrying diesel exhaust, and she zipped her coat to the collar. She had only walked one front-line outpost from the inside at Medyka, with its broken depots. Lublin made that outpost look small, with orderly rows of watchtowers, artillery, and armored carriers.

Lublin was not a city holding on to the past, but built for a single purpose. The outer perimeter ran for miles, trenches reinforced with steel and concrete, checkpoints bristling with autonomous turrets while drones traced slow grids overhead. The AA batteries sat motionless, vast silhouettes along the horizon, ready to punish the sky. Near one artillery rig, a technician adjusted a radar dish welded from a stripped civilian weather tower. The dish turned in a careful arc while cables curled in neat twists around the mount, elegant and functional.

Lublin stretched away beneath the wall, less a city than a constructed answer to fear. Trenches ran in bands that traced the approaches, hard lines cut through frozen soil. Rail-fed artillery sat on prepared spurs, muzzles trained east in neat rows between the warehouses. Drones pulsed overhead in slow loops, their hum passing through the stones of the city.

Elena followed the wall until the noise from the command tower thinned behind her. Ahead, a corner tower rose above the line, metal scaffolding wrapped around poured concrete. A directional antenna sat bolted near the top, slightly off true. The bracket had slipped, or the crew had chased the wrong line of sight. The misaligned antenna made her hands itch to help with repairs. Below her, allied armor rumbled; the wind carried diesel, blood rust, and snowmelt mist. Ahead, an electrified prison enclosure sat inside the fortress.

Inside the enclosure, a dozen prisoners moved with the weariness of men long unclaimed, mercenaries, conscripts, unlucky survivors. One prisoner rose from the others, younger and taller, his coat too modern for the wire.
“Hey there,” he said. “They tell me my English is almost perfect.”
Elena stopped with one foot still lifted. Broken English had familiar shapes in this war, but his voice carried a different pattern, too measured and too practiced.
“My passport is red, white, and blue,” he said again. “My parents were Americans living in Crimea when I was born in 2011.”
Gray threaded his temples too early, and the lines around his eyes cut deep for a face that young.
“What’s your name?” she asked.

“I am a field Warden in the Russian military.” He tapped his shirt. “They saved me,” he said. “There were thousands with dead parents fostered into Russian homes. We rotated homes every six months, new faces, new rules until Unimilitarya.” He gave a dry smile. “When Ukraine collapsed under our weight, I got all new Western gear.” His gaze dropped to the coat. “This coat is American.”

“The earliest ever promoted to Warden,” he continued. “After all my training, I’m back with the foster program. That’s why I’m here. We were transporting a new litter from the front when the West caught us. They said I wasn’t the only American child in the program, but I never met the others.” His hands gripped each other loosely, the way someone might hold a prayer. “Someday I want to go,” he said. “To use my American passport.”
The way he said American thrummed in Elena’s ears. She studied him, his American vowels, and his enemy eyes. He wasn’t a volunteer who’d signed up for anything. He was something else.
Elena stood close enough for the wire to cast a line across her. “You’re not the only one they took,” she said. “But you made your choice to fight.”
He replied with a single, simple nod.

“Goodbye.” That was all Elena said, spoken as a firm boundary. She turned and walked back toward the ramparts, her silhouette thinning into the fog-slick dusk. The lights of Lublin bled through the haze ahead of her, sharp and cruel as searchlamps.

She stopped near a group of Polish soldiers clustered around a mobile command screen. Their eyes flicked across drone feeds while Elena set the thermos on an ammo crate and laid out the cups in a straight line.
“You’re one of the Americans,” the sergeant said.
“My name is Elena.”
He grunted and then extended his hand. “Welcome to Lublin. The last place in Poland the Russians will ever take.”
“You have to keep moving,” she said.
Another soldier, young, early twenties, looked up. “She doesn’t believe in us?”

Elena’s gaze moved past them to the vast machinery of the compound, to barricades and rows of tanks, to steel filmed white along the seams. Crates rose in stacks two stories high while lines of troops threaded through the checkpoints in practiced patterns. Their movements were orderly but untouched by real combat. Slava’s lesson returned to her with unwelcome clarity. The walls we build will not stop them. At the front, she saw the tide. Any city could fall, even this one. She drew her attention back to the soldiers.
“Gear up,” she said. “Long day coming.” She filled the last cup and left it beside the sergeant’s screen before turning back toward the inner compound.
As the words left her mouth, their headsets crackled to life with fresh orders. The soldiers gave her one last salute, then marched off toward the barracks.

Elena retraced her steps toward the inner compound where Reese, Logan, and Jack had gathered. The stillness was gone, vibration coming up through the stone and into her legs. The Lublin command went full tilt. Walls hummed with fluorescent light and strained ventilation, panels rattling faintly with the rush of air. Logistics teams pushed through the corridors with tablets raised and crates on carts, while officers barked into headsets and pointed at screens no one outside the room would ever see.

Elena lifted the machine gun from the table and checked the ammunition belt before loading it. Beside the brass, the thermos cups sat empty in a row, each one already offered and returned. She pulled the belt free, took a dry cloth, and cleaned the rounds one by one before feeding them back into place. Metallic clicks joined the hum of generators and distant radio traffic. Around the room, the others settled into their own preparations. Reese cleaned his scope with slow precision. Logan bent over his controller, pale screen light washing across his face. Jack repacked his camera kit piece by piece. Felix tightened his pack until the webbing groaned. Across the room, Rupert stood beside the operations board watching their preparations.
“Stick to the route, and help will find you,” he said. Elena watched his careful cadence and the weight behind his words. How could he know that?
“Execute,” Anika ordered. “No deviations. Don’t fall behind. Move out.” She pointed to the map, its digital glow washing across her hand.
“Above-ground movement is limited. We only travel when weather conditions create blind spots, but we have to move fast enough our arrival aligns with the assault.”
Jack said, “I’m not liking this plan.”

Reese zipped a weatherproof pack closed; Logan crouched over the medkits, redistributing tourniquets and ampules no differently than sorting poker chips. Elena looped an extra sling of rifle mags then stood, steady and unsmiling. The heavy door opened behind them with a groan of steel as Rupert entered. His expression gave little away; fatigue sat heavily on his face, but his eyes remained sharply awake.
“Come,” Rupert said, approaching the team. “This is Ryba. He has moved people through the eastern sectors for twelve years.”
Jack went toward the water tank near the back. Logan was there already with his cup in hand listening just enough to be counted and not enough to be involved.
“You’ll follow him,” Rupert continued, “and Anika will get you through.”

The man moved with an easy border-city stride, the loose gait of someone used to crossing checkpoints. His coat was waxed canvas, cut long and heavy, the hem stained with diesel and old weather. His gray knockoff Talons from the same base model Reese was wearing. Ryba’s didn’t have laces doubled back. Only worn tread along the outside edge and a bit of tape holding them together.
Elena stayed with the shoes longer than the face. Ryba took the open space between them.
“Ah,” he said, flashing a grin toward Anika. “So this is the team.”
“Team, this is Ryba,” Rupert said. “He knows the local crossings, the eastern sectors, and the people who work them.”
Reese shifted. “A smuggler?”
“Navigator is kinder,” he said, his voice modest enough to make the correction kind.
Jack didn’t raise his camera. “Smuggler works too, yeah?”
Ryba gave a small, tired smile. “Smuggler is a hard word. I know roads, checkpoints, and people who need to cross them.”
His attention dropped to Reese’s feet. “Where I’m from, those Talons would’ve made you a king.”
Reese remained unfazed. Elena looked from Reese’s stillness to Ryba’s grin as Anika moved to close the distance. Anika acted quickly and jabbed at the map.
“You’ll get us through this route,” Anika said. “Into Ukraine.”
Ryba smiled, shrugged once, and leaned against a crate while Elena watched the moment play out.
“And here I expected you to ask for something difficult.”
He spoke with easy charm. Ryba tilted his head, turnedto Elena, and smirked with a jagged smile. “Why does Anika babysit you?”
Elena turned away from him. The loose buckle on Ryba’s bag clicked against the crate between them.
“Ah,” Ryba said. “So serious.”
“Enough,” Reese said.
Ryba raised both hands, saying he was only making conversation and that the long road left everyone jumpy.
Ryba’s words reached her through the ache in her cracked ribs. His hands stayed exactly where she could see them, and his ease had the quality of a performance.
Felix racked his shotgun. “We’re not waiting. We’re choosing not to move.”
Jean Pierre thrust his MAS rifle forward. “Then we move.”
Felix snapped to attention, quick to respond, while Ryba looked toward Elena. She ignored both of them.
Anika said, “Go.”

The last gate of Lublin groaned open behind them while Elena held for one private moment at the exit. Ryba moved ahead in his frayed shoes, carrying the promise of a live camera feed in Kyiv and the weight of their own resurrection. Behind the wire, the prisoner watched her leave. She tightened her grip on the rifle sling, turned her back on the city, and slipped past the gate. Behind her, the doors closed again, ahead only the long road east.

Murk swallowed the outer wall of Lublin before the squad fully cleared the gate behind them. Concrete barriers emerged beside rusted floodlights, razor wire, and supply pallets stacked against the inner checkpoint. Steam rolled from vents beneath the guard towers while soldiers watched the eastern road and refused to look at the wreckage beyond the walls.

Above the mist, logistics drones passed unseen along fixed corridors with a conveyor-belt hum. Some carried batteries east, some carried plasma west, and some carried sealed black cases between the towers without showing their shapes.

Ryba crossed through the gate first. The haze took him at the shoulders, returned one sleeve, then took him again. His knockoff Talons scraped over brittle road grit while the others adjusted their pace around the broken pavement.

Jack lifted the compact camera beside his cheek and tried framing the road east of the walls. Moisture spread across the lens glass while the image screen softened into pale streaks, burned van outlines, and occasional drone lights above them.
He wiped the lens against his sleeve and raised the camera again.
The blur remained across the display while another supply drone rasped overhead.
Ahead, Elena moved several paces behind Ryba with Felix and Jean Pierre spread beside her. Their conversation drifted backward in pieces that dissolved beneath the engines above them.
“Forest line.”
“Longer route.”
“Too exposed near rail.”
Jean Pierre pointed somewhere inside the white distance while Felix answered without slowing. Elena watched Ryba, the road shoulders, and the tree line while the conversation continued in fragments.

Elena struck the lighter near her thigh. The gloom enlarged the flame into an orange bloom before the cap snapped shut and she walked on behind their guide. Reese walked near the middle of the formation with Logan beside him. Reese watched windows, drainage ditches, rooflines, intersections, and the road behind them without lingering anywhere too long.

Anika followed along the rear edge of the formation, far enough back to watch everyone at once. Near the outer gate hinge, she slowed and tested the frozen chain with gloved fingertips, then caught the moving formation before the distance widened. Seconds later, the gate vanished behind her.

Jack raised the camera toward a melted satellite dish across the roof of another van while blackened cables hung from shattered windows in hardened loops. White paint beneath the news logo turned gray from years of being parked still at the gate.
Jack slowed down his pace. Another burned van was beyond the first ruined shell. Then another emerged farther ahead after that. The convoy was burned out.

The road dissolved into wreckage before Jack understood how many vehicles surrounded them. A media caravan had died here long before this morning, long before Lublin opened the gate for them, long enough for moss to thicken inside door seams and snowmelt to polish old burns into dull black scars.

Press decals blistered away from doors in curling strips, while tripod legs remained fused to the pavement. Camera cases lay split open beside the ditch with snow gathered inside the foam interiors, and old weeds grew through melted wheel rims.
Logan bent toward one broken decal and frowned.
“These aren’t fresh.”
Reese stopped beside a van with its side panel melted inward. He put one hand against the scorched metal, braced his bad leg on the bumper, and stretched the knee against the old wreck.
Logan laughed before he could stop himself.
“Are you stretching on a crime scene?”
Reese kept his heel planted against the bumper.
“Old men adapt.”
“You’re thirty-something.”
“Old men start early.”

Jack aimed the camera toward Reese, the van, and Logan’s first real smile since the gate. The lens fogged before the frame settled. Above them, unseen logistics drones beat steadily through the clouds. Their rotors merged into a dull industrial rhythm that filled the gaps between footsteps, jokes, and loose metal pinging inside the ruined convoy.
Elena’s lighter flashed again ahead of them. The flame made a small orange square near her thigh. Jack saw Felix look toward the signal, then toward Ryba, then back toward the road.

The squad entered the wreckage. The vans had settled into the road as if the asphalt had been poured around them afterward. Jack lifted the camera toward a burned windshield. Condensation blurred the display. He filmed shattered lens glass beside a collapsed door, but the wreckage offered evidence without sequence, damage without answer, and objects no one had collected.

No bodies remained near the convoy, only equipment sinking deeper into mud. Rust stains spread beside black water and dead weed stems. Supply drones crossed overhead without slowing above a road crowded with unburied witnesses. A notebook lay swollen open beside one destroyed tire, its frozen pages sealed beneath gray slush.

Elena’s voice carried backward from somewhere farther ahead. Jean Pierre walked around a collapsed antenna mast while Felix scanned the eastern tree line. Ryba never turned toward the Americans following behind him.

Anika disappeared beside a burned van near the rear edge of the convoy behind the wreckage. Then she emerged, adjusting one glove while rejoining the moving line. Jack lifted the camera instinctively toward the van she had left, where a thin wire caught light near the wheel well.
Above them, a heavy cargo drone with a load swinging beneath an unseen frame. Reese finished stretching his leg on the van, rejoining the group.
“Better?” Jack asked him.
“Ask my knee after Kyiv.”

Moisture gathered under the lens cover while the screen struggled to separate weather from wreckage. The machine could not hold the image steady long enough to preserve anything useful. Reese watched Jack attempting to get the picture.
“You can’t get a good shot.”
Jack looked toward the ruined convoy stretching ahead of them.
“Neither could they.”
Ahead, Ryba moved beside a line of dead trees while Elena and Felix argued quietly behind him. Jean Pierre checked rooftops through binoculars without slowing.
Jack adjusted the camera strap before looking at Reese.
“When did you know?”
Reese kept walking without looking toward Jack.
“Know what?”
“The order was wrong.”
Overhead, another drone train passed in staggered intervals, rotors clicking through the cloud cover inside the sky.
Reese watched the road ahead before answering.
“Before I admitted it.”
“That isn’t really an answer.”
“It’s the one I’ve got.”
Condensation softened Reese’s outline across the display until his face lost detail.
“You stayed anyway.”
“Yeah.”
“Why?”
Reese rubbed one gloved hand across his face before answering.
“The important thing was I left eventually.”
Jack looked toward the destroyed convoy surrounding both sides of the road.
“That sounds weak.”
“I was.”
The answer landed flatly between them while the formation moved east and the sky conveyor ground overhead.
Ahead, Elena’s lighter flashed once more in her hand.

The flame revealed Ryba’s shoulder for less than a second before the brume closed around him. Elena pocketed the lighter without stopping, a small mark placed in the air between herself and the guide. Jack filmed the orange afterimage incorrectly again. The image failed before the screen could hold the moment.

Anika remained behind everyone else in the formation. Jack noticed Anika kneeling beside the drainage ditch near another burned van. Her hands disappeared below the road edge for only seconds before she stood and continued walking without hurrying. Something metallic clicked near the ditch, almost lost beneath the drone traffic overhead.
Jack turned the camera toward the spot.
“You knew before the arrests started?”
“Yeah.”
“Before people stood up.”
“Yeah.”
“Then why stay?”
“Because people convince themselves tomorrow is as good as today.”
Jack frowned toward Reese through the haze.
“Is that an excuse?”
“Yes, Jack.”
Logan walked over a forgotten camera case with moss growing through the seam.
“Maybe Ryba knows what happened here.”
Reese did not look toward the guide.
“Probably.”
“Should we ask him?”
“No.”
“Because he’ll lie?”
“How would we know?”

The drone corridor overhead throbbed harder for several seconds. Cables trembled faintly inside the burned vans while loose metal clicked inside hollow bodies. Jack filmed a collapsed tripod, half-buried in slush, near the shoulder. The image softened into gray distortion across the display.
“Did quitting help anyone there?”
“No.”
Reese refused to defend himself. He kept moving, his eyes kept checking the road, and his hands stayed away from any gesture that could become an apology.
“What answer would’ve satisfied you?” Reese asked.
“The truth.”
Reese walked for a few more steps before he replied.
“Truth doesn’t repair anything by itself.”
“Then why record anything?”
“Because forgetting helps the people who are worse.”

Ahead, Ryba drifted through the white distance in fragments while Elena, Felix, and Jean Pierre followed the road east. Jack filmed Reese again through the shifting haze. The image failed before the screen settled.
“You think people stayed doing these bad things because the crowd was too hard to part with?”
“Yeah.”
“You say that too easily.”
“No.”
Reese kept watching the road ahead.
“Maybe don’t cross-examine the man beside the dead press convoy.” Jack kept his attention fixed on Reese. “You quit because of them or because of you.”
“Both.”
“That’s convenient.”
“No. Convenient would’ve been staying.”

The road narrowed beside a concrete barrier, half-buried beneath dirty snow. Loose metal shifted inside one burned van while wind moved through broken windows, and the drone corridor kept its invisible traffic overhead.
Logan adjusted the drone case strap across his shoulder while the convoy faded behind them.
“Can we save the interview? 60 Minutes doesn’t run for a reason, guys.”
Jack kept staring toward Reese instead of Logan.
“I wasn’t asking you.”

Ahead, Elena had halted near a drainage culvert where Ryba stood at the edge of a shallow ditch. She unscrewed the cap from her thermos, poured a cup without looking away from the guide, and walked back toward Logan through the drifting gray.
Logan looked up only when the cup reached him.
“You look awful.”
“That a medical diagnosis?”
“Operational observation.”
Logan took the cup with both hands. Steam rose between his face and Jack’s camera, and neither of them spoke.
Felix called from ahead. “Are we moving or decorating the road?”
Elena answered without raising her voice toward him. “Moving.”
Logan held the cup a few seconds longer before giving it back to Elena. “Thanks.”
She capped the thermos while watching the guide. “Stay closer.”

Anika crouched beside another burned van near the rear edge of the convoy. She disappeared behind the melted wheel well for several seconds before rising and clearing road grit from one glove. Jack thought he heard another faint metallic click near the wreckage, but drone traffic swallowed the sound.
Reese caught Jack briefly by the camera strap, and pulled him forward with limited force. Then Reese reached backward and grabbed Logan by the shoulder strap of the drone case.
“Close the gap.”
Neither American answered him while the formation compressed again. Reese kept them moving east through the ruined road. “You can hate the answer later. Right now, nobody drifts.”

Burned vans receded behind the squad until only fractured outlines remained. Old burns, old metal, and old press marks weathered beside a gate that still opened and closed every day. Jack lifted the camera one final time, but the lens fogged before the image settled.

Ryba moved ahead through the white distance while Elena, Felix, and Jean Pierre followed without speaking. Reese kept Jack and Logan inside the same moving line as the formation entered thicker forest. Farther behind, Anika walked alone.

The open space left Logan with nothing to do but count distance and tell dead jokes. They slipped through wet brush and low branches, footsteps muffled by damp leaves. They avoided roads with working streetlights and crossed terrain where enemy drones struggled to navigate.
Logan tapped his goggles, drone already logging the perimeter. The cold was sharp on his lungs. Trees stood rimed and stiff, black needles iced over beneath a sky the color of still dark water.
They followed Ryba, who walked at a steady pace without hurry.
Elena said. “You keep saying you run this route often.”
Ryba shrugged. “I do.”
Elena said. “What else have you been smuggling?”

Ahead, they cut down a low embankment and crawled through a fence coated with rust. Metro tunnels lay just beyond, empty and unlit. Logan hesitated at the edge as Ryba outlined the crossing plan. Metro tunnels, then surface streets, then another tunnel beneath the city. Following Ryba’s route, the plan kept them underground whenever possible. Logan walked beside the column, feeding sarcastic prompts into his whisper pin to pass the time.

Inside the metro tunnel, their footsteps echoed. Station after station was the same, benches intact, signs straight, trash bins upright, with dry rails showing little damage. Logan kept a hand on his rig as they ducked the turnstile into what Ryba had called a known path, mapped and remapped to keep the black-market arteries breathing.

Ryba’s shoes were knockoff Talons, not Reese’s grade, but close enough that you’d look twice. His stride was narrow, but the tread worn heel to toe. His gold bracelet caught the faint light out of place next to his threadbare coat. Nothing about his appearance followed a consistent pattern. When Ryba turned to say something to Felix, his mouth opened wide in the dim light, and Logan finally saw his teeth. Broken things, crowded and dark at the edges. He looked away before Ryba noticed him looking.

They passed through a collapsed platform where the ceiling had buckled inward, was jagged with rusted rebar jutting overhead, and was slick and damp. Logan took slow, deliberate steps; the air held the smell of a long-sealed place and rot. Ryba moved easily about the station.

Inside, they crossed through passenger carriages stripped to metal ribs. Seat brackets jutted from the floor, window frames opened onto black tunnel air, and they kicked dry concrete dust through the mildew. One carriage had become a shelter, with old cots, Polish-labeled crates, and medical gear arranged too neatly for abandonment. A hanging blanket divided the train from a narrower access tunnel where the walls pressed closer and the air turned wet.

They emerged into pale daylight to crashed drones that lay scattered through yards and tangled in fences, some half-buried in thawing mud, others draped across rooftops where they had failed to clear the approach. Nearly every house carried damage; small impact scars marked siding and brick. Fire residue blackened window frames and garage doors. A bicycle still leaned against one garage.
The group pressed on. Felix keyed his radio. “Distance to the border?” he said.
Ryba’s voice came back evenly. “Not far now.”
He tracked each turn, each narrowing passage, and the route folding back on itself until the spiral became clear.
Felix’s steps ground into the gravel behind him. “Ryba, we were just here.”
But Ryba didn't turn. “You should know drones attribute meandering to locals. Straight lines are for soldiers.”

They surfaced only to connect to other underground tunnels. Unless underground, broken villages and frayed forests were the best cover they had. They stopped at a farmhouse at the end of a long, dead road. A single lantern flickered in the window. Inside, an old man sat alone at a wooden table, a hunting rifle next to him. He gestured to the kettle over the fire as they entered.
“Eat,” he said.

While the others rotated watch shifts, Logan paced the edges of the main room, feeding the farmhouse inventory into his whisper pin until it returned useless jokes about structural collapse. The farmhouse sagged at the rear, its roofline warped from an old artillery blast or something worse.

Logan went outside without armor or a rifle. Footprints marked the ground near the trough and beside a collapsed section of fence. Reese moved toward the treeline; a figure stood between the birch trunks a hundred paces out. Ryba’s stride. Logan thumbed his whisper pin for range, but the little unit only pulsed amber against his collar and offered a confidence score that meant nothing in the trees.

He advanced while Ryba vanished behind the birch trunks, then crouched beside a snapped fence post with one hand on the wood. His whisper pin suggested three possible alternative routes. Far ahead, Ryba reappeared, and Logan moved after him through the trees. Logan pressed his hand against a leaning birch, forcing his breathing into the cold grain. He had miscalculated. The whisper pin warmed uselessly beneath his collar. He circled wider anyway, searching the ground for the print Ryba should have left behind.
When he returned to the farmhouse, the chimney barely smoked, and a faint light showed through the kitchen window.

The stove pulsed low. The old man was still there, Elena's scarf still hung from the chairback, Felix's gear rested by the wall, and Ryba stood exactly where he had been, as if he'd never left. Logan shut the door, pulled off his gloves, and set them on the bench. His hand shook, not from cold.
Logan discovered a pantry where above a felled drone crashed through the roof. The chassis was mostly intact, the optics unit half-dangling from a bent armature, rotors sheared but recognizable.

Logan crouched, brushing salt crust from the drone casing. Beneath the melt lines, a stamped plate caught the light. He ran a hand across the edge, eyes narrowing as the detail registered. "Why's this labeled in English?" he said.
Reese gave a guess. "Someone’s laundering contracts.”
The main board bore Cyrillic part numbers, and the stabilizer marked in Mandarin. Another module shrink-wrapped with a barcode in an American font unscarred.
Ryba stood in the doorway with one hand against the frame, studying the wreck. “Pop the underside. The fuel rods might still be hot.”
Felix shifted his weight off his front foot. “Doesn’t look stable.”
Ryba didn’t blink, voice steady. "Quick hands make the difference. You’ll be fine if you move fast."

Reese planted his heel, jammed the drone plate, and pulled hard until the latch popped. Dense vapor hissed out, hot and acrid with chemical residue. Reese recoiled as the burn flashed red across his skin.
Ryba crouched beside the coils, expression cool and detached. "Still warm.”
Reese wiped his hand on his jacket, the motion deliberate and slow. “You planned for this.”
Ryba replied. "I suggested a possibility, but you made the decision."
Elena was by Reese’s side quickly, unspooling a med wrap without hesitation. Logan didn’t take his eyes off her.
"Where were you when he opened that panel?"
Elena looked up. “I can’t watch both of you at the same time."
"You usually do," Logan said, voice quieter now.
Elena bound the wound fast and tight, setting the wrap harder with each pull. Reese forced air through his teeth and kept still.
Logan gave a dry laugh. "I’m moving up in rank, Reese. You’re the low man now."
"Who can handle this liability?” Anika asked, her eyes never leaving the old man. Ryba leaned into the doorframe, arms folded, weight balanced.
"Nobody’s going to listen to him," he said in English, each word carefully spaced.
He turned and left the kitchen, where the old man still sat at the table. Firelight shimmered in cataract-clouded eyes that never blinked.
"He’ll never have an audience that matters," Ryba said.
Anika turned and left with Ryba. “Where next?”

“From here,” he said as he pointed toward the window. “Past that is Narol, a small, blown-out city on a river we have to cross on the south edge. There will be drones on the bridges, but the water's low this time of year.”

And then they were moving again. As they went, the trees thinned, and Logan heard a voice call behind them just as they cleared the tree line. The old farmer had come after them, shuffling, something white in his hands. Logan couldn’t make out the words, only the weight of them, the man’s shoulders bent, his hand pressing a folded scrap into hers.

By the time Anika caught up, Logan had not asked, but she handed the document across anyway. The brittle pamphlet still carried the faded crest of a ministry office above the title: Temporary Soil Rehabilitation. Sterile Cycles Reset in One Season. Logan studied the marketing material meant for farmers.
Anika interjected, her voice steady, "He claimed this was proof."

Logan folded the page back on itself, sharp at the creases, and put the evidence in his front pocket for safekeeping. The pale salt fields stretched ahead of them. The trees gave way to open earth where long pale lines snaked across the flats, salt trenches, laid fast and uneven, sterilizing the fields in a hurry. The terrain had no beauty here, just land broken by dead grass and blackened husks of brush and fiberoptic threads poking through the brush, taut and glinting nerve filaments.

Every step was a risk; ahead, the tower loomed as the checkpoint drew close. The gravel abruptly gave way to harsh concrete beneath the grinding thrum of a heavy diesel generator. Above them, faint light carved vertical slashes through the trees. Floodlights in a half-second rhythm beamed down from three towers full of Russian guards.

They weren’t ready for this. Logan started sweating, drenched, now bleeding through his undershirt to his jacket. A cold sweat broke across Logan’s forehead. They were close enough now that Logan saw the checkpoint signage and the guards' faces.

Elena was second in line, shoulders taut, shifting her weight forward as they moved.  Ryba moved ahead without looking back, and that was worse. Just as they reached the checkpoint, Logan's foot crunched down on a hidden piece of plastic, the sharp synthetic echoing, drawing every gaze towards him.

Beams swept across the road, holding gates open. Towers loomed; they were here, reinforced and real, being consumed by cold metal. Logan put eyes on the guard towers first, two on the left, elevated, with no clear line of sight from the south. Below the towers, a corrugated outbuilding sat half-collapsed.
As Ryba walked on, he adjusted his straps for the third time in just a few minutes, and Elena slowed her pace. They were about to walk into the lion’s mouth. Logan kept his eyes on the checkpoint.

Ahead, three Russian soldiers lounged around a fire with rifles hanging loose across their laps, yawning between cigarettes and flicking ash into the ground. Their casual comfort grated on him; Logan tightened his grip on his heavy drone case. Beside him, Elena stood with rigid shoulders beneath her winter layers, while Jean Pierre melted into the treeline two paces away.

Logan kept testing increasingly terrible jokes against his whisper pin while the trees stayed empty around them. The score dropped with each attempt. He still glanced upward whenever the branches shifted, expecting a spotlight or a drone camera to break through the dark, doing anything possible to avoid eye contact with the guards.
Felix checked his radio signal, positioning himself a step behind Elena. “They didn’t care.”

Jean Pierre moved ahead with his rifle tight and his eyes on the sky while Anika stayed close behind. Elena didn’t look back again. They crossed into Ukraine without a shot fired, and Logan kept his head down while the checkpoint fire shrank behind them. Ryba’s grin, the bored soldiers, and Anika’s command all belonged to the same impossible arrangement. Ryba kept hiking ahead.
“We will pass around Lviv now,” Ryba said over his shoulder, voice light. “Welcome to Ukraine.”

They walked. Behind them, the checkpoint fire shrank to a smear of orange against the dark, the barrel smoke thinning until it disappeared. They walked through what had once been a village near Lviv. The buildings lay stripped bare, with unhinged doors, shattered windows, and scorched signs, while torn Ukrainian flags clung to broken poles.
Reese muttered beside him, “Where the hell is everyone?”
“Most of Ukraine is empty now,” Anika answered.
“Gone.” Ryba didn’t turn around. “Or waiting to see if you get them killed.”
Logan checked Reese, but the sergeant’s face had already tightened. “And what the hell is that supposed to mean?” Reese snapped.

Up ahead, Ryba stopped and nudged a road marker. A high whine threaded through the trees, followed by crackling rotors, metallic chirps, and branches shivering under unseen movement. The drones brushed the canopy, and one cut between the trunks close enough to strip needles from the pine.
Logan said, “So this is what a top-dollar education gets you. A foil cap and gown?”
Reese, two meters away under his own cover, snorted. “Hey, Elena’s the only one here with a top-dollar education.”
Logan joked again. “My thermal blanket smells.”
Reese laughed with him. “I bet Ryba had yours last then.”
“Ah, yes, disgusting.”

A branch cracked above them, metal ringing through the canopy as splintered wood dropped around them. The ground vibrated. “I’m questioning whether ‘rank’ refers to a distinction between volunteer and soldier,” Ryba hissed.
Logan doubled down. “Tell you what, if we live through this, I’m buying Ryba new clothes.”
Reese piled on. “Nice not having to see him. I don’t mind the blankets.”
That even got a suppressed laugh from Jack.
Ryba said, “Enough.”

Logan did stop, but the swarm overheard hadn’t passed yet. The forest buzzed as the drones sliced air low over the treetops, the occasional leaf shredded mid-flight. The swarm crescendoed, then lifted; the buzzing faded as they moved off the highway with one final stutter of motion in the trees.
“You good?” Reese asked.
Logan’s breathing was shaky. “Better than Ryba looks.”
“Low bar.”
Anika rose smoothly, rifle tracking the treeline. “Skies are empty. Perimeter secure.”
Ryba was already walking again.
“Guy really knows how to kill a vibe,” Logan said.
Reese patted him on the shoulder. “You’ll get him next time.”
They were on their feet faster than they should have been. Nervous sparks between Reese and Jean Pierre, the air almost lighter. All except Elena. She hadn’t laughed, hadn’t spoken.
They infiltrated Lviv in a loose line. Ryba led without checking his six; the pace stayed constant.

Logan had expected something more bombed-out. But Rynok Square greeted them, preserved and abandoned all at once. The buildings still stood, stoic and yellow-pink against the gray sky. Tables still circled the dry fountain, umbrellas half-folded as if the baristas had just gone in for a smoke. Posters curled around lamp poles; a child's scooter lay overturned near the tracks.

They spread out instinctively, each picking a perch across the stone plaza. Anika and Jean-Pierre posted near the café entrance, quiet, watchful. Reese dropped his ruck and pulled water. Jack took the highest ground and began scanning the edges with his camera.
Logan stood with his hands on his hips, his drone still zipped tight in its case, where it hadn’t moved.
"Have you ever tried a cold throw?" Reese’s voice snapped him back. Logan turned red; Reese held his drone under the chassis the same as a discus. “Just throw and flip mid-air.”
Logan squinted. “You’re so full of shit.”

Reese smiled. “You’ll see.” He launched the drone in a slow spiral pass. Mid-air, he tapped the controller, and the drone came alive with a low, waspish whine and stabilized, caught the wind, and banked out over the rooftops. Jack let out a low whistle, catching the attention of everyone around. Even Ryba, who was squatting near the base of the statue, looked up.
Reese grinned. “Your turn, my guy, let's go.”

He popped the seal and yanked the drone free. He focused on the motion: throw first, then power. But the throw wobbled, and the power surged too late. The drone veered off course, crashing hard into the wall of a former chocolatier’s shop, shards of cracked plastic scattering across the cobblestones. Jack let out a single snicker. Logan crouched over the wreckage, reached for the twisted frame.
Then Elena was there, moving quickly across the housing, checking the arms, inspecting the rotors. “All fine,” she said, before he could stop her. “Just the nose.”
He remained crouched, allowing her to work. She stood with Logan’s drone in one hand and his controller in the other.

“Watch.” She didn’t throw. She moved, pivoted on her heel, and let the drone slip from her grip, mid-spin, and tapped the power the exact moment the drone tipped nose-down. The drone caught, caught again, then steadied, the wings lighting faint blue.
“It’s not just the throw,” she said, handing the controller back to him without looking. “It’s the reaction. Start-from-anywhere training is about recovery. That’s all.”
Logan said thanks. His ears burned as he caught Reese's subtle nod while Jack remained absorbed in watching the sky.

And Ryba stood, brushing off his pants. "I thought you were the drone pilot," he said, squinting with a smile at the broken bits near Logan. “If you can’t do a simple thing, then what is your role?” His English sounded better than Logan remembered.
Jack moved beside him as they crossed back toward the fountain.
"You know who flies real good?" Logan said. "Talon."
Jack pointed down at Reese's shoes. "You think Reese had a poster in his room?"
"Definitely," Logan said.

Reese let them talk. The group left Lviv’s emptied center, passing broken boulevards and leaning tram wires. The city fell away into frost-lined roadbeds, sagging power lines, and countryside with too much room overhead. No checkpoints followed them now, only kilometers of open sky.

The hours smeared together. Ryba pressed on ahead, humming an old soldier’s tune under his collar. Jean Pierre scouted from elevation, moving through mud and half-crumbled roofs. Elena had gone quiet since the drone. Logan trailed farther than usual as they passed defaced road signs, black paint over blue-and-yellow names, and a charred children’s library with Russian slogans across the walls and flowers at the door.
By mid-afternoon, the forest grew denser, primarily consisting of pine trees with an occasional birch. A cracked brown sign read: "Zalissia National Park.
“Do they still live here?” Logan asked. After he spoke, he just tasted salt. The bison turned its massive, scarred head, muzzle coated in a white crust.

The Desna River shimmered beyond the timber as the Zalissia forest closed around them in damp twilight. Logan let the gap widen, not enough to disrupt the line, only enough to separate himself. Reese and Jean Pierre talked battery life, Jack filmed moss, and Elena had not spoken in hours. Logan had run dry of jokes. When the trail opened into a glade dusted with gold needles and fractured birch, Anika circled back and fell into step beside him.
“Logan, come with me,” she said. She turned, lowering her pack beside him.
“You need a minute,” she said. “You’re tired.”

Logan hadn’t meant to cry, but the tears came anyway and kept cutting hot tracks down his frozen cheeks even as he wiped them away. He had no name for this, not fear exactly, not grief exactly, only the particular exhaustion of a person who has been performing for too long.

Anika surveyed the group still chatting ahead before bending down to the ground. Her hands sank into the cold earth, mixing pine needles, dirt, rock, grass, and bark. She crushed them together until they formed a smeared paste. Adding a small bit of water from her flask, she kneaded until the paste clung. She swiped the mixture across her own cheek, then applied another layer across her forehead.
Then she reached for Logan without cleaning his face or wiping away the tears. Instead, she painted over them, folding the grief into the pattern. “Make your strength,” she whispered. “Do you see?”
Logan kept his eyes on her hands as the dirt dried across his face. They stood side by side, faces smeared with earth.
“Felix. Look at Logan’s camouflage. We can use this,” Anika said.
“What the hell?” Reese laughed. “Logan, that’s quite the look. Hiding in plain sight!”
Jean Pierre tilted his head, amused. “Ils l'appellent Camo,” he said.
Jack snapped the lens cap shut. He moved closer, scooped a streak of leftover mud from the ground, and dragged a thick stripe across his own cheek. “What else are you holding back?”
Anika was already with Ryba again, two steps ahead. She had seen him. The canopy thinned, and the ground dipped as the trees stood farther apart, sickly in the cold.

Reese had stopped joking, his rifle tracking the tree line and the strip of sky between branches. Beside Logan, Jack kept his camera holstered, the strap pinned under his grip instead of the shutter. Jean Pierre moved lightly over the frozen ground. Up front, Ryba kept the same loose-limbed ease, a man leading a funeral procession.

The trees thinned as they pressed on. Trunks pulled farther apart, opening long sightlines across matted grass and exposed dirt. Overhead, the sky widened, pale and flat, full of places a drone could hang and watch.

The cover dropped away with a swimmer’s sudden depth. The dried camouflage tightened across Logan’s cheeks, cracking at the edges as the trees opened. Jack drifted closer between two bare birches, scarf crooked, woodland pattern mismatched against a jacket worn pale at the seams.
“So,” Logan said, vapor clouding in front of him, “how is the filming going?”
Jack laughed. “I’m just trying to be better now.” He tapped the camera. “I had no idea what I was doing. Half my shots were the back of someone’s shoulder.”
The group loosened as the trees opened into wider spacing, pairs and trios forming and breaking across the path.
Jack went on, tapping his camera. “When everything slides downhill, we can’t fix the slope. But you can make something that holds. That is our little act of resistance to all of this.”
The drone logs flashed the captured shots over Jack’s shoulder as the swarm reoriented.
“You think anyone will want to watch this?” Logan asked. “Every day here is worse than before.”
Jack answered him. “If we’re somewhere near the bottom now, then we’ll capture the beginning of what comes next. Something will cause the turn. Why not us?”
“I think you need to say more,” Logan said and laughed. Jack was beside him, wrapped in his own camouflage, but for the first time since Heathrow, Logan wasn’t alone.

Logan and Jack, now at the back of the group, jogged to close the gap and eventually caught up with the others. His calves burned, and his shoulders throbbed; Anika walked closer to the center of the group, and Ryba stopped humming altogether.

As they crested a low ridge, the treeline opened up once more. Anika and Ryba were the first to crouch down, and the others quickly followed their lead. The clearing below was a dead zone. The mud had hardened in churned ridges, and half-buried concrete ruins jutted from the earth. Buildings had once stood here, perhaps an outpost or something more significant. An old Ministry of Emergency sign lay face down in the dirt, its corner torn, its body twisted sideways.

Logan dropped his pack, crouched low, and retrieved the drone. He then executed a flawless cold drop, no warm-up, just as they practiced before. He launched the drone in one fluid motion and watched the climb, stabilizing into a reconnaissance spiral, from the drone's eye: miles of "temporary damage," farmland the same as the old man's.

The clearing opened up, revealing concrete remnants covered in moss. Old roads branched out from the center, resembling scar tissue. At the heart of the clearing sat the hatch, surrounded by poured concrete and overgrown earth, a bunker mound hidden beneath decades of decay.

Logan brought the drone back, landing it in his falconer’s fist. He set the controller down, stood, and squared himself toward Anika. The stance came out uneven, heels too far apart, shoulders overcorrected, chin lifted.
“Anika, the perimeter’s clear.”
Anika looked over. “Thank you, Logan.”
Reese shifted his rifle. “Proactive and productive. Who are you?”
Logan didn’t take the bait. “Anika, the ground is dead around that hatch. Frost, concrete, and our heat signatures. Nothing else,” he said.
Ryba strolled forward, coat lifting in the wind. “This is where I earn my fee.”
Anika looked from the empty scan to the steel door. “Then take the lead.”

Ryba descended into the clearing first, pulled more by gravity than by choice. Jean Pierre slunk forward, his weather cloak swallowing the light. Felix mirrored him, shotgun up, shielding Elena’s egress. Reese trailed with his rifle held low, next was Logan, and Anika brought up the rear.

The eight of them crossed the open expanse in single file. Around them, churned dirt and salt-burned moss surrounded an old mound of earth and poured concrete that resembled a man-made tumor, with no cover and no way to deviate from the path.

Ryba stopped beside the steel hatch; a gloved hand hovered above the surface before slowly lowering. The others gathered behind him. The oxidized steel of the hatch radiated a dense, penetrating chill, its rusted seams choked with thick, wet moss. Weather or hands had stripped the paint. A barely legible radiation marker still clung to the hinge: a faded, but clear yellow sign with a red triangle surrounding a red trefoil.
Reese muttered, “That’s comforting.”
They didn’t laugh, not even Logan.
“No choice.”
Jean Pierre knelt beside the hatch, the mud-stained hem of his poncho scraping over packed dirt. He tapped the locking mechanism once. “Someone has used this,” he said.
Logan said. "Recently?"

Jean Pierre’s gaze stayed fixed on the tree line at the edge of the clearing. “Recently.” A low rustle rose behind them, and the group turned as one, weapons raised, eyes moving across trunks, brush, and the last open ground. Nothing emerged except wind moving through the branches.
Logan watched Anika as she approached, pressing both palms to the seal. Anika shook her head. "There are hundreds of Soviet-era sites. Most are junkyards," she said, and stopped.
Reese tossed a cigarette. "Define ‘worse.”
Ukraine kept dark places, old fortresses, biohazard facilities, black sites that didn’t appear on any coalition map.
Ryba didn’t glance back as he replied, his voice even. “Solitude makes a fine judge. Shut a man in the dark, no sound, no sky. Strip the inputs and wait for what breaks first.”
“Do volunteers always get the best assignments?” Jack said.
Reese snorted. “Yeah, this goes straight to the brochure.”
Ryba didn’t smile. “Americans are the worst of us.”
Logan cut in. “And you’re not afraid?”
Ryba sat up. “Afraid? I deal with reality on the ground. The rest of you deal in delay.”
Felix said, “Hell of a way to stomach radiation poisoning.”
Anika’s voice cut through the farmhouse. “Rotate watches every sixty minutes. Electronics go completely dark.”
Logan looked up from his whisper pin. “Define completely dark for us.”
“No whisper tech,” she said.
Anika crouched low, pressing her palm to the ground beside the steel.
“Felix,” she said. “Toss me your shovel.”
