Felix unslung the entrenching tool and tossed it to her. Anika caught the shovel with one hand, steadied herself, and drove the blade into the seam where rust had consumed most of the gap. The shovel took hold as she used her weight to leverage and break the seal. A groan came from the warped hinges, followed by a puff of stale air from the depths below.
Anika drove the shovel in again and pulled the hatch open. The slab dropped inward to darkness beneath her.
Felix leaned back on his pack, grinning. “She needed my gear to do that.”

Jack's laughter echoed around them; Elena studied the hatch as Reese shifted, seeming to dig into the ground beneath him. Above, the wind coursed through the salt-scorched trees, sending dry branches rattling. Ryba lay sprawled on his back.
Ryba stirred. “You should follow her,” he said.
Logan turned. “You’re not going in?”
Ryba grinned in response. “Never first. Last, preferably.”
Logan checked Reese, then Jack, then Jean Pierre, whose hand had already settled on his sidearm. Elena remained still beside him as the wind shifted through the opening.
Anika’s head popped out of the darkness, reappearing atop the mound, silhouetted against a pale sky. “The way is clear,” she said.
With that, she disappeared again, and Logan followed without thinking. At the top, Logan saw a cavity carved into a hill of dead earth and buried metal.
Reese dragged himself over the last ridge. “Hope we’re not walking into a live reactor.”
Elena followed him. “How would you know?” Then she dropped into the dark.
Logan crouched, grit grinding into his palm at the seam. He grabbed the edge, pulled himself through, and disappeared into the dark.

Her flashlight beam pierced the darkness ahead, across a wide service tunnel built for clandestine movement. Aging Soviet concrete wept moisture beneath black mold, and dust around the column with a greasy weight churned by many footsteps.

Ahead of Elena, Anika slowed where two tunnels split beyond the light. Her gloved hand traced faded Cyrillic on the crumbling wall, identifying a Soviet access point and the remnants of a Spetsnaz fallback route from the Cold War era.

Felix checked his radio while Jean Pierre pressed ahead, his rifle hardware ticking against the stock. The air grew heavier with damp stone, and a rusted junction box ticked. In the blackout, the tunnel sloped downward in a long spiral, Soviet-designed for deep descent. Hours blurred as the damp narrowed around them, and Jack’s voice finally broke through the dark.
“What exactly is the plan?” Jack asked, his voice sounding smaller beneath the concrete.
Reese grunted from somewhere behind her before answering. “Go live from Ukraine.”
“No, I mean, what’s the plan?”
Elena stopped. “We say we’re going live,” she said, “but we haven’t decided anything. Do you want to talk about who is  speaking?”
Reese exchanged a glance with Logan, who shrugged in response. “Not much to discuss,” Logan said. “Reese and I aren’t public speakers.”
"You can talk and Jack can film," Reese said. "We're here for the ambiance."
The decision settled over her, made without her, the expectation tightening around the broadcast before a single camera had gone live.
“Elena,” he said. “Jokes aside. You okay with the plan?”
Her eyes stayed on the tunnel ahead, where the flashlight beam dissolved into black concrete. “Of course,” she said.
Logan rubbed the back of his neck beneath his collar. “Well, in that case, Jack, what would you say? Let’s hear the opening.”
Jack raised his hands, cleared his throat, and began. “Hi. I’m Elena Morales. The resistance is back, and we’re here to fight the Russians. Stay tuned.”
Laughter echoed in the confined space while Elena kept her eyes on the darkness ahead.
“A bit heavy-handed, don’t you think?” Logan said.
“Needs more gravitas,” Reese added. “What about I was dead, but now I’m back with the red, white, and blue.”
“You’ve thought about what you’ll say, yes?” Ryba said. Elena didn’t slow her steps. Ryba matched her stride with his hands loose in his pockets, as if they were walking.
“All of the world will be watching,” he said, unbothered. “You don’t want to be caught unprepared, do you?”
He leaned closer, voice above old stone. “You know the enemy will have armies in Kyiv, and if the Medyka attack fails, do you know what they’ll blame?”
Elena kept her eyes on the tunnel ahead. “This isn’t all about me.”
Ryba gave a soft, amused chuckle beside her. “No, of course not. But you’ll be the one people remember.”
His words dropped into the dark where she had nothing ready to answer them.
“We are all the bait, but you’ll be the face. You understand that, don’t you?”

The march pressed deeper into the tunnel, footfalls scraping old grit while the walls tightened close enough to punish a swinging rifle. Elena drifted left to keep distance from Ryba as Felix and Jean Pierre whispered ahead in harsh German and French.

The ceiling dipped lower, moisture coursing cold along rusted cables above. Logan’s light caught the droplets and fractured them against the concrete. Elena ducked beneath the lowest cables as the passage tightened around them. Then a click resonated from the depths ahead, precise and patient. Anika raised her fist, and the group halted. Rifles came ready around Elena as figures emerged from the dark in torn cloth and scavenged gear, layers of dust obscuring their faces.
The scouts led them deeper, toward lanterns hanging from rusted pipe hooks and cracked tile exposed in wavering bands.

The scout remained focused, staring straight ahead, offering no acknowledgment as Felix pressed on. “My cousin, Iryna. She was with the resistance and a fierce fighter stationed in Kyiv before the fall.”

Still, the scout’s gaze stayed fixed while the small battery lamp at his hip buzzed against the dark. Felix's voice softened, a glimmer of hope lingering in his words. “Iryna? I’m her cousin Felix. Please, she would have mentioned me.” Only the scout's lamp kept buzzing.
Reese’s voice cut through the lantern-lit corridor. “Hey, Felix. I think these guys want us to be quiet.”
Ryba’s voice returned at her side, too close. “You don’t know what to say, do you?”
“But if not now, when?” Ryba asked, matching her pace. “What if the burden didn’t have to be yours? Isn’t being seen enough?”
She quickened her pace, but he pressed on. “I could do this. If you don’t have the words, when the time comes, Ryba can be speaker instead.”
Elena stopped as the others kept walking ahead, their footsteps echoing without her.
Ryba’s voice dropped beneath the footfalls. “You think I don’t see it? The hesitation?”
Anika doubled back and placed herself between Elena and Ryba.
“Ryba,” she said. “Enough.”
Ryba smirked as though the warning had missed him. “You need me, Anika. I’m not one of your pet Americans.”

Anika moved before his smirk finished forming, sidestepping his stance and driving her knee into his gut. Ryba hit the tunnel wall and collapsed to one knee, the grin gone as Anika crouched beside him.
“You’re job is over, Ryba.” Anika locked eyes with Elena beneath the tunnel light. “You will find the words.”
Elena looked back once the tunnel bends hid Ryba from sight.
“Should we salute Anika?” Reese asked, loud enough for the scouts ahead to hear.
Logan shrugged and replied, “I mean, no, right?”
Anika rejoined them with her steps still sharp from the confrontation. She tapped Reese on the arm and slapped Logan on the shoulder as she passed.
“Thank you,” she said.
“For what?” Logan blinked in confusion.
“For showing respect. Don’t be a Ryba.” She continued walking without looking back.

The scouts dragged aside a rusted maintenance panel and slipped through a body-wide gap. The stone narrowed until shoulders scraped against the rock, and the rot shifted among rust, engine oil, stagnant water, black mold, and wet paper.
The lead scout tested each stone with his heel before shifting weight. A thin wire shimmered low across the path, and beyond it a chasm opened along rusted steel.

They pressed on through snares, shallow water, and dead ends until one corridor glistened with a chemical sheen. Elena struck the borrowed lighter once, revealing black residue beneath the water, then followed the scout’s heels through each turn.
The passage widened into figures pressed along the walls, first a handful and then dozens, rifle safeties and sling clips ticking nervously in the lantern light.
The Kyiv Resistance had gathered shoulder to shoulder, rifles slung and eyes burning beneath dust, and no one spoke.
Anika spoke with the scouts surrounding her and pointed down the passage. “We need to surface to make a diversion for an offensive to retake Medyka.”

One figure advanced with quiet authority, studied Anika, and whistled once. A dark gate creaked open behind him, spilling generator light over damp stone, cable runs, water buckets, and the first narrow beds beyond.
“We can help you surface,” he said. “Come see what’s left of us.”

Inside, the cavern opened high enough to swallow Elena’s flashlight. Thin daylight cut through ceiling cracks onto trays of seedlings, water barrels, moss-dark drainage seams, rough doorways carved into stone, and cook fires warming the settlement by degrees rather than comfort.

Hundreds, perhaps thousands, filled the space in purposeful motion beneath old Soviet arches. Supplies dangled from netting, shovels rang against concrete, and every strike widened the hidden city by another inch.
“Felix?” A woman emerged through the crowd with a faded NATO patch on her uniform, cropped hair, and a German accent worn raw by years below ground.
She wrapped Felix in a tight hug, then checked his face for injury before sweeping her eyes across Elena, Reese, Jack, and Logan.
“And where are the rest of you?” she asked, speaking in English to Elena, Reese, Jack, and Logan.
Iryna’s stare was neither suspicious nor forgiving, but an inventory of the circumstances laid bare. “We are the cavalry,” Felix offered, a quiet declaration that did not calm the room.
“Hmph.” Her hand tightened briefly on his arm before releasing. “We’ve been expecting a few more of you.”
Iryna led them through phosphorescent command marks into a chamber serving as both command post and armory.
Hurried Ukrainian broke around the center table while concern threaded through every voice. Felix barked a German demand, and Jean Pierre checked the safety on the MAS rifle without a click.

Anika spoke over the commotion, and the room bent toward her. Ukrainian snapped from her in short, tactical bursts. She pointed to Elena, then Jack, crossed to a fighter, pulled the knife from his belt, and raised the blade high. Rifle butts struck concrete and fists hit the table.
Elena crossed to Anika and pulled her attention away from the fighters. “Can I have five minutes?”

Elena left the command room and followed the lantern-lit tunnels. Dugouts cut the cavern wall in uneven rows, some packed with bunks, others widened into gathering spaces where resistance fighters sorted ammunition beside water pumps and cook fires. Children moved between cots and crates while shovel blades scraped farther down the passage.

She continued alone past rock, supply stacks, and handmade shelters until the rally noise thinned behind her. Ahead, an old woman sat beside a tin-pit fire, the low flame catching on yarn moving through her thick, weathered grip. Elena reached the lighter in her pocket and left the cap closed.
“USA?” the woman rasped in broken English, her Ukrainian accent evident.
Elena knelt beside the old woman’s fire. “Yes, and we’re here to help take back the city.”
The woman tilted her head toward the passage. “You have an army?”
“No, we’re creating a disruption. The plan is to draw the enemy in and open things up at another battle.”
The woman studied Elena before responding with sudden firmness. “You are here to make our resistance your bait?” Elena didn’t respond as the old woman continued.
“We all grew up on the same songs, you know. We share those lullabies, that’s all we share.”
Elena stood, removed a ration pack while the old woman watched the seal, and then extended her hand.
“This is a family war,” the woman said.
Elena walked a step, then stopped beneath rough tiers of suspended lights. She removed a second ration and turned back.
“What’s your part in this, child?”
“I’m the bait,” Elena said.
The woman squinted through the tin-pit firelight. “So America is back.”
Elena met her gaze without reaching for the lighter. “Not yet.”
The old woman watched her for a long moment. “This is the end, isn’t it?”

The underground city loomed darker as she walked back. Even in a complicated new place, Elena wasn’t worried about retracing her steps. As a child, she and Andrew built labyrinths in the woods, doubling back and laying false trails. They would separate and go quiet, not to avoid getting lost, but to invite the challenge. She counted every turn, every step, every pause.

When Elena returned, Reese gripped her shoulder while she passed him the first thermos cup and kept walking. Jack worked the camera rig, Logan painted camouflage across resistance faces, and Reese caught her up: hold the public places long enough for Kyiv to wake, then let the metro stairwells feed a bottom-up offensive through the city.
Elena entered the command room and said, “Anika. I’m ready.”
Anika’s answer came in English for all of them. “Then we go.”
Packs settled across shoulders, and Elena nested the thermos cups before following Anika and Iryna toward a chimney-sized tunnel, climbing into darkness.
“Knives first,” Reese said. “No shooting until they see us.”

Anika’s hand flexed over her knife while Jack tested camera placements, Reese checked ammunition, Logan cleaned his drone lens, Felix packed shotgun shells, and Jean Pierre judged the room through the ticking sling hardware of his precision rifle.
A steel ladder rose toward the sealed opening above, and Elena placed one hand on the rung before climbing.
The ladder was cold beneath Elena’s gloves, the corroded rungs scraped by countless hurried climbs before hers.
“Breaching in ninety,” Anika murmured as light glowed above them.
Gear scuffed on the ladder while the countdown pressed them upward. Freezing air slashed Elena’s face when she emerged into Kyiv, the ruined expanse too bright and open after the tunnel.

The square beneath the monument stretched wide, encircled by crumbling buildings and charred glass towers. At the center, the Independence Monument rose through soot and the scars of occupation, while Berehynia, Ukraine’s guardian, held her scorched gold above the gray sky.

Elena anchored herself beneath the goddess of protection, over cracked stone, dried blood, enemy banners, and facades burned beneath flags raised Moscow red. She looked up once, then back to Jack and the camera.

Resistance fighters fanned out from the tunnel as small teams swept stairwells, snipers climbed windowless frames, and others hauled oil drums into alleys while every sound struck hard across the square.

Elena turned toward Jack, who concentrated on assembling the broadcast array at the base of the monument. He moved with determined precision, every action deliberate. Beside him, Jean Pierre gripped the steel of his precision rifle, scanning the sky for the remote drone swarms. Fire escapes rattled as fighters climbed and disappeared into buildings to avoid the drones.
Elena held at the edge of the open square, the camera before her, and the next few minutes deciding whether any words would matter.

At the edge of the square, Anika sprinted toward the rusted shell of an old tram, half-buried among the debris from a collapsed building. She slid behind the tram to establish cover while Jean Pierre vaulted over a broken barricade, disappearing into the remains of a bombed-out office tower. Several soldiers armed with long rifles followed him, matching his swift pace. Felix veered to the western edge, avoiding firelight as he rushed ahead. He knelt behind rust and ruin, shotgun braced, watching the square.

Logan waved once and sent his drone above the monument square, where the unit stabilized at altitude. Jack’s camera hummed to life, green LED pulsing. A blue light steadied as the broadcast locked, cutting a hard line along Elena’s cheek. Elena tracked the barricades and sightlines while Jack adjusted the rig one last time.
“The hard part is over. We’re here,” Logan shouted.

Elena opened her mouth, and the first rifle report cut through the square. Logan jolted as if pulled backward by a wire, held upright for one second before the next dropped him to the concrete. His controller struck beside him. His drone kept circling overhead without a pilot, awaiting commands that would not come.

Elena snapped the safety off, on, then off again. Her rifle crossed the rooftop, the drainpipe, the balcony, and the broken kiosk without choosing. Beyond Logan’s body, the Maidan arranged itself around the shot: muzzle flash behind the drainpipe, birds lifting from the fountain, and the first edge of the drone swarm darkening the pale sky.
Jean Pierre answered first. His rifle fired twice from the elevated position. The figure behind the drainpipe staggered backward, and the sniper rifle struck stone with a dull thud.
Reese started toward Logan before Felix cut across his path and drove one hard forearm against his armor.
“No time,” Felix said.

Logan’s controller blinked against his vest, still clipped in place despite the open hand resting on the stone beside him. Above the square, his drone held position three seconds too long before drifting half a meter toward the monument and correcting against the wind. A small whisper prompt chirped from the cracked screen beneath the gunfire, requesting manual confirmation.

Elena’s rifle came into her shoulder, and rooftop, drainpipe, balcony, tram shell, and broken kiosk locked into firing lanes around her. Gunfire rolled street by street while Logan’s body remained in Jack’s frame.
Felix’s shotgun cracked nearby, Anika’s suppressed fire tapped through the Maidan, and Elena tracked the battle as chaos widened in concentric rings.
The old kamikaze drone units had thicker fans and made a heavier buzz than the new models. The first one slammed into three scouts on a balcony as flame and steel erupted.
Jack turned away from Logan, keeping the camera steady as the rig’s blue light pulsed in the gunpowder haze. “Tell them what you see.”
“This is Kyiv,” Elena said. “This is live.”
Jack moved the lens from Berehynia to the tunnel mouths where fighters climbed into daylight with rifles, med packs, radios, and children wrapped in coats too large for them.
“The city was not empty. The resistance did not leave. They buried themselves under the city because no corridor came.”
A mortar burst above the square, dropping stone across the barricades. Elena slammed a fresh magazine into her rifle and pointed toward the southern smoke.
Jack swung the camera where she pointed while Logan’s body remained at the edge of the frame, controller blinking against his vest as his drone circled without correction.
“They told you we were dead. They lied about our bodies, and they lied about this city.”
Another explosion rolled through the Maidan; fighters dragged a wounded soldier behind a marble slab while Anika’s squad closed around the first Russian flank.
She pointed toward the tunnel mouth as another family climbed into daylight, into artillery strikes. “Rupert, if Medyka is moving, move faster. You need to open the corridor,” she said.

On the western edge of the square, unarmed civilians began emerging from alleys and broken shopfronts. Anika pressed a pistol into the nearest man’s hands and shouted for the fighters behind her to arm the rest.

Elena tracked Anika across the plaza, her voice sharp as she barked commands. The fighters responded to her orders as each step gathered rifles, pulling magazines from the fallen. She handed a weapon to a woman with silver hair and another to a boy with a brief, firm gesture and exchanged simple nods.
“Take position. Hold the line,” Anika instructed.

Anika grabbed a panicked civilian by the shoulder, shoved a rifle into his hands, and pointed him toward the nearest pile of sandbags. The crowd grew, drawing forth from alleyways and shattered shopfronts, and from blown-out tram cars and hiding places in cellars. Some hands were already armed, while others brandished metal rods, kitchen knives, and glass bottles wrapped in oil-soaked cloth.

Jack adjusted his camera, sweeping left and then right until the square opened across the frame. Elena, positioned at the center, had the superior vantage point. Across the Maidan, citizens took cover behind hastily constructed barricades. Others moved cautiously toward the perimeter, assessing their surroundings. A woman ignited a Molotov cocktail using a flame from a burning car and dashed toward the southern street. In the center, the lighter pressed a hard square against Elena’s thigh as the drones crossed overhead and the damaged camera fan kept ticking.
“My name is Elena Morales,” she said, her voice rising above wind, flames, and gunfire. “They claimed Kyiv couldn’t be retaken.”
Jack kept the camera on her until something went wrong in the smoke behind Elena.

Eight sleek steel legs moved atop the structures at the square’s edge, then the spider drone surged through a breached wall, minivan-sized and black, its talons striking pavement as it locked onto new targets.

Along with predatory grace, each leg bore surgical blades. The pilot halted mid-run, then climbed the nearest building. The spider plunged from the rooftop, eight limbs slicing downward with deadly accuracy. One fighter was seized and slammed against the wall, a single shot ringing out when his rifle dropped. The drone’s eye shifted, distinguishing real targets from rubble and revealing a compact turret that unfolded with mechanical precision on the others.

During her final year at NGCS, these models remained theoretical, as was typical of the charter network's coursework. As new technologies emerged, pilots and maintenance technicians were pre-trained to deploy them effectively. A remote pilot made the behavior unmistakable; the drone cornered three resistance fighters near a burned-out metro kiosk. They froze with mouths open as the twin barrels locked onto their chests and the turret servos whirred higher.

The drone ascended the building back to the rooftops, limbs functioning as climbing picks, while blood dripped down behind, streaking the facade. People within the square sought refuge in hiding places if they could, but most fought the enemy soldiers wave after wave as they advanced on the square. Elena remained rooted in place, her eyes forward as Jack continued to film. Around the square, shouting surged among the resistance, as barbaric yells mingled with the enemy's own war cries. Gunfire punctuated the smoky atmosphere, bursts echoing from barricades, shattered windows, and crumbling stairs.
A resistance fighter hurled a Molotov cocktail at the thorax. The drone fired, the fighter went down, and Elena caught herself as the machine circled closer.

The drone moved clockwise, sector by sector, until its apparent chaos resolved into formation. Elena held through the pivot, sidestepped through the scan, and stopped when the spider climbed. One rear leg lagged under load, and the cooling vent opened beneath the thorax before each reset.

Elena shifted three steps away from Jack, splitting the drone’s line between the camera and the shooter. When the vent opened again, she raised her rifle. The shot hit behind the third leg at the joint beneath the spider’s knee. Elena moved before the drone finished scanning where she had stood, positioning herself next inside the pilot’s upcoming blind spot.
Her second round struck the same joint and locked the leg rigid. The drone swung wide as onboard correction took over, blades deploying for the final melee strike.

Elena remained in position after the second shot. The spider drone pivoted, turning to face her once more. The third leg rested in a guarded position behind where the pilot anticipated the incoming strike. She struck the same target a third consecutive time, causing the drone’s leg to buckle. The drone's front crashed into the stone, releasing smoke from beneath the chassis. The red lens blinked once before Elena advanced with her rifle still raised.
Behind her, Jack kept filming. “Elena, we are still live! I caught all of that. How did you do that?”

Jack captured each detail of Elena’s victory. Surviving resistance fighters gathered in the square, their eyes on Elena, who shouldered her rifle and looked past the dead spider drone at the Maidan beyond it. Its black legs rose from the broken stone in a frozen arch, less wreckage now than monument, another shape added to the square’s long inventory of ruin. Flames flickered at the edge of the square. The sky loomed gray and heavy, but the for the first time that long day, Kyiv was calm.
As she crouched low, Elena spoke to herself. “Same moves,” she said.

Felix reached Elena first, disregarding the broadcast as he moved over the fallen spider drone and clapped her on the back, laughter erupting from him as his radio crackled to life. “Medyka is secure. The enemy falls back.”

Elena counted the survivors in their group as the sky erupted again. Another wave of artillery thundered overhead; shells struck low and hard, the initial barrage tearing through the southern Maidan. Stone erupted in waves, and resistance fighters vanished from view. The subsequent heavy artillery strike landed even closer, causing Elena’s ears to ring painfully. By the third strike, her hearing collapsed into the hard tick of a damaged drone somewhere inside the smoke. Through the haze, Elena’s voice rose above the terror.
“Another wave! We have to hold!”

The remaining fighters dragged wounded behind cover and slammed magazines into place. A girl with blood across her cheek raised a Kalashnikov and shouted, “Slava Ukraini!” before Russian foot soldiers advanced again through the smoke.
Jean Pierre fired from elevation with measured precision. One shot, then another, and a fourth Russian rolled forward with a grenade as Reese rushed to cut him off.

Anika led the counterattack through the broken columns with a knife in one hand and a pistol in the other. Felix shouted commands at the western barricade, his voice rough and commanding. Jean Pierre dispatched another Russian while the spent casing clicked once against broken stone. As the ground shook, Anika cut through the smoke and took command. Anika transformed from a mere combatant into a force of resolve. She surged forward through debris, smoke curling in her wake. Her rifle discharged, claiming two targets with precise shots. A swift pivot followed, leading to a swift knife strike. One Russian faltered, another dropped his weapon, and a third fled. Anika turned the tide amid the ongoing artillery strikes.
“Jack!” Felix shouted from a distance. “You need to move!”

Russian troops flooded the square while resistance fighters answered from barricades, windows, and shattered stairs. Motion flashed from behind the kiosk; Elena rose before the grenade landed. The explosion erupted with a deafening roar, rupturing the air and throwing concrete dust across the square. Elena staggered against the broken tiles, ears ringing again, rifle still locked against her shoulder.

In the concrete-dust, the camera’s light flickered through the smoke, lying flat against the ground. Jack was down beside the rig, blood soaking heavy through his shirt while jagged iron shrapnel held beneath his ribs. His hand stayed on the camera body, holding the square in frame while his other hand dragged across broken stone.

Elena crossed the open ground before Reese reached him. Rounds snapped across the kiosk behind her and cut pale dust from the stone. She dropped beside Jack hard enough for pain to break through her ribs, pulled a dressing from her pouch, and pressed both hands against the spreading blood beneath his vest. Warmth came through her gloves too fast as she packed the dressing under the broken plate edge and watched the gauze darken.
Jack moved her toward the camera and the small courier drone half buried in concrete dust beside his hip.

Elena looked from the wound beneath her hands to the machine beside him. The blood would not stop, but the latch might close. She tore the cartridge free from the rig, forced open the courier drone’s narrow compartment, and seated the proof while blood smeared across the contact strip.
Jack’s eyes stayed fixed on the indicator.

Elena wiped the strip against her sleeve, cleared grit from the slot with her thumbnail, and seated the cartridge again. The latch refused to budge until she struck the casing twice with the heel of her palm, forcing the compartment shut.

Elena turned the indicator toward Jack long enough for him to see the light change. His hand tightened around the camera grip, keeping the feed alive while she thumbed the launch command. The courier stuttered against the stone, dipped under the first wash of rotor force, then lifted between them on a thin electric whine.

The drone rose through ash and smoke, past Logan’s drone still circling above the square without correction, carrying Jack’s proof west toward Poland. Elena stayed by Jack until the courier cleared the monument’s broken sightline. Then she took the blood-slick camera rig from his hand, braced the frame against the stone, and turned the lens back toward the living fight.

With nowhere to turn under the artillery strikes, Elena pressed playback. The screen showed the lantern-lit tunnel beneath Kyiv, the farmhouse near Lublin, the burned media vans at the gate, and the campfire where Anika, Jean Pierre, and Felix first entered their war.

A blast scattered grit across the glass as Elena wiped the screen with Jack’s blood drying across her glove, and the camera recovered to Logan beneath Berehynia, camouflage drying on his cheeks, smiling ear to ear.
Reese’s hand closed over the screen, covering Jack’s face with a gentle palm across his eyes.
The artillery had stopped somewhere beyond her notice. Dust settled in slow sheets across the broken stone, and the ringing in her ears had thinned enough for Reese’s breathing to sound close.

Iryna wrapped Felix’s arm around her shoulder, guiding him away from a blown-out barricade. He limped through dust and blood, thigh dark beneath the torn uniform, yet he stayed upright and barked orders at the fighters nearest the smoke. Cheers and laughter mixed with mourning cries as news of victory moved through the square.

Elena stood apart from the celebration with Jack’s camera heavy in her hands. Across the Maidan, Anika stood where Slava would have stood. Elena killed the spider, but Anika broke the enemy’s will under their own artillery.

Anika knelt beside Logan’s body while above the square, Logan’s drone hovered at a hundred feet, a loyal machine observing the scene. The drone remained steady, a sentinel enshrined in the air. Anika’s hands bore the stains of blood and earth, but her movements were certain. Before Anika, Logan remained untouched, still curled on the ground. She eased the controller from his hand, and the drone tilted before descending in a precise line through the wind.

Anika caught the drone with one hand and placed the casing on Logan, centering the casing above his armor while the controller rested beside his hand. Afterward, she reached up and smeared blood and ash from her cheek, gently drawing close his eyelids. The gesture hung in the air as the square’s noise thinned around the bodies. The wind dropped, and only Logan's drone casing ticked against his armor where Anika had placed it. Anika left the drone and the controller as grave goods on his body.

Elena helped Reese carry Jack across the broken stone and lower him beside Logan. Reese knelt beside Jack and worked through his pockets slowly, one side and then the other, finding nothing but dust, blood, and torn lining. Elena reached past him, closed Jack’s empty vest pouch, and tightened the loose camera strap.

Elena raised one blood-dark glove toward the nearest resistance fighters, and they began gathering the dead shoulder to shoulder beneath the monument. She watched the Russian drone swarm wheel north, rotor ticks thinning over the rooftops. Voices rose beneath the wind as survivors surfaced from tunnels.

A girl with bandages around her hands and a sunflower pinned to her coat, ran forward and wrapped both arms around Elena’s legs. Elena rested her palm on the girl’s shoulder while the murmurs across the Maidan grew stronger.

Standing by the immobilized spider they spoke her name. They had watched the resistance emerge beneath the city, Logan die first, Jack fall with his camera in his hand, and Elena kneel beside both of them with blood on her gloves. Elena set Jack’s camera carefully on the stone beside her.

Morning light cut between the buildings around Independence Square, striping the pavement in long, hard bands. The light moved through the columns, shattered windows, and over the golden statue of Berehynia at the top of the monument in the middle of the square, still standing facing East. A woman with a bloodied bandage across her neck knelt beside the center of the square and repainted blue and yellow stripes onto a piece of scrap canvas. Two old men erected a scaffolding and tied a hand-drawn sign across the top. Київ вільний. Kyiv is free.

He started stacking the rifles at the north wall of the square without being asked. They scattered across the plaza in the way that weapons end up after a battle, dropped, abandoned, pulled from hands that no longer needed them. He walked the perimeter, gathered them, carried two or three at a time, and set them against the wall, barrels pointing up, evenly spaced. His hands already knew.

The wall was broken concrete and old brick, and the rifles leaned against it the way baseball bats lean against a dugout wall after the last game of the season. The way they looked when everyone had already left.

He kept walking in the square. He kept collecting. Logan's gear was still near the eastern approach where he'd gone down. Jack's bag was further out. Reese brought those back, too, and set them apart from the rifles, against a different section of the wall. He stood there a moment; the controller was still clipped to the case, tight enough for a launch. Then he went back for the next one.
The square was quieter now. Smoke still rose from the northeast. Somewhere behind him, survivors moved through the rubble. He didn't turn around. The row of rifles grew longer.
To the north of the plaza, Anika hissed instructions while managing the triage efforts. Felix and Jean Pierre worked alongside her, piling supply containers to construct a defensive boundary.
Anika crossed from triage without breaking stride. She didn't look at either of them. "Logan and Jack's bodies will rest here. We need to decide what to do with you both now."
"We must talk to Rupert immediately, Anika," Elena insisted, her tone resolute.

Anika tilted her head forward, making the gesture a door, not a welcome. Reese and Elena followed her through, leaving Logan and Jack carefully laid out behind them. Across the square, the enemy stacked with all the care of unloading mulch.

Anika walked across the broken plaza toward Felix and Jean Pierre, who clustered around a cracked tactical board salvaged from the rubble. Felix drove the pencil into the plywood. The tip snapped. He kept writing.
"Too many approaches," he said. "The city's shape is wrong for holding; they'll come from the northeast or cut across the river. If they bring tactical nukes, none of what we plan now will matter."
Jean Pierre went quiet, his eyes cold. "We mine the crossings," he finally said. "We can rig the intersections too."
"Not enough," Anika responded. "We'll need more than that."
Felix gave her a tired look. "And what about the prisoners?"
There were hundreds: some Russian regulars, others foreign contractors, others local collaborators.
"They'll slow us down," Jean Pierre added. "When the counterattack comes, they'll knife us in the back."

Anika's gaze moved past them toward the square's center, where survivors still emerged from the tunnel mouths in slow, uneven lines, many covering their faces against the lingering chemical stink. She shifted her weight and looked to Felix's cousin, Iryna. "Take the prisoners underground," she said. "The tunnels are still full of our dead from the spider's phosphorus strike, children, elderly, anyone who could not outrun the fire. Put shovels in the prisoner's hands and make them useful." Her eyes moved once across the captives. "If they obey, do not harm them. We are not the enemy. But if any one of them gives you cause, shoot them where they stand and leave them beside the others. Do you copy?"

Felix and Jean Pierre turned away as Iryna accepted the work. Reese hunched over Jack's camera,  finding the aperture ring the way Jack's had. A medic taught a boy to bandage with nothing but duct tape and a bottle, sunflower seeds passed hand to hand. No one asked who led them.

Anika crossed the plaza and climbed the rusted chassis of a half-sunken Russian tank, her voice cutting through the wind, facing fighters, civilians, and children. Reese stood back with Jack's camera at his side. He watched the crowd, not Anika.
"No reinforcements, no air cover, only us."
She tapped her boot once against the armor beneath her. The hollow sound echoed.
"This city doesn't need heroes. Kyiv needs orders."
She turned on the tank, eyes on Jean Pierre near the tactical board.
"This is Jean Pierre Marchand. Former Captain, French Alpine Chasseurs. He led five defenses, and three of them held."
A soft laugh rippled out.
"Jean Pierre will find the Russian armory; it will be buried in one of the old police stations or behind a decoy storehouse. We need rifles in the hands of every able-bodied person."
Anika kept going.

"Felix," she called, turning. "You have the tunnel teams. Everything that came up from below with us, fighters, medics, techs, I want them shaping rings with shovels in hand again forming positions. Sentry drones and small arms up front, fallback layers behind, tiered all the way back to the Maidan. When you need to, retreat to the center. Clear?"
Felix saluted and moved with haste.
Anika pointed toward the southern edge of the square.
"Clear the non-combatants. Mark the exfil routes. When the barrage drops, panic is their primary weapon. Strip it from them. Don't let chaos do the enemy's work for them."

They weren't clapping; they were in motion, forming lines, children weaving between crates, a priest passing ammo to a teen, a man in a tracksuit hauling sandbags and barking orders, more arriving by firelight, drawn by her voice and stance.
Anika pushed through the crowd to him and Elena, Logan and Jack's covered bodies with Jack's camera between them.

Eventually, her tired, sideways smile caught Reese's eye. "I have a plan for nearly every person in this square," she continued. "Fighters, cooks, techs. Everyone." She gazed at each of them in turn. "Except for two."
Reese turned the drone controller over in his hands. He set it down without using it.
Reese picked up Jack's camera; Elena grabbed her rifle.
Anika leaned back on her heels. "What are we to do with you?"
Elena stood with something older than defiance, something settled before she had entered the square.
“You don’t have to send us away. I can fight, and so can Reese.”
Anika's expression didn't change. "I know you can," she said. "That's not the problem."
"Then what is?" Reese said.
Anika brushed ash from her sleeves and closed the distance. Her voice dropped. "You don't understand how big this has become.”
Elena shook her head. "I'm not a problem."
"Yes, you are," Anika said. "You are a liability. You can't stay."
Reese circled them both. “So what is this? You’re exiling us?”
"I am sending you away so you don't die."
"You can't send us home."

"I don't have the resources to evacuate you," Anika said. "But your presence degrades my defensive line. You leave." She looked beyond the Maidan. People were still building defenses, organizing food stations, and helping the wounded into makeshift shelters. "You became a weight I can't protect. And I can't spare the people to try."
"Anika," Elena said, stepping closer, almost a whisper. "There's something you should know."
Anika stopped talking, brow knitting.
Elena dropped her voice. "I know their pilot."
“Who?”
“The spider drone,” Elena said. “The one that burned the square. I know how they move,” Elena said. “The patterns were familiar. The pilot’s name is Andrew.”
Anika ejected her magazine, checked the rounds, and reloaded without looking up. “So he’s American?”

Reese looked down at the controller in her fist. Logan’s blood still marked the seam near the dead screen. Across the room, Jack’s camera sat on the table with its strap cut through and the lens turned toward nothing.
Elena’s eyes did not leave Anika. “Yes.”

Anika responded sharply. "Did you think there weren't volunteers from your country on both sides?" Anika turned and redirected her gaze east. Anika crossed the plaza toward the makeshift command post where the comms rig was mounted beneath a tarp and lashed into a building's wiring. Then she whispered, "Good to know your enemy."
Felix called. "We've got Rupert."
Rupert didn't waste time. "Forty-eight hours. Give or take. Air recon confirms heavy armor moving from Gomel and Belgorod. Rail lines are still intact."

"We're launching the leaflet drop now," Rupert added. "Allied bombers are dropping tens of thousands of flyers over every viable rail route and treeline. 'Destroy the Tracks' campaign. These are dropping now."
"We've started the rings. Jean Pierre and Felix are in place. I've got engineers scanning for kill zones. Civilians are building fallback corridors."
"And the girl?"
"Elena is here."
Rupert said nothing. His hand stayed on the document.
"I'm getting her out tonight. Reese goes with her. They'll be off the grid by dawn."
Rupert checked a document before speaking next. "Our guide is available again. There are other locations to broadcast from next."
Anika folded her arms. "Rupert, we are surrounded."
"I want to win. We both know Elena won't be safe in Kyiv. But if she can go further east, the better option is to extract her by sea."
"Rupert, they'll die."
His hand pressed flat against the document. "That might be enough to take the heat off Kyiv."
"We won't last fifteen days, Rupert."
"This is the only option I have for you."
The map beside Rupert showed enemy indications rolling westward.
"Rupert," she said. "At the Maidan… Elena noticed what was familiar."
He raised an eyebrow; then he leaned back in his chair, slightly.
He looked back at the map. "Get the Americans out of the city."
She held his gaze. "Yes, sir."
And then she cut the line and rotated back to Elena and Reese. "You heard him. You're leaving, now."
Felix's voice crackled through her comm as she turned away. "North wall's secure. We have traps set, and we've removed the bridges."
"Jean Pierre, where are we?" Anika barked into her collar.
"Prisoners are stored, weapons caches have been discovered. We have new turrets at the main intersections."
"Copy." Anika leaned toward Elena and Reese.
There was no debate; they followed Anika out of the smoldering city. Central Command's decision was Odessa. They moved fast dressed as fleeing Russians.

They reached a half-buried house behind sandbags and scorched bikes, where a woman waved them upstairs to dress in torn coats marked with Russian insignia. "Pretend you've already lost everything," the woman said. The words came without rehearsal.

They reached the gate ten minutes later, where the city wall was a patchwork of crates, burned-out cars, and copper-laced razor wire. Two fighters stood watch, and a narrow gate creaked open at their approach. Just beyond the gate stood a man they hadn't planned on seeing again. He had his hands in his coat pockets.
"Ryba," Elena breathed.
He smiled. "Fewer Americans to hide this time, I see?"
Ryba leaned against a rust-bitten signpost, flicking ash from his cigarette, watching Anika.
"You're late," he said.
"You're lucky," Anika replied.
Anika reached into her coat and pulled a roll of bills, crisp and heavy, bound in a black band.
"The other half comes when I see footage of Elena alive, walking, and viral."
Ryba held the money in one hand, testing the weight. "How can I guarantee a performance?" he asked.
Anika closed the last foot of distance. "Don't fuck with me," she said.
Ryba smiled. "So much venom," he said. "I was starting to think you missed me."
Reese shifted his pack. Ryba smiled wider. "Odessa, then."

Reese followed Ryba with Jack's camera in one hand. They walked out through the narrow gate into the gray morning, Ryba a few steps ahead, hands in his coat pockets. The city receded behind them; Elena pulled her collar up against the wind.
Reese watched the back of her neck for a moment. The spider, the square, Jack's camera still in her bag. All of the same thread.
"Who was the pilot?" he said.
Elena kept walking. "Andrew."
"Andrew flew that thing."
"Yes."
Ryba's pace didn't change. His head didn't turn.
"Known him long?" Reese asked.
"Since we were kids."
Reese adjusted his pack. "And now he's in Hollywood."
"Kremlin money."
They walked on. Ryba stayed just far enough ahead that his face never showed.
"I believe you," Reese said.

The edge of the city erased behind them. Soot-marked buildings gave way to open ground, shallow ravines lined with ash trees, and a road curling south beneath a smoke-streaked winter sky. Kyiv receded behind the slope. No parting words. No line drawn. Just the moment when they had been somewhere and weren't anymore.

Reese tracked Ryba, who talked as he walked, though no one had asked him to. The tone was casual, yet his words rambled. He described the soil as good black earth and said the region was once forest-steppe, ideal for wheat and barley, and other crops that grew long before the drones. His voice moved in and out of range, steady but worn.
She kept her eyes down, tracking the damaged path with the kind of focus that had little to do with footing.
Ryba spoke again, more to himself than to them. “They finally built a modern road, and not one car drives here.”

They took the highway together. The cracked pavement stretched south, lined with old scorch marks and dead roadside brush. Ryba took another drag from his cigarette and let the smoke curl across the wind.
“There’s a field depot in Odesa, just off the Black Sea,” he said. “Remember, we’re fleeing the heathens in Kyiv. If anyone speaks to us, I’ll do the talking for us, naturally.”

They reached the first village about an hour after leaving the highway. No sign marked the name; the frame stood empty, poles upright as grave markers. A gravel path veered off the main road, winding past salt-burned brush into a cluster of low houses. Most had once been white, now dulled to gray.

Some traces of life remained. A laundry line stretched between two sheds, cloth frozen mid-motion. A child’s chair sat half-buried beside the step. On the concrete, chalk sunflowers had not yet disappeared. Reese shot the road, then the sky.
Ryba lit a cigarette. “No one ordered the end of places,” he said.

They rejoined the M05 under a sinking sun that gave no warmth, with a drone passing overhead too fast to track. The land opened around them in dry, muted rolls, fields gone pale with salt and tree stumps blackened along the edges.

By the third day, the air carried the faint metallic bite of old ash and standing water. A child's drawing still clung to a kiosk window, bright with crayon against all the grey. Reese ran the perimeter twice on foot before they moved. The route a scout would have covered in one pass.

He passed the controller to Elena, and she had the drone stabilized before the feed finished clearing. The camera swept the farmland, locking onto burned barns and scattered animal heat signatures. They took shelter that night in a hollowed gas station with a sagging roof and no fuel, watching the sky in shifts. A second drone passed before midnight, slower this time, but at a distant altitude.

The highway narrowed, shouldered by dead farmland and the remains of repurposed plows, one still bearing a family photo behind cracked glass. Ryba called the war on the land collective punishment. Elena said, at a grain depot reduced to rubble, that Ukraine once fed half the continent. Neither of them said anything after that.
By the fourth morning, Reese had dry rations warming over a smokeless flame before Elena woke, while above them a scout drone passed lower than any before so far.
Reese asked, "Have you ever noticed his teeth?"
"On the first day. Where have you been?” she said.
"Maybe he went to the dentist," Reese said.
"He's scouting," Elena said. Something in her voice had gone lighter. "And so far, everything he's told us has held."
Reese gave a small nod.
Thirty minutes passed before Reese rose and brushed the dirt from his knees.
"He said he'd be ahead." Spoken to the road, not to Elena.
Elena unfolded the map and studied the lines. "If we hold, we'll hit the ridge route regardless."
Reese gave a short nod. "Then we keep going."

Together they walked on as the terrain rose, the salt fields breaking into scattered patches of pale moss and brittle stalks, thin traces of growth returning where the earth had been gray and dead. The softened ground swallowed their footfalls until Reese finally spoke.
"You ever notice he talks in circles?"
"He's been out here too long," she said.
"Definitely."
Nothing behind them. Flattened scrub. Half-vanished tire ruts marked where someone had tried to force a road through here once and given up.
"Do you trust him?" he asked.
Reese looked back toward the ridge where Ryba had gone. Nothing.
"I don't distrust his greed," Elena said. "His payout depends on us reaching Odessa alive."

The land rose again, a shallow hill with a dead comms tower at the top, its cables frayed and coiled. From the ridge, they could see the basin below, a stretch of lowlands scattered with broken irrigation lines and skeletal grain elevators. What might once have been a village lay in ruin beside a drainage channel, with no smoke, no trails, and only the faint outlines of structures that no longer belonged.
They held a position beneath the ridge. Elena scanned the sky while Reese kept his attention fixed on the route Ryba had taken into the trees.
"He's way past late," Reese said.
Elena didn't look up.
"You think he's watching us?"
Elena rose and started down the hill toward the valley.
Reese exhaled and rubbed his eyes; he hadn't slept more than a handful of hours in days. The salt lingered in his throat, his clothes, his hair.
"You holding up?" he asked.
"I'm fine."
"You don't look fine."
"Then shit."
The wind picked up for the first time in days, carrying a bite, and the clouds pressed lower and thicker until the gray above them no longer looked passive.

They crested the next hill and came upon an outpost that stood by accident at the edge of an old industrial yard, a handful of prefab bunkers half-buried under dirt berms and sagging camouflage netting. Faded Russian markings clung to the doors, and crooked watchtowers leaned against the horizon.
They approached carefully. The ground showed no fresh tracks, only a few scattered shell casings near the entrance. Whatever had happened here had happened long ago.

The bunker's atmosphere choked them with the sharp, acidic tang of rat urine and oxidizing iron. The freezing air clung to the stripped metal bunks. The air still stank, even inside. Elena pried open a crate and finding stacks of laminated translation cards, each one stamped with a flag and lined with phonetic phrases. Reese pulled several free and flipped them over in his hands.
"North Korean," he read. "Chinese. Ukrainian."
Elena held one up: Surrender or die translating Chinese to Russian.
They exchanged a glance.
"Who were they expecting?" Elena asked.

They sat in the bunker’s rear corner, backs against a crate of tangled rebar and rusted bolts. Around them stood cans with dissolved labels, cracked lanterns, and a Soviet toolset still fitted in foam. Reese leaned against a beam while wind scraped the wall. Then the canisters rattled as the floor shivered, the hornet sound multiplying above them. Reese snapped the light off, and Elena crouched at the narrow window. Hundreds of sleek, unmarked drones swept overhead in low waves, banking toward the flatlands beyond the outpost.
"Not Russian models," she said. "That's a custom job."
"So what happened?"

Reese exited by the back door for a clearer view of the sky. His second step landed on something buried beneath the sand. Metal shifted under his boot with a soft mechanical give. He froze, looking at Elena, who was urging him to be quiet. Around him, radio mics clicked dead one by one as they dropped into crouches behind walls and doorframes. Elena started sprinting for the garden wall. Then the first shell landed wide, shaking dust from the stone. The second struck where he had been standing.
"Reese, keep your head in the game," Elena said.

After Reese triggered the sensor, artillery pummeled the outpost for half an hour before the bombardment shifted elsewhere. They remained inside the house beneath cracked plaster while dust drifted steadily from the rafters and fresh impact estimates pulsed silently across their muted whisper pins.

Reese worked through the abandoned crates while Elena kept watch near the windows. Rifle parts filled one container; dead comms gear filled another; factory-sealed translation headsets sat beneath neatly stacked manuals.
He pulled one free with a cover of rows of Chinese characters. The manual showed diagrams of irrigation equipment and soil management. Reese held the booklet toward Elena.
"What the hell?"

Elena crossed the room, took the manual, and turned several pages. More Mandarin. More agricultural diagrams. Reese opened another crate; a deck of cards lay wrapped in yellowing plastic. Chinese characters again. Another crate held boxed translation units, each still sealed.
"Why would they leave this?" Reese asked.
"Maybe they didn't leave," Elena said, still turning pages.
"Then where are they?"
Elena set the farming manual aside and pulled a faded command log from the bottom of the crate, cloth-bound and handwritten in blunt Russian.
"Daily records," Elena said, half to herself. "Routine orders. Food. Fuel." She turned another page, then stopped when the same word word was written everywhere. Соль. Солить.
"Solit," she said, half to herself. "Salt, over and over."

Outside, the wind shifted; by morning, the drones were gone. Reese walked out of the outpost, shoulders hunched ever since the ambush. Elena stood near the berm scanning south where the land dropped away toward the sea. Gray morning light spread gradually across the fields before they moved again.
They passed an orchard gone to seed, then a row of bent flagpoles, then an air defense tower with a melted candle sagging in its frame.
Reese adjusted his pack. "How good a pilot are you?"
"I’m good," she said. "But in the end, I'd keep the drones running. Andrew would fly them." She didn't smile, but something in her voice shifted.

They kept walking together and reached the sea before dusk. The destination came in stages, hills falling away, brush thinning, the air turning cooler and heavier until salt edged the wind. From the crest of a low ridge, the view opened all at once, and Odessa spread below them.

They moved downhill together without hurry as the earth grew sandy underfoot, sea grass twisting from the roadside, the air smelling of rust and cold metal. A shattered tourist marker lay face down in the reeds. Reese nudged the marker over with the toe of his boot. A warning scrawled in red spray across the faded slogan: Здесь больше ничего нет. They needed Ryba for that.

Soon their destination became clear; Odessa spread beneath the slope, wide, low, washed in rust-colored haze. Cranes bent toward the horizonin the middle of their work. Rowhouses lined the bluff, some still standing, others collapsed inward. From here, the city still breathed.

The sky deepened into purple-gray layers. Reese left the trail. "I want a better look." He climbed, slipping on loose shale, using an old drainage pipe for balance until he pulled himself onto a cracked cement pad with rebar poking out.
Elena came up more carefully, crouched by the fence, and read the lowland in sweeps. "You see anything?"

"Not yet. Gimme a sec." Reese unhooked the camera and swept the coastline. The wind cut sharper up here, biting and cold. Reese tracked the coastline through the viewfinder. The freezing wind bit. He stopped the pan. Through the haze, shapes on the water.
"Something's out there," he murmured.
Elena knelt beside him, squinting into the haze. "Where?"
Reese pointed. "On the horizon. Long hulls, vertical towers. They're not moving."
Her eyes narrowed. "A navy."
"Whose?" Reese asked.
"Does it matter?" she said.
Reese steadied the camera, the lens catching the flat line where sea met sky. "We’re very far from home. I’ve never had that feeling."
Elena watched the horizon, where dark hulls held disciplined spacing, each ship separated by enough water to survive another vessel’s missile strike.
"We have to get closer." Reese slipped the battery back into his coat.

They pushed across the slope, nervous energy carrying them forward when they saw him. A figure was cutting in from the southeast tearing through sand and brush along the lower rise. Ryba had no rifle, no coat; his hair was plastered wet against his forehead. One arm raised in a sharp wave.
Elena lifted her hand. "Ryba?"
The gesture came again, harder this time, a vector more than a greeting.
"Is he smiling?" Reese asked.
"No," Elena said, her eyes narrowing. "He's shouting."
The wind dragged his words, twisting them thin, but Reese caught enough: Move. Back. Now.

Ryba turned before reaching them and ran. And then they ran. They plunged through a cut between hills, past a rusted irrigation wheel, then down into a shallow basin. The air pressed heavier, as though the land itself were closing in around them. Ryba stayed just far enough ahead that his face never showed, only his silhouette against the haze, arms pumping, voice breaking apart in the wind.
"Keep up, come on, move!"
Elena stumbled. Reese steadied her with a hand on her shoulder before he knew he'd moved. They hadn't seen Ryba in hours, and now they were running after him.
"How far is he going?" Reese gasped.
"Suck it up, Reese," Elena said. "Come on."
They pushed harder. Anika didn't waste trust. He drew level with Elena, lungs burning.
"Let's go. We've got this."

Rupert Collins sat alone beneath the terminal’s artificial dawn and watched his Guinness settle without drinking. His phone lit beside the glass. The flight log was updated once, confirming three passengers, two military escorts, and one oversized freight crate cleared through the lower terminal. Rupert read the line, locked the screen, and opened the dossier in front of him. No holographics, no retinal feeds, just matte paper thick with age and inference, the front stamped in NATO gray.
RAINES, ANDREW / CLASSIFIED: VOLUNTEER CANDIDATE

He pulled them out of that absurd tower in Malibu under a dozen names, cleared the airspace, rerouted the limo, and smuggled the entourage through on a private cross-PAC diplomatic visa. All of it, for this bar. For this table. And for the boy who might still believe he was a king. Above him, the camera drones shifted to privacy mode as Rupert keyed the override, cutting every feed and trace until the decision belonged only to the room.

This time his glass remained untouched. Beside the glass lay a confidential report, folded back to an inside-page headline announcing Chinese rail-and-logistics investment across newly occupied Allied territory. The article called for reconstruction of the war-torn economies along the eastern front.

Across the concourse, a burst of laughter broke through the ambient noise. The handler, Ridge, was already talking metrics, the other Cam clicking across the terminal in designed heels that squeaked on the synthetic tile. Noor walked beside them as if there was somewhere else to be. And trailing just behind them, slightly out of focus, was the drone pilot Andrew Raines.

The terminal was regulated for an arrival without fanfare. Only the matte jacket, stripped of smart thread, sponsorship tags, or insignia. A garment worn before the world had learned what kind of weapon he was. Rupert registered that detail and filed it with a dozen other quiet indicators. Andrew had not come for a briefing. He had come because Elena was already here. Rupert remained seated, hands flat, back straight, the dossier placed so the paper’s edge caught the light. Nothing in his posture invited warmth.
Cam arrived first at the table. “This is a sterile place for a meeting.”
Rupert didn’t blink. “You’re late.”
Noor’s eyes narrowed. “We weren’t briefed.”
“I know,” Rupert said simply.
Andrew stood just behind them, gaze flicking to the drink. He hadn’t looked at Rupert yet.
“Guinness,” Rupert offered.
Andrew’s eye flickered to the Guinness, then away. That wasn’t the bait that would work on him. Rupert opened Andrew's dossier on the table. The manila folder had been opened many times before.
“You were eleven when they ranked you Tier Three,” Rupert said, his voice low.
Andrew went rigid. Cam’s shoes squeaked against the synthetic tile. Noor crossed behind the table while Ridge held outside the arc, absorbing everything.

“She’s ahead of you again,” Rupert said. “Did you even know she was in the second jeep? That’s when I met her, right after you nearly killed her. Despite all that, she made her way out. That’s what makes her rare. That’s why you’re here, isn’t it?”
Andrew moved slow, even; his eyes were somewhere else. “Is she in Kyiv?” he asked.
“She’s not safe,” Rupert said. “But she’s making big moves. She only needed one stream for her to become the most famous Northrop Grumman Charter School grad of all time.”
Cam laughed, too loud, a brittle sound. “You think Elena Morales is more famous than Andrew?”
Rupert looked at him now. “You can go down to the freight terminal for the package.”
Cam snapped back. “Andrew, we don’t have to listen to any of this.”
Rupert was ready. “She didn’t bring her stylist. Can you at least make yourself useful to Andrew and look after the chair?”

Andrew replied, “Cam, Rupert is right. Go check in on that.” He leaned forward, eyes on the table, where the glass of Guinness remained untouched. Rupert turned the page in the dossier and let him see what was inside, not the text about Andrew, only a single image. A grainy, heat-captured still from Medyka showed Elena crouching beside a supply crate with her rifle raised. His thumb stopped on the page edge, pressing hard enough to crease the paper. The concourse noise continued beyond the table, boarding calls and luggage wheels moving past without reaching him. They all turned to Andrew, who hadn’t moved.
Rupert spoke quietly. “She’s a leader now. She runs with some of the world’s best. Do you recognize the name Anika Kowalska, Andrew?”
“Elena knows Kowalska?” he said.
“Yes, Andrew, Anika has been a target of yours before. Two of your greatest competitors have teamed up.”
Ridge had had enough. “So you’ve been watching him for how long?”
Rupert didn’t look at Ridge, only at Andrew. “Long enough to recognize greatness.”
Ridge folded his arms. “You mean long enough to put Elena in front of him again. We went there. He talked to Elena’s parents.”
Rupert’s mouth didn’t move; he didn’t take the bait. That was direct and accurate, but poorly played.
Noor set her phone down and leaned forward. “You aren’t saying anything specific.”
Rupert let them talk, allowing the illusion of control to settle around the table. Chairs shifted back from the table. All except for Andrew.
“She’s not alone,” Rupert said eventually. “But she is in danger.”
Cam confirmed his data with his whisper first. “You lost control over the number one trending media subject in the world? Her live stream is setting world records, and you misplaced her?”
“Yes,” Rupert said. “I heard her broadcast. The way she spoke. I did my best to hear her words, what she was asking for. She’s asking all of us for help, and she’s worth helping. Don’t you agree?”
They all turned to Andrew, who hadn’t moved. His eyes remained fixed on the image in the dossier, Elena in Medyka, smoke curling behind her, purpose carved into her expression.
Ridge remained standing, one hand tightening around the chair back. “Why the secrecy then?”
Rupert turned to him fully. “Because the house is on fire.”
Noor leaned in. “What’s coming?”
Rupert tapped the folder once. Not hard, not loud. “The allied swarm is coming online.”
Even Cam’s grin faltered with that.
“We built a new system. A thousand drones flying in the same pattern, without using artificial intelligence. No central network. People give the orders, not machines. No one has done that before.
“You built a swarm that doesn’t think?” Noor said.
“We built one that obeys.”
“How does it handle? How bad is the lag?” Andrew asked.
Rupert responded carefully. “We built the system around the best pilot on the planet. The whole design is from the Talon dataset.”
Ridge turned sharply. “You stole Andrew’s data?”
Rupert didn’t flinch; they were smarter than they looked. “Data isn’t private.”
Noor stood, turned away a fan who recognized Andrew, then came back to the table. “This is blackmail.”
“A plea,” Rupert said. “We are desperate for help, and so is Elena.”

Rupert pushed the first folder aside and took out a second. He opened the folder halfway. A single schematic lay inside, part drone, part map, with no digital overlay. Rupert handed the page to Andrew. “From one professional to another,” he said, quiet now. “What do you think?”
Andrew flipped through the folder, scanning the driver profiles, and stopped on one.

Rupert’s right hand tapped the table. Rupert had to grab his hand with his other before a second. “The system requires proximity. You can’t sit in Los Angeles and run this. The system needs the pilot to close. Five to ten kilometers, depending on interference.”

Rupert gave him time to read, then continued, voice steady. “The swarm runs two modes. One is your first-person drone tether. Manual hop. You can switch from lead to recon to striker. Whatever you want. Watch the view or take control.” He tapped the second schematic line. “The second mode is admin, command field control, real-time adaptive presets. You don’t fly them.”
Noor closed in. “If you’re so desperate, why hide this?”

Rupert said, “Pilots degrade after six hours, and eight is the outer edge. We need someone who can absorb a foreign system quickly and operate under combat conditions. And Andrew, we can save Elena if you help us,” Rupert said.
“Who you think I came for?"
Rupert replied, “I’m not asking you to pick a side, I’m not even asking for loyalty.”
Andrew’s stare was steady; he was no longer looking at the system dossier, only at Rupert.
Rupert leaned in closer. “You won’t be the first man to fight both sides, Andrew, or the last,” Rupert said.

Rupert dropped his voice until Andrew had to lean closer to hear. “There is no debt to repay. I don’t care how many allies you’ve killed. I don’t care if you've hunted Anika on burned half of Kyiv. None of that should limit you.”
Andrew’s team backed off and started talking all at once. Each of them was trying to talk louder than everyone else.
“What does one battle mean to Genghis Khan? Or Hannibal? Or Rommel?” he added, even more softly. “If Elena got you here, then that’s good enough for me.”
He extended an open hand. “Friends for now?”
Andrew replied, "Show me the system."

Rupert accepted. They left the airport, and their convoy of black SUVs moved through London, without lights, no sirens, just matte-armored transports gliding low through rain-slicked cobblestone, tires dampened by weight and water. Three vehicles, all black, no markings. The kind of movement London learned not to notice.

Across from Rupert, Andrew sat facing toward the window. Cam sat beside him, legs jittering with unreleased momentum, while Ridge held a bolt-straight posture, eyes fixed on the sealed cabin door. Noor tapped notes into a cracked device, pausing only to check a flickering vitals feed pinned behind Andrew’s shoulder.
As the vehicle turned past a fog-draped crescent of royal redbrick, Andrew’s voice cut through the air. "Which jeep was she in?"

Cam’s boot heel tapped a restless rhythm against the floor. Ridge’s shoulders squared, posture hardening. Noor’s stylus froze in mid-glide, the hand holding it perfectly still while her eyes shifted to Rupert.
Rupert met Andrew’s eyes. “The second.”
Rain began tapping against the roof. Rupert continued. “You put her into an infirmary.” Andrew turned back to the window and gave him nothing more.
The convoy turned south, the roads narrowing into familiar ground between domes and iron gates. The third vehicle peeled away while the lead transport halted at a feature in the road.

They exitedstriking wet pavement, the air metallic with ozone and city grease. Masked security personnel at the black door. The group passed inside, into a hallway narrower and dimmer than expected, its walls re-sheeted in blast-dampening foamcore sprayed to resemble brick. The route led not toward the auditorium but beneath it, through a passage once meant for stagehands and sound engineers.
The corridor ended at a blast door, welded into place with a biometric slot polished smooth from use. Rupert pressed his hand against the reader. The seal released with a hiss, and the door opened.

They entered the former main hall, its previous life forgotten. Velvet had given way to steel, the orchestra pit replaced by recessed storage racks, drone arms suspended from the girders, butchered birds sleek and painted in industrial gray. The dome above braced with motion-dampening lattice, the ceiling restructured for war. A dense, machine-warm smog rolled across the steel grating, the air vibrating with the low, electric thrum of thousands of dormant batteries.

At the center of the hall stood the swarm pod. The pod resembled nothing familiar, longer than a coffin, an armored transport, mounted on adaptive treads with anchor clamps for mobility. The matte hull bore visible scars. The front lay open, a cockpit without a plane, screens flickering in dormant cycles. Within was a harness, neural ports, a finger cradle, and an empty dock for a new pilot’s preferred chair.
Rupert saw Noor looking at the tires. “Designed to be portable.”

Andrew moved forward at a measured pace, Ridge’s gaze drawn upward to the ceiling where ranks of drones roosted. One by one, they approached the machine that would alter the course of the war. The hall was far from quiet: orders cracked in short bursts, gray-jacketed techs and unmarked medics hurried between gurneys and crates, uniforms threading through the space with deliberate precision. Along the walls, embedded nodes pulsed from red to amber and back again, cycling through timing loops and prep thresholds.

Above them, the ceiling shifted, not yet with motion but with the weight of what it held. Drone nests hung from custom rigging, steel arms clamped tight around each frame. Rows upon rows of bats in iron cocoons fit with modular weapon racks, auto-adaptive fins curled in on themselves, their talons at rest.
Andrew stood in the center. Since stepping onto the floo,r he had said nothing, and Rupert had not expected otherwise.
“Clear the room,” Rupert barked. “Anyone not cleared for swarm launch protocol, out.”

The floor shifted around him, but Andrew stayed at the center. He walked toward the pod as if it were the only solid point left in a collapsing map. The rig was industrial stripped metal, with heavy cables dropping into power columns. The seat was plain, built for control, made to lock a body into place.
Andrew placed his hand on the panel, and the display woke in bands of graph light, system checks running without confirmation pings.
An operator with a sealed helmet case greeted them. “Fit check,” she said.
Andrew took the helmet. “I’m ready,” he said.
The swarm nests above vibrated.
Rupert spoke to Andrew and Andrew alone, “Start slow.”

Andrew sank into the rig. Heavy mechanical restraints slammed shut across him, the cold steel pressing brutally hard against the frayed Calibrator Rank 1 patch of his old jacket. He didn't adjust the straps to protect the leather. The spinal plate locked with a metallic click, one arm secured, then the other. A voice filled the loop, calm, clipped, without gender. "Pilot seated. Calibration code green." Andrew’s brushed the edge of the frayed patch just beneath the steel restraint. "Lock the feed," he said.

The screen lit with maps and flight corridors. Andrew selected one node, and the simulation flashed as the drones responded at once. They moved without instinct, yet the formation held exact intervals beside him, stabilizers trembling through the floor panels while Rupert tracked the sweep from the observation line.
Rupert continued. “The drones are powered, and the crew is standing by.”

Andrew dropped his hand into the control cradle. The interface flashed amber; two drones dropped from their mounts. He pressed harder until the warning chime began. A thousand small motors began to wake beneath the platform, the rising vibration climbing through the console and into Andrew’s hand.

Beneath the platform, the swarm woke in sequence, a rising vibration passing through metal and bone until Andrew could no longer distinguish his command from the machine’s response. The first drones lifted from their mounts and drifted toward one another in the hangar, hovering nose to nose for a suspended moment before breaking apart into a new formation.
Rupert moved closer. “Your team is ready,” he said. “Elena needs you now more than ever.”
Andrew never looked up, absorbed in page after page of loadout details, focused.
"Can we get the chair placed next?"
“That we can do,” Rupert replied.

The first secret was out, but one remained. Andrew would reach Kyiv without Elena beside him, and Rupert had already accepted what that might cost him. Some men survived history; others drove forward. The world would see Andrew Raines locked into the war, and every military office on the planet would know Rupert Collins had put both American volunteers in motion.
Rupert saluted beside him. “Andrew Raines, command is yours.”

Behind the glass, logistics techs wrestled Andrew’s old leather chair through the rear entry before bolting the chair to the floor. The swarm answered Andrew almost immediately. A thousand lights shifted across the overhead display, sharp enough to make two gray-jacketed operators exchange a glance. Andrew settled deeper into the worn leather while the system tightened around him, pilot, chair, and swarm. Rupert’s machine pointed east.

Anika walked through the broken streets of Kyiv, prepared to dig. Felix walked beside her; Jean Pierre trailed them both, his shoulders locked beneath the clouded sky. A white earbud sat tight in Anika’s left ear, dulled by street dust. The city had no defense left except them.

A medic passed in the opposite direction at a full sprint. Overhead, the shattered casing of a drone swayed from a hanging streetlight. Wind moved through the street while damaged loudspeakers bled static in short, uneven hisses. Near the Maidan, an improvisation of cables and resistance hands had repurposed the tower’s audio array to disrupt Russian feeds. While Felix took the sector report, Anika pressed a sensor beacon into the broken plaster beneath the tower stairs and wiped the dust flat with her sleeve. Their first move was to preserve the Russian signal blackout, turning the occupation system back on its owners while Kyiv remained cut off from the wider world and prepared the defense.

Anika, Felix, and Jean Pierre stood at the center of their makeshift command. A Polish fuel report and a Ukrainian casualty sheet lay beneath the same stone paperweight. A boy barely old enough to shave raised his hand in a weary salute.
“Updates from Sector D,” he said, his voice holding steady. “Two columns sighted past the Naddniprianske line. All rail lines remain quiet for now.”
Anika took the map from the runner and looked over his notations.
“What about the tunnels?”
“Cleared. We collapsed the access points three hours ago.”
She handed the map to Felix, then turned. “Jean Pierre, send a runner to the comms team at the substation near Khreshchatyk. Tell them to begin dynamiting all bridges.”
Jean Pierre took the order without speaking. Radio static filled Felix’s side of the exchange, and Anika tapped the white earbud once before turning back to the street.

Runners trailed the three of them with paper in both languages, Polish supply slips folded beside Ukrainian sector lists, stacked for Anika’s next signature. They turned down a corridor choked with sandbags and broken stone. The city narrowed in this quarter. Smoke rising between the buildings gave the streets a sepia tint, as though time had spoiled the air itself.
Beyond the awning of a shuttered bakery stood an older civilian wearing a pale jacket that had seen cleaner days and a face worn thin. The exhausted man moved forward without invitation.
Jean Pierre’s hand moved instinctively toward his weapon. Anika intercepted the motion with a glance, steady and final.
“Talk,” she said.
The man hesitated for only a moment, then replied with quiet insistence.
“The signals you’ve blocked reach more than the enemy. You’re silencing the civilians, too. They have no news. We are voiceless beyond these walls.”
Felix crossed his arms. “There’s a war on.”

“I know,” the man said. “And that’s why the first thing you should do is open all of the lines. Give this city back our voice and let every man and woman see what’s coming. Let us decide where we stand.”

Anika looked beyond the man, toward the row of boarded shops along the edge of the square. A shattered phone booth slumped against fractured stone, glass scattered beneath in a widening fan. Beside the booth, resistance paint covered the windows.

She studied the old phone booth a moment longer. The frame still held shape, though the door hung ajar. Nothing remained inside but the rusted coil of a voice line, exposed and useless. Sharp-edged images and old symbols covered whatever surface remained.
“If we open, we lose control,” Felix said.
“What control do you have?” the old man said.
A damaged loudspeaker hissed above the square. The white earbud thinned the noise into a metallic thread before Anika gave the order.
Then Anika said, “Do it.”
Felix blinked. “Anika.”
“Open the comm channels and provide full access. Strip the firewalls and set broadcast priority for civil groups and journalists.”
She turned to the civilian. “I expect unwavering support for the defense from you, personally. I won’t prevent discord. But in exchange, I have an ask of the people.”
Then she said, “Full conscription.”
The old man gave a single nod, and his back straightened. One knee shifted forward; the gesture hovered between recognition and collapse, but he stood tall in the end.
“Even here, the people will riot,” Jean Pierre said.
