Felix kept his voice steady. “The armory’s full enough. What we lack are soldiers.”
Anika anchored the room, her voice flat. “Prioritize the rifles. Every citizen who stands will carry one, regardless of their uniform, their rank, or the name they given at birth.”

Anika remained standing near the far doorway, one hand resting against the rifle sling beneath her coat while she watched the corridor. Reese scanned the old reception hall again. Three entrances, solid walls, and enough floor space for too many people to defend.

A Polish quartermaster came with ammunition totals. A Ukrainian clerk came with names from the housing blocks. Behind them, people arrived singly, each step deliberate, shoulders squared beneath smoke and dust. A girl in work overalls anchored herself before Anika, a faded Civil Defense band on her sleeve. She kept her hands at her sides, offering no salute.
“They told me to report.”
Felix tilted his head slightly. “Name?”
“Lara. I’m rifle-qualified.”
Anika gestured to the corridor. “Go to the staging bay near the courtyard. If they give you an old bolt-action, ask for a scope.”

The girl didn’t thank them but turned and walked, followed by more people, an old man with a chipped police badge, a pair of sisters from the tram depot. A baker brought his own flak vest and asked to stand in his father’s place; a former engineering teacher asked where she could be of use. A boy with a limp who asked, not to fight, but to carry ammunition.
Felix recorded no names and asked for no papers. After the sixth, he leaned back slightly, tapping the end of his shotgun against the floor. “This is taking too long,” he muttered.
Anika didn’t smile. “You’re right.”

Across the atrium, an older child hung a Resistance flag, threadbare, on a wire across the far railing. The child’s sister, a few years older, was getting a rifle from Felix when a new figure with military posture approached. He stopped exactly three paces from Anika and removed his cap.
“I fought in Donetsk,” he said. “How will you stop them now?” He gestured toward the city walls.
Anika didn’t linger. “There isn’t time for fear.”
He gave the barest nod. “Of course.”
Felix looked up. “Then what do you need?”
The man’s eyes went to the flag, then back to Anika.
“Forgiveness,” he said. “For how late I was. I missed the fight when you arrived, but I’m here to help now.”
Anika grabbed him. “You can make up for lost time. Go into the city and find ten more capable people, the same as you.”
. The line around them kept growing; Polish forms, Ukrainian lists, old police badges, ration cards, and rifle requests crossed Anika’s table in the same dirty stack.
The assembly gathered out of curiosity, commitment, and by command.

Anika remained standing as Jean Pierre lurched forward in his poncho. “Anika, we can begin with the rooftops in Shevchenkivskyi. From there, the view runs across three avenues. I will go and mark the positions.”
“You’re manually marking your spots?” Felix asked.
Jean Pierre turned to him. “They all deserve a chance.”
“That’s going to take more time than we have.”

Anika addressed them both. “We’ll collapse in rings and draw them inward. Place nests here, here, and here. When the second circle goes down, move to the next location here. Fallback lines at Saint Sophia and the tram depot.”
Jean Pierre checked the map before answering. “I’ll need at least seventy capable fighters and rifles with scopes. I’ll place them.”
“You’ll have them,” Felix said. “But building an army in an afternoon is slow work.”
“Keep conscripting,” Jean Pierre said. “Replace the ones in line with scopes as I go.”
Jean Pierre left with the next batch of shooters while Felix geared up for his next task. Anika was ahead of him. “Start pulling from the housing zones, we’ll start the process going door to door.”
Felix raised his head.
“This is necessary,” Anika said.
From the far wall came a cough, dry and unhurried, drawn from somewhere deep in an older man’s lungs.

Anika turned her head, the motion restrained yet unmistakable. Her voice carried without strain. “You may go if you choose, but no fighters will be assigned to defend your retreat. You are free to make your choice.”

Felix tapped twice on the map, marking the second ring with a fading red grease pencil. Anika crouched near the side doorway, pulled a tripwire low across the threshold, and returned before the pencil left his hand.
“Anika, we’ll mine the basements here. Stage fallback ammo caches between the stairwells. If they breach past the second line, we collapse the center from four sides.”
Felix rubbed his temple. “How many survived the tunnels again?”

“Short of two thousand,” Anika said. She kept her gaze on the panel where names and housing blocks scrolled in uneven rhythm. “Every ring draws inward, following the same logic as the tunnels. As the enemy advances, we’ll pop up behind them, but only in places without chance of crossfire.”

Felix moved toward the wide hallway where the line was growing. A Polish courier entered from one side with batteries. A Ukrainian runner entered from the other with housing lists. More conscripts arrived behind them in a steady procession.
Felix spoke low enough that only Anika could hear. “We have far more conscripts than volunteers and that’s a risk.”

He lifted a hand toward the atrium where four ranks of new arrivals pressed forward. Layered voices thundered against stone and scaffolding. Equipment crates lay open on the folding tables, weapons drawn and examined again and again by those tasked with distribution, their exhaustion plain in every motion.
“They’re going to start trampling each other just to get assigned.”

Anika moved away from the map. “Everyone, stop.” Her voice carried without volume, and the room received it at once. Movement faltered across the hall as heads turned and hands paused in mid-gesture, canvas straps still held, paper tags suspended in the air. At the far wall, the medic team dropped their kits, their attention drawn entirely to her. Anika walked to the center, where the flag hung. She faced the crowd.

“If you already hold a rifle,” Anika said, “take a second from the front. Carry two full kits. When you return to the line, place the weapon and gear in the hands of the next. Get what you need and get out.” She scanned the room, her voice low but clear. “You’ll answer if I see one person standing equipped two minutes from now.”

With that, the room burst into motion. The hall filled with motion, no shouting and no chaos. Pairs formed around the nearest crates; armed volunteers lifted crates and shoulder bags, rifles passed from hand to hand. Names called out, medics summoned, loose ranks coalescing into platoons.
Felix nudged Anika. “That was good.”
“We must set the pace,” she said. “What we ask of them must be asked of ourselves, Felix.”
He looked back toward the courtyard, where the flood was thinning, now a flow of movement outward, out toward staging zones and the first rings of defense.
“Then to the back of the line we go,” he said gleefully, grabbing a second rifle and kit.
The returning Jean Pierre met them as they left. His finger pointed back to a cluster of apartments near Lukianivska.
“Too many open angles,” he said. “We’re nesting in the facades instead. If they advance from the north, we should drop the towers on them because we have the dynamite.”
Felix sighed. “We’ll have to explain the strategy before we evict the people living there.”
“No,” Anika said. “Find the old man from earlier, with the pale jacket.”
She headed toward the back of the line, speaking to the crowd as she passed.
“While you fight,” she said aloud, “While you fight, these buildings are subject to conscription.”

Her voice held steady while the old man watched her mouth shape every word. The white earbud picked up the crowd noise late, a fraction behind the rifles' movement. As she spoke, the old man with the pale jacket returned. He stood near the conscription line, watching each rifle change hands. Anika moved to him, placing a firm hand on his shoulder.
“Those still in their homes will be asked to leave. We are forging the city into a blade, and I need you to carry this message to the people.”
The man stood firm, eyes on her, and finally replied, “You’re not asking permission.”
“No,” she said.
“And where do you want all of the people to go for safety? The churches?”

Anika removed her hand from his shoulder. “There’s no need for gods in a city that remembers itself. We must be very gentle with them and very honest. Tell them the truth. If we don’t shape the city for defense, together, none of us survives.”
The older man buttoned his shirt and left, carrying Anika’s message. At the door, a Polish logistics runner handed Anika a fuel request already creased from too many hands.
“If not now, when?”
Jean Pierre snapped the lid of a case closed. The rifle list was already growing in his hand. He started to leave again with another set of shooters.
“I’ll place the next shooters now,” he said again. “Be back within the hour.”
“Jean Pierre, place the best shooters last,” Anika called. “Save hardened fighters for the middle.”
He left again without another word.
“At some point, the waiting becomes its own excuse.” Felix had been quiet for a stretch before he spoke again, lower this time, not for Anika. “Ryba might hand her over,” he said. “You know that.”
Jean Pierre didn't look up from the map. “Yes.”
“She trusted him because we did.”
“Felix.”
“Iryna survived this long for this? Elena is with Ryba and—”
“Felix.” Jean Pierre's voice didn't rise, but stopped him. “Anika made the only choice available. Ryba was the road. You don't blame the road.”

Anika chose a corridor with a clear line of sight stretching several blocks, giving her a view of the horizon. “They’ll try to break us fast,” she said. “Sweep in before we’ve settled. We know that’s their doctrine.”
Felix drove the pencil into the plywood and left it there. “Our first ring will hit them hard,” he added. “The signal interference will be total. We’ll be blind.”
“That’s why we use paper, and place nests by hand,” Anika said.
Felix finally stood. “You need sleep.”
“I need another thousand meters of anti-personnel fencing.”
He started toward the conscription line again. “If we survive this,” he said, “what next?”
“Every day is a gamble.”

Anika left the square without an entourage, just a sidearm, and the streets of Kyiv lay before her. She moved through the perimeter, watching her plan unfold, not to micromanage or fix, but to listen. She didn’t expect perfect obedience. That wasn’t the point. She had laid out a score, and now she was hearing the notes strike back.

Where corner cafés once stacked broken chairs, sandbags now sat in layered barricades. Razor wire looped through bike racks; flower beds uprooted and replaced with roadside bombs. She marched the perimeter of Lukianivska, pulverized brick coating her throat with sharp, gritty dust, where Jean Pierre’s nests had sheared away the facade panels. Everyone worked fast and smart; a boy rushed past with two empty crates, too busy to recognize her the way she preferred.

A low breeze danced through the broken lanes, thick with grit and the settled fires long since burned, the kind that leave a city not in ruins but in slow, enduring collapse. Behind her, armed strangers waited for the next command.

Her childhood unfolded west of the Dnipro, near the Polish border, where war lived as television static. Those borders had once been clear, and she remembered watching a ceremonial crossing from the roadside as a child.

When Donetsk began to fracture, Anika was still in school. Friends whispered names of cousins who had vanished into protests, or joined militias, or traveled east and never returned. Some, they said, had been taken. Some crossed the border and were never allowed to return, while others disappeared before they even reached it.

Yet the worst were never the fighters. The worst were the children. The ones gone for months, absent from every list, until their faces emerged in state broadcasts, positioned in rows within resettled education zones, renamed, re-clothed, recited. Some mothers reconnected with their children’s; most didn’t.

Anika passed beneath a balcony where three women tended a battered kettle, steam rising from a chafing dish balanced on salvaged bricks. One of them looked up; their eyes met across the distance. The woman gave a slight nod, neither deference nor fear, only recognition, quiet and unspoken. Anika returned the gesture without slowing.

She turned onto Bohdana Khmelnytskoho Street, where the drone netting had nearly reached completion. Strands of metal-thread mesh stretched between rooftops, forming a canopy of torn geometry. The pattern would fracture aerial scans, or at least delay them long enough to matter.

At the next intersection, she stopped where red paint circled the foundation of a building marked for demolition. Anika fixed a cracked mirror to the corner brick, angled the glass toward the street behind her, and moved on. Above the breach, lost names chalked in careful lines, not as warnings but as elegies. Anika had no name among them and no childhood claim to the city, only the old certainty of what came next.

The first wave would come thin and urgent, hoping for panic. Then the shells, then the drones, then the knives in dark stairwells. And if resistance held, they would shift again, shedding every mask of convention and escalating beyond the rules. Anika had fewer than four thousand fighters. Most could barely hold formation, but that was not the measure that mattered.

The patterns of occupation followed the tempo of doctrine and the blind spots of arrogance. Their orders arrived late, spoken in certainty rather than need. The pattern was familiar: certainty instead of need, commands from people far from the consequences.

The city did not belong to her, but the defense did. As the pieces of her plan struck against one another across the city, the signals returned. Cell towers must have flared to life. The blackout net lifted, allowing scattered drones to switch to mobile phone triangulation, and civilians, some by accident, discovered they could reach the world again.
Civilians cupped dying phones and rationed battery percentages.

Blue light flickered first in the dark alleys, small faces lit from below as phone screens came on all at once. A pair of them darted past Anika at the intersection of Yaroslaviv Val and Reitarska. One of them had a tablet held high, laughing as they live-streamed the view. Another was FaceTiming a cousin in Warsaw, weeping as she walked.
She passed a man leaning against a half-collapsed bakery, weeping in Polish, and an old woman in the shell of a minibus, whispering into a Nokia handset from a different decade.

A girl, maybe ten, stood in the middle of the road, phone out, dancing. The movement was slight, all hips and hands, dodging invisible lines in the air. A tunneler team emerged from a pit thirty meters ahead, dusty men in rebreathers, hauling up power coils to lace around the next ring’s barricades. One of them raised a hand to warn the girl away, but she’d already slipped past, moving with a rhythm that had nothing to do with war. She posted the clip; seconds later, she held her phone up again, eyes shining.

“Looking for me?” she said to the camera. “I am Elena Morales. You can find me at the corner of Malopidvalna and Lysenka, Kyiv, baby. Alive and kicking.” She winked, blew a kiss, and posted. Anika approached, but the girl didn’t notice until she was a meter away. “What are you doing?”
The girl grinned. “Kyiv is trending!”
“Can you tell me more?”

“Everyone’s watching. It’s gone crazy in just the last few minutes. There’s this whole wave; we’re all doing this ‘I am Elena’ thing. Everyone is using fake geotags and scrambled faces. There are already thousands of posts, and the AI’s freaking out.”
“You’re… pretending to be Elena Morales?”
“Yeah! We all are, no one knows which one’s real anymore. She could be any of us!”
The girl pointed at her screen. “Look at this; I just hit ten thousand.”

Her eyes stayed open until the blue light blurred. She crouched and took the nearest strip of dirt from the ruined streets, a mix of rock, dust, and decay, and dragged the earth across her cheek in a single motion. One tear cut through the dirt, and she packed more filth across her face.
She sent Elena away, under the cover of being a refugee. Logan, unsteady and frightened, had asked Anika if he would be enough. She had told him he would be reshaping his weakness into a weapon.
The child in front of her asked Anika. “Are you okay?” the girl asked.
“When your done dancing,” Anika said, “get in line for conscription.”

Elena followed Reese through broken container yards and half-submerged rail lines, past rotted tankers and skeletal docks. The sea was well behind them now, and what unmarked navy they saw on the distant horizon behind them, too.

Ryba pushed ahead. The street narrowed as they pursued him. Elena jogged a few paces behind Reese slipping slightly on the broken asphalt. He stayed fast enough to remain out of reach. Without Ryba, there was no line to follow, no second perimeter, no hand on the map. Reese moved without question, his stride tight, deliberate.

She had never been to Odessa before. The pattern ran through the direction and the way light shifted between buildings. Everything seemed designed with purpose, the roads wide and tree-lined, the structures low and carefully spaced. She ran past old archways, curved balconies, and mismatched columns, each century reduced to cover, exposure, and route.

A city built in layers, stone and intention laid over each other. Even while running, Elena tracked each block as they changed. The beauty remained intact, but no one watched from the windows or called from the doors. Ryba cut toward the water, forcing the squad through empty streets while the unnamed navy held its spacing on the horizon. They had crossed half the city.
“Come on. We need to catch up.” Reese picked up the pace, but not enough to leave Elena behind. He was a good runner. The wind pulled his coat behind him.

They crossed into a formal garden ringed by marble benches and a low wrought-iron fence. The path curved through trimmed hedges and white gravel lanes. Everything was spotless. The flowerbeds had been cleared, the lamplights worked, and someone had swept the stones as Elena slowed. The buildings stood polished and whole, their facades repainted in colors untouched by ash or soot. There were no signs of shelling. These were buildings meant to be lived in, not reminders of Kyiv’s interior ministries during the first wave. But who would move into the center of a war zone? The enemy lacked manpower.

She kept moving, drawn deeper into the park. They passed beyond the clean buildings and into open space, where the hedges broke apart, and the trees rose tall enough to leave no cover. Only then did the exposure become clear. Soldiers came from every entrance, rifles raised already closing the distance.

The unmarked soldiers braced their weapons; their armor carried no designation; but the serials meant nothing to her. Whatever allegiance they served, they didn’t fly a flag. Reese slowed beside her, his oxygen clouding in the wind.

Then the trap closed; a dozen rifles leveled in unison, surrounding them in a complete circle. The motion was smooth, each barrel lifting as the circle tightened. A man stood out from the formation, separating cleanly from the others. He did not wear a mask and did not carry a weapon. His jacket bore no insignia, but his posture carried the authority of command.
“You will go to the Russian Federation in Moscow for trial,” he said, his English stripped of accent or affect. “This corridor is under our protection from state-sponsored terrorists.”

The commander met Elena’s eyes, his gaze giving nothing back. Around him, the ambush stood fully formed now, there were two dozen soldiers, maybe more, spaced with intention, now fully encircling them in a flawless sweep. Elena turned the safety off.
“Our orders are to assist in eliminating your insurgency.”
Reese’s voice came from beside her, pitched low and steady. “We’re following a guide. If this is the wrong place, then that’s all it is. We’re not fighters. Trying to get through.”
The translator’s gaze held firm. His voice dropped.
“Why?” he asked. “Do you talk?”
He turned to Elena. “You will be recording another message. Your broadcast reach is substantial, and you left a few things unsaid.”
Reese moved between them. “No, that isn’t happening.”
Reese crossed the distance with the easy confidence that made his Talons look good, and there was no time to thank him.
The translator turned to another unmarked soldier, confirming once before continuing. The soldier raised his rifle.
Reese weaved between the soldier and Elena, calm and open-handed, a comically large smile on his face.
“We’re not fighters,” he said. “We’re a traveling group of singers, that’s all.”

“Old songs,” Reese went on. “Holiday carols. We move between the two lines; Russian priests and Western priests are our regular audience. But you all are in luck; we are ready to sing for you now.” He took one step forward, his arms still out. And then, clear and steady, he began to sing — King of kings, and Lord of lords, and he shall reign — his voice carrying the harbor wall the way it must have carried the stadium once, before the arrests, before any of this.

The shot came before the word ended, cutting his song short. Reese’s body snapped backward and struck the concrete, his coat twisting beneath him as the sound cracked against the harbor wall. Elena’s hands opened at her sides. The harbor smell hit her all at once, brine, rust, and something chemical and cold, as if the gunshot had cracked her senses open along with everything else. That was the thing she could not stop seeing. Not the blood, but his coat. Jack’s camera followed him down, landing inches from his outstretched hand, the lens fracturing against the ground, a second, unblinking eye.

Elena remained calm. The soldiers approached her; their coats carried the salt-stained scent of seaweed and machine oil, and when they seized her by the shoulders, she didn’t fight back. She was guided, not dragged, positioned rather than thrown, until she was seated in a cold metal chair bolted to the flat steel of a rusted railcar.

Behind her, the ocean spoke in hollow bellows, pressed low against the stone understructure of the dock. Wires clanked above as skeletal arms of the cranes, threading broken cables into percussion. The soundscape narrowed. The rest of her had already receded into a place beyond reaction. The welded steel railcar radiated a deep cold that leached the heat. Elena had not spoken since the dock, not since the chase through the container yard. A pulse-beacon blinked red near the hatch.

Ryba sat across from her in the same posture he kept through every retreat, upright while the seat latch clicked beneath the train's uneven pull. There were no cuffs on his wrists, no guards positioned at his side, but nothing about his presence suggested freedom. His coat hung open. His eyes were bloodshot, not from injury, but from something quieter and more enduring.

Elena did not meet his gaze; her attention moved across the walls of the railcar, painted in a Soviet green that once signified belonging. But the markings were not Russian; each stenciled instruction followed Chinese industrial conventions, crate labels in Mandarin, maintenance diagrams translated only partially. The bilingual posters gave orders in both Russian and Chinese, but the Russian always followed second, smaller, as if added as an afterthought.

The train slowed, not toward a full stop, but into a measured crawl that set the frame vibrating along its welded seams. Beneath the bench, the floor groaned under shifting hydraulics, the momentum bleeding out through metal instead of brakes. Through the narrow viewing slit, the outside world fractured briefly into view. Armored personnel carriers rolled parallel to the train, dark green and low-slung, their hulls of serial numbers or signals. The design was unmistakably Chinese-built, adapted for long-haul suppression. A full column stretched into the treeline, punctuated by marching infantry moving in loose phalanx. Their uniforms pressed, their faces calm, and no unit carried a flag. At the crossing ahead, the directional signage bilingual, but Mandarin took precedence, larger by half, stenciled first. There was no attempt to conceal the hierarchy.
Chinese infantry moved through in small groups, painting over Russian boundary markers and swapping Cyrillic checkpoint signs for Mandarin ones. No one unslung a rifle.

The magnetic seals disengaged with a breathless hiss, and the hatch swung inward as a woman in a black coat, no insignia, and hair bound tight entered. She moved without announcing herself. Her English was perfect, the tone flat, the cadence refined for broadcast. There was no warmth in the delivery, but no threat either. The voice was even, neither greeting nor accusation, and filled the railcar without needing to rise.
“I’m here to prepare you for what follows,” the voice said.
The woman entered the central aisle with her coat hanging still and her hands set evenly at her sides. She didn’t carry a clipboard or any weapon and showed no visible mark of her station.
“You go to Moscow,” she said, as though this had already happened. “Not as a prisoner, of course. You will be a demonstration trial in political form.”
Elena’s voice came rough, pulled from someplace too long left untouched. “And if I don’t?”
The woman allowed a brief curve of the mouth that resembled a smile, though held no mirth.
“You already spoke,” she said. “In Kyiv, you did this, and you’ll do this again in Moscow.”
Elena studied her more closely now. “You’re not Russian.”
“No.”
“But you’re in command.”
“Not formally.”
Elena let the words hang, then added, “I know what that takes. Women don’t usually reach this far.”
A sparkle flicked behind the woman's eyes. “That’s a Western trick, Elena,” she said. “You are mistaking presence for power. In China, power doesn’t perform; in China, power endures.”
Elena leaned back, recalibrating her defensive stance. “What do you want from me?”

The woman didn’t hesitate to respond. Her cadence quickened with Elena’s questions and the excuse for a conversation in English. “There’s nothing we need from you,” she said. ”You are a mistake we are fixing.”
Her hand lifted, not a gesture of threat, but of inclusion, and she indicated the walls of the railcar, the vents still humming, the foreign lettering etched across the panels.
“Russia asked us for help,” she continued. “We don’t mind Russia looking strong; of course, we’ll help them bring criminals from abroad into the frame of justice.” She stood up.
“You swam in the wrong water. To survive across generations, a good society learns to snuff out sparks quickly and without spectacle.”
Elena tried to keep her pulse steady, thinking of a time not long ago when this had been easy.
She thought of her father, who used to set a plate of warm food on the worktable without asking.
With that, the woman turned to leave, apparently aware that Elena had moved on. “I enjoyed our conversation.”

As she left, the door hissed shut behind the woman, metal clasping metal in a sound engineered to separate before from after. The noise did not linger, but the effect did. The woman’s coat was immaculate, not a single crease, not a thread out of place. Neither Russian guard spoke; one adjusted his collar as the train’s steel complained beneath them. The other looked first to Ryba, then to Elena, then down toward the steel floor. Elena captured, and Reese was dead. The train’s rhythm wasn’t steady, steel dragging against steel in an uneven, grinding complaint.

And in the end, she followed Ryba. She had followed the one Anika drove to the ground in the tunnels beneath Kyiv. She had prided herself on reading systems and vulnerabilities, yet she had misread the smuggler. The one time she allowed hope to override her instincts, it had delivered Reese to a bullet and herself to a cage. The cold had not reached her ribs until now. She was shaking, but only from the wind.

That moment on the train returned with perfect clarity. Two guards sat with rifles resting on their knees. Ryba stood beside them while the carriage light ticked above his bloodshot eyes. She didn’t look at him, not even once. The markings on the train’s walls told a different story. Cyrillic signs half-erased beneath sharper Mandarin. Bolt lines were misaligned where old panels forced to meet new ones. Russian fixtures welded into bulkheads built for a different machine.

Fields passed in long frozen bands; the soil had split from the cold. Fences sagged under wire stretched too many times. Grain silos sat empty. Fuel stations were shuttered, logos painted over.  Transmission towers leaned in the wind, cables swinging out of rhythm.

Near the old depot towns, trucks moved in columns with no insignia. Military, but unofficial. Roads were cracked open, but the convoys kept coming. A mosaic of a Soviet soldier stood above a collapsed service station. Someone had spray-painted Mandarin across the base.

Elena tapped her finger on the rail at the tempo of the spider’s eight legs approaching on the concrete of the square. The train rumbled forward. The terrain had changed, and there was less space between settlements now, more roads under repair, more fences that weren’t collapsing. Industrial zones returned in layers: transfer stations, water towers, construction yards with prefab shells rising to a new future under new management. Elena kept her focus on the window. Fewer blank billboards now. The closer they came to Moscow, the more obvious the Mandarin signage became.

A door opened at the end of the car, and a Chinese officer entered. They didn’t issue any orders, and there was no reaction from the guards. One Russian rose, caught itself, went still. The Chinese officer looked at him longer than he looked at Elena. The railcar's heater clicked and failed again, leaving only cold air and metal drag beneath the floor. Through all of what she saw, she had not looked at Ryba, who had remained in her periphery throughout.
That’s when Ryba spoke. “I saw what they had, Elena. Navy ships off the coast and drones from the sea flying patterns I’ve never seen. When that kind of firepower comes ashore, you don’t say no.”
“They offered yuan, too. Not rubles, not gold, not the dollar. I didn’t think twice.” His voice dropped further. “I assumed they’d kill you faster.”

The rail corridor tightened, with fewer open fields now. The train ran between leveled factories and residential communities organized by central planning. Every kilometer brought more personnel, more fencing, more checkpoints without signage. Men in uniforms with blank patches. She tracked the shift in her body before allowing the thought to form, her muscles already taut from the restraints.
When he spoke again, he asked, "Are you worried they’ve classified you as a state-sponsored terrorist?”

His tone promised he would keep going until she gave him something. Ryba stood up, showcasing his freedom, then returned to his seat with nowhere else to go. That’s when he leaned forward and tried talking to her again. “I think you should be happy about the part you played.”
She squared toward him, slowly, and looked not at his frame or his face, but at his feet for the first time. He was wearing Reese’s Talons.
“You sold me cheap,” she said.

Plane engines spooled in unison, the high-frequency whine buried beneath the slow, sovereign thunder of coordinated ascent. Each aircraft rose in turn, black-marked wings folding east above the Thames, bearing signalers, and engineers all threaded into a vast, quiet directive. One housed the drone command pod near its center. Andrew remained within, held rather than seated, the gel-woven harness pressing at every point.

One by one, transport planes lifted heavy as a vault door unhinged, their engines screaming against the English air. The convoy rose slowly into the slate sky, holding a legacy formation unchanged since the 1940s. Nothing faster carried that much weight, and every aircraft became a fat, slow target on a one-way flight to Kyiv.

Rupert was on the plane where Andrew flew, and so was Andrew’s entourage from Los Angeles. Others flew with them, two engineers whispered in coordination behind one bulkhead panel, their gestures absorbed by the pulsing light. Their presence reached him in fragments, through a boot heel dragging damply against the deck, a ration seal hissing open, and the fading weight of a hand withdrawn from his shoulder.

By the seventh minute, they had cleared London. The city dropped into the clouds and then into nothing. No turbulence met them; their takeoff was smooth. The fleet held position progressing eastward in measured flight coordination and present flying formations. Kyiv remained five hours distant, depending on corridor clearance. The others would spend that span in onboard briefings, while Andrew mapped the new system with every available minute.
The plan explained itself as twenty-two transports climbed into formation, each radar return a slow ellipse on legacy frequencies, every aircraft fat with a thousand drones and exposed at altitude.

The old leather chair was aboard before dawn and bolted into the pod. The deck crew had locked the seat frame through four fresh brackets, but the carrier’s vibration still worked through every seam and bruise. Andrew had a handful of hours to learn the system. The pod answered Andrew’s commands, and each instruction routed through his chair, pushing farther toward the edge of what the system would allow. The forward view held nothing but gray. Below, the sea in brief white fractures before mist reclaimed each glimpse.

Within the pod, the forward screen widened to the horizon. The convoy’s advance would draw a response. Andrew had counted on that because he needed the practice before Kyiv. Scout drones would arrive first, meant to observe and bait. Then, fighter teams, jets, and other manned aircraft would follow once satellite locks confirmed the fleet’s route. And Andrew had to be ready for ground-launched cruise systems that could start to fire at any time along their direct route to Kyiv. Andrew’s role sharpened in light of the sequence of events he could foresee. Inside his pod, Andrew held the drone command suite, primed to scatter thirty-two forward units into a defensive web to protect the convoy. Andrew’s net would hold long enough to carry the carriers into safety, with Kyiv ahead and Elena somewhere beyond the line.

Andrew began deployment, releasing the first thirty-two drones into the air. Each drone peeled from the pod, took a bearing, and climbed. The wind bypassed his nerves; his system processed atmospheric drag coefficients. His neural link locked seamlessly into the swarm. He began shaping alternate strategies. The signal lag built a half-second gap, but the execution remained precise.

Each of the thirty-two answered. The sync-lag had dropped to four milliseconds. The pod demanded fluency. Contact with the enemy became more certain with every passing minute. With thirty-two drones airborne, the system stopped cycling commands and pressed their positions into his nerves.

The dormant drones answered slower than they should have after sitting cold for so long. Andrew deployed the hunters anyway. A cold line moved through his vision as the old leather chair ground harder against the bruised place along his back. The new carrier bolts answered each correction with a faint metal groan beneath the seat frame. Andrew shoved the pain flat with the buffer and kept both hands on the controls while the amber rest indicator flashed for the eleventh straight minute.
"Vitals are spiking," Noor's voice clipped through the peripheral comms. Andrew flexed against the rig. "Hold the feed."

His heart slammed against his ribs, and his pupils blew wide as the drone’s movements crowded out his own. Lorna’s blend put to use; its rich, volatile, precise formulation injected. Around Andrew, he could hear his staff, but as distant echoes.

The sea dropped away behind them. Ahead, the land turned rigid and exact. Canals ran parallel in measured cuts, angling across flat farmland divided by hedgerows and dirt access roads. Narrow rivers shimmered beneath the wings, flanked by wind turbines and winter-stilled orchards. Farmhouses in clusters, brick-walled and steep-roofed, their yards squared by stone.
Around him in the aircraft, static and noise behind the harness.

The forward drone pressed beyond the corridor boundary and registered a new structural signature. No friendly tag surfaced. These were enemy scouts, flown ahead of their own interceptors. Andrew fixed on them and accelerated.

Dark forest belts broke open below into wide industrial corridors. Rail lines ran straight and exposed, tracing east from refineries and processing stations, their yards stacked with still freight. Between them, river arteries shimmered, the Rhine, the Elbe, half-frozen and channeled by levees, lined with ports that had gone quiet. Civilian zones clustered close to the rivers, rooftops visible in groups, tidy and residential, not yet evacuated. The exposure lay bare.

New presets bloomed, urging patterned dispersal and tactical retreat. Every default favored caution when outnumbered. Wide defensive arcs spread across the display, designed to minimize loss and study movement before striking. Andrew closed them all. He leaned forward into the old leather chair and pulled the spider under manual control. The seat tightened hard against his back as the feedback kicked through him. Andrew swallowed the strain.

One enemy drone passed close and broke upward. Andrew followed, no different than racing a poor pilot on a difficult setting set to beginner. This enemy scout drone didn’t have the advantage or the tools to adapt to escape from Andrew. More drones in sequence; this first meant to bait Andrew, as the others enveloped him.

Every limb moved in sync with the dormant rear-defense drones. Andrew had not routed the connection. His glove seams burned at the fingertips, and blood gathered at the corners of his eyes. The chair bucked through three hard pulses, each one driving deeper into the old point along his back. His hands stayed on the controls. The fleet behind him remained untouched, as if the network were sending kill commands into open air. Andrew took the feedback, and the attack held its line.

Each target registered on contact, then vanished from the lattice without bloom or flash. The feed marked impact, removed the body, and corrected the formation around the absence. New signatures tore across the upper quadrant. The missiles came from long range, ground-launched in staggered saturation arcs, each cruise-class weapon carrying enough mass to breach the carrier line. The formation was exposed.

The swarm advanced under admin control. Preset patterns bloomed into intercept radii, elegant in simulation, collapsing under live speed. Standard paths recalculated. He used that, letting the admin net run vector estimation to recalculate its targets under sharper constraints.

He pushed targets out, one per drone, committing each vector before assigning the next. In the feed, flat country stretched toward every horizon while the Vistula cut through the middle. Black trails marked missile impacts, some still burning, others trailing smoke, one half-buried in a field with the nose still aimed at the sky. Red and blue emergency lights pulsed along the horizon where ambulances and fire crews had arrived too late for most survivors.

Heat ran through the chair before Andrew forced the nausea down and reset the forward drone. The enemy MiGs closed. He opened the pack wider, dropped the outer ring low, and drove the forward tier ahead before the pilots could establish range. The old seat fought him for the first thirty seconds, then settled under his hands. Amber warnings flickered, thinned, and died. Andrew stopped testing the lag and began dismantling the formation.

The drone's blurred past safe operating limits on Andrew's screen. The heat sinks flashed red. He leaned deeper into the harness. He baited the pilots, flared signals, then closed with sharp counters. These opponents were not machines; they carried faces, and some pilots looked straight through the final lens. One by one, he reached them, not striking from afar; each of his drones closed to within visual range. Through the final lens, he saw his opponent’s eyes widen before the tag disappeared. The pilots scattered.

The foothills receded behind them, and the land opened wide again. Below, the soil had turned black. Long bands of char ran through the fields, some still smoldering, others scored by artillery paths. Where roads had once provided geometry, instead cables now glinted, half-buried fiberoptic threads lit faintly under starlight. The convoy pressed east over Poland, steady at altitude, while Andrew dropped his pack low.

Now thirty-one drones fanned beneath the cloud shelf, sweeping over open fields, over clustered towns, over fractured industry. Up ahead, Andrew saw the frontline miles before arriving, the black fields slashed unnaturally across the land. On one side of the miles-long divide was green earth, on the other, a mix of black and white. Andrew flew his pack lower, closer to the ground, to investigate at scale what he’d never seen a full picture of.

Their individual sensors cast grids across the blackened earth, turning fields into numbered cells. Next, he set their routines to search for faces. Time ran short before their arrival, and Andrew needed to practice what the skies would not give him. Every contour of every face mattered now. The system needed to learn what to recognize and what never to strike. Andrew was responsible for teaching that lesson.

Admin handled altitude separation, collision avoidance, and pacing. Manual control shifted the forward scouts from road to forest, from river bend to train yard, tracing surfaces too jagged for presets. The balance of modes required concentration across two theaters, two visions running together, one controlled and one systemic, one airborne and one on the ground. Neither theater dropped a signal. His body ran the search, and his hands held the inputs.

Among the thousands of faces lifted from haze, one template returned, failed, and left pain behind the eyes. The drones learned what Andrew needed them to see. Elena emerged from the grain of every pattern, then dissolved before the system could hold her. Sometimes as fragments, sometimes whole. A jawline caught in the slant of another woman’s face. A gaze glimpsed through smoke as a soldier on the frontline fired a rifle. Now, nearly every time, but the real Elena stayed beyond every frame. Andrew used the system to refine those signals. He would need that calibration to be perfect when the fleet reached Kyiv.

His hands tightened on the console. The face-match threshold dropped as then dropped again as the sweep widened. The drones would learn her as a singular north star, though every false return pulled the formation off balance. When the ground below turned to fire, she would remain untouched.

Forests broke apart into lakes and low towns, reservoirs gleaming between treelines, summer cottages at the water's edge, untouched. For a few seconds, he held the drones in a loose orbit and did nothing. No targeting, no search pattern, no threat assessment. Just the lakes below and the pale winter light, and then the Zhytomyr Oblast road signs locked into the feed, and Kyiv lay next, and the land beneath him turned black.

The forward field became a compound of tasks with small battles now being fought continuously as they approached the former border marker, now a scorched post in an open field. Drones tangled with enemy scouts while missiles traced long curves toward the carriers, and jets burned the upper lanes. Beneath every fight, Andrew’s private program searched faces among ruins, teaching the swarm to preserve one sacred silhouette while killing around her.

Andrew rode both at once while the kill feed poured down the edge of the display and red enemy markers vanished across the map. Beside him, the transport crew worked through overlapping calls and flashing panels while the convoy closed the remaining distance. The old leather chair shifted beneath Andrew as he pulled both operations through the same set of controls. One of the new carrier bolts gave a sharp squeak beneath the seat frame. The sound stayed with him longer than the warnings.

Andrew’s scouts reached the boundary of Kyiv before the main formation closed the gap. Beneath them stretched a city of scars, surrounded by blackened fields where farms had vanished, towns erased to flat brown rubble, bridges left broken across dried rivers. Kyiv itself rose from that ruin. Andrew pressed his drones closer, calculating their chassis as acceptable collateral for visual data.

The closer view revealed the design. Streets cleared in jagged channels, buildings collapsed not by accident but by calculation. Barricades traced a geometry older than wars fought on this continent for centuries. The medieval star fort emerged from its geometry; each approach punished by firing lanes carved through the standing city’s deliberate destruction. Andrew traced the kill zones on his display. They formed a perfect geometric star, every overlap intentional, every gap a trap. Elena built this defense. He traced the defensive perimeter she built.

His scouts pushed into Kyiv’s outer ring, and the signal return went flat. Thousands of enemy drones smothered the upper air, dense enough to blot the sun, innumerable but unrefined. One drone Andrew sent forward survived long enough to send back one last lens-frame before being destroyed. The signal loss sheared through his legs, severing the neural link. His opening formation was now cleaved to fewer than half. Each collapse in the network severed through him, a rupture behind the eyes. His allied unit counter ticked from nine-fifty toward the low hundreds.

That’s when the enemy swarm above Kyiv turned toward Andrew. He opened every rack from the transport planes in one surge. Kyiv filled the forward glass of the transports as the convoy released its payload, and nine hundred and fifty allied drones spilled into the air. The old chair took the full weight at once. The carrier bolts squealed under the seat frame, and copper rose on his tongue. The display sharpened.

His command rate doubled. Yet one rule overrode all the others. Every drone checked in before firing, each confirming before the protocol allowed release. No strike commenced without confirmation; the vow mattered more than the rush, and every feed returned to Elena’s face before Andrew allowed the next strike.

Below, the city revealed the pattern he glimpsed from above. The star-fort geometry held under fire. Streets carved to channels, towers collapsed to clear lines of sight. And in the center, beyond barricades and black smoke, the Maidan opened. The square’s geometry matched the map he’d stored from the spider-drone contract, but now he was coming from above.

All levels from earth to sky fractured into layers of conflict. Cruise missiles arced in from beyond the horizon, supersonic jets slashed, and the enemy’s drone wall pressed downward in black sheets. Andrew carried all of them at once. Counter-missile packs drew their nets across the upper tier, fighters baited into spirals that ended in collapse, and the swarm adjusted vector within 0.3 seconds of his input, no lag, no confirmation delay. The air around Kyiv began to clear.

At the same time, Andrew was losing his fair share of drones. The losses rose with the size of the enemy. Pain didn’t care that Andrew was winning. Blood rose and stained his teeth. His neural processor threatened to fracture, not from weakness but from the strain of running too many channels at once. Yet every time doubt flickered, another enemy contact dissolved. Another missile burned before reaching the convoy, now cycling through Kyiv. An enemy fighter turned and broke from the field.

Frame vibrations reached him through the spinal mount. Voices clipped short behind the partition. A console key struck harder than usual. The carriers were safely over Kyiv. They formed a wide circle above the ruined fortress-city, unbroken and intact. The sky was retaken. His display showed nothing but his own formation.
The mesh ran the face-match algorithm again.

That made things worse for the enemy on the ground. The battle narrowed for Andrew. Enemy signals thinned to single points, a finite number of enemies left to extinguish. Smoke drifted where the swarm had cut through, leaving trails that marked victory in every quadrant. Across the lands, thousands of freshly destroyed enemy and allied drones speckled the ground. A mirror of the stars in the sky. Andrew remained in the harness as the mounting cost moved through him, copper in his mouth and blindness gathering at the edges of his vision. He started the recall sequence.

Kyiv stretched beneath him in the shape of deliberate ruin. The same hand that carved the kill zones had also collapsed the buildings above them. He stopped issuing commands for three seconds. Low passes confirmed what the first view suggested. Sniper nests marked the ridges of broken walls. Barricades funneled armor into kill zones. Concentric circles of bodies spread outward, the pattern of waves extinguished with merciless precision. The city had not resisted: it had invited the enemy to advance, then consumed them. Andrew descended through the smoke with his packs fanned wide, willing every lens to study the arrangement, willing every sensor to find Elena.

Faces surfaced among the rubble, women carrying rifles, fighters scrambling from stairwells. The mesh algorithms scanned for Elena’s biometric signature in each frame. His drones strafed city blocks, destroying armored columns and striking fortified enemy positions that pressed against resistance lines. Thousands of enemy soldiers died in minutes without air support, trapped beneath the closing fire. Yet whenever one figure moved with Elena’s outline, he checked fire. He allowed protection instead of annihilation for the figures whose outlines refused to resolve. The vow remained unbroken, even within slaughter, as Andrew inspected every face of the enemy he destroyed.
The swarm extended farther as packs ranged past the outskirts of Kyiv.

The city lay broken beneath his reach, but Andrew did not release command of the swarm. Victory meant nothing if the wrong face vanished in the smoke. Every frame of capture carried risk. He forced the drones to linger a moment more. He drove the search loop. One target remained. Resistance fighters raised rifles from behind barricades, standing and cheering Andrew as he flew past them. Children ran across courtyards where fire still burned. Women darted between walls that leaned as if ready to fall. Andrew moved through the faces he had logged, checking each one again, desperate not to miss her.

The swarm continued to work around him, burning through armor columns and striking the last fortified emplacements, each quadrant clearing in sequence, but his mind folded into a smaller corridor. When the passes concluded, no confirmation came.
The empty channel held its spacing inside the lattice, answering with nothing each time the swarm called for her. Andrew forced the swarm higher, circling, the search loop running unbroken.

Kyiv registered clear and no hostile contacts remained in range. The swarm circled above in patient tiers, stabilizing until their recall. He guided them back in sequence, docking pack after pack until order returned.

The carriers descended toward prepared strips outside the city. Landing gear ground one by one, heavy against the scarred soil. Inside the pod, signals dimmed, harness locks withdrawing in sequence, the chamber unsealing in a sequence timed to the withdrawal of his spinal ports. Hands clipped across Andrew’s harness, his handlers working to detach the spinal ports, to unplug the channels that had bound him to the system. Andrew didn’t say goodbye; instead, he issued one final command to the system that he would return to them again.

He was still trying to hold a swarm that wasn’t there, each absent unit keeping its place in his hands. Tap, drag, tap. Then his hands grabbed the chair instead. They dug into the black leather hard enough to split the surface along the right armrest, cutting through the red wing decal. His chair straps catching him before he hit the floor.

The carriage jolted forward with an exhale through the roof vents at the final stop. Elena did not resist as the guards pulled her to her feet, her wrists still cuffed in front of her. Then one of the men placed a black hood over her head. The hood’s cotton edge brushed briefly against her hair and nose before being pulled tight. She smelled old sweat, canvas, and a faintly caustic scent beneath the cloth. From her first step, she began her map. For most others, this was standard protocol, transporting a prisoner of war from the train to the trial.

The transfer began with a walk down a short corridor and a narrow steel ramp. As Elena walked, a dull, old, mechanical siren sounded in the distance, repeating once before ceasing. When the coolness of outside air ended, a door ground open on heavy hinges, followed by a sound she had come to recognize. There was a static pause of authorization, then a cold voice speaking low and local, too close to a microphone. Elena descended a confusing staircase, a floor plan that led to another hallway, then to an elevator.

Elena counted every step, memorizing each turn. After another long hallway, Elena turned right after fourteen steps. On the turn, her shoulder brushed a cement wall, the guards lazily guiding her as the new corridor narrowed. Behind the hood, Elena’s internal diagnostic mapped every turn and pause. Echoes, drips, and the change in air where old paint met stone marked the route. She counted to herself mouthing the numbers. If there was a chance to escape, she would remember where to go.

For hours she had a hand at the back of her neck, steady and gloved, and pushed her forward down the smooth, washed concrete hallway. The guards' pause signaled a room. They pushed Elena along, expecting a wall to lean on, but the floor only offered the next steps down a corridor that refused to end. Fifteen steps forward. Elena counted them by heel contact, stepping loudly on purpose so the sound of her heel would help her keep track. She tried to manage a consistent gait between her strides.
The guards tracked in tandem beside her, one slightly ahead, one trailing, the same spacing as every corridor before.

The new sound had the ring of a coin dropped into a metal bowl; it echoed once inside a recessed chamber, then died. Up until here, she could retrace her steps perfectly back to the train. Three more steps. The guard on her left moved. A latch clicked. Hinges opened with a soft pull, no screech. Her arm was guided inward by her guards. The air inside the cell changed abruptly, becoming dry, filtered, and still. They turned her, uncuffed, then nudged her back until her shoulder hit the rear wall.

The guards did not walk away. They remained outside, their breathing audible. One shifted weight, and his leather creaked. Then the coin clinked again, but more quickly. Then another door opened. She heard the coin again. Elena leaned her head back against the wall, unblinking beneath the hood. She still had not been spoken to and not once had anyone spoken to her. Only the metallic clink of the coin moved them.
The hood pressed tight against her face, already soaked through before she realized she’d been crying. The cloth was heavy, soaked through, and holding the sound in.

She still hadn’t been asked any additional questions. There wasn’t an intake system. She was here. Her throat cracked with thirst, even as the guards provided water, and sometimes tea; and breadcrumbs. That pattern did not change for several days. Each extraction followed the same sequence of fifteen steps forward beyond her room, then one left turn, then ten steps more, then the coin. The clink arrived precisely when Elena expected the coin to arrive, with the same volume, the same pitch, and the same pause afterward.

Each night, she drew the floor plan in the dirt beside her cot, marking corridors, doors, stairwells, and guard posts with a broken stick. When the route held, she wiped the map away. The recording in the room played again and again night and day, her voice, but with incorrect words. “...if you kill 100 Russian children …” Elena physically flinched, her own voice stripped of pain and made sterile. She pivoted toward the sound, but concrete blocked the angle before she could see the source. Somewhere beyond the wall, her manufactured voice repeated through a cheap speaker, flattened by static.

The next walk began as before. Two guards came and led her out. Fifteen steps, left turn, ten more, then the clink again. Elena kept the numbers in her mouth. The door opened as she braced for the usual pivot into the chamber, but they passed it. Three more steps followed. Then four. There was no new sound, no change in air, the floor remained concrete, a vent hummed, but she was someplace new. Her right shoulder brushed against the same patch of wall twice. She was someplace new.

The moment the latch clicked, Elena was back in the same cell. The route taught her to trust repetition, and now repetition betrayed her as she pressed her palms to the wall, and the cement gave nothing back. Her body remained in place while the corridor shifted beneath her. The route blurred, and she lost her place. Her hands trembled against the cold stone of what resembled her cell.

Her breathing became more shallow. As she closed her eyes inside the hood, she no longer believed the space around her answered her. She couldn’t say which was worse, the turn that came too early or the one that arrived too late, the pace no longer keeping a structure.

When she returned to the cell, the door opened without the familiar groan of rusted hinges. Above her, the low vibrational hum of the vent stuttered and failed, cutting the airflow entirely before resuming at half strength. The shift disoriented her. When the door closed behind her, she spoke into the empty room, but her voice didn’t come.

The guard’s hands moved beneath her arms again, but this time she stood before they lifted. Her eyes remained open beneath the cloth as the dark ceased to unsettle her and she took one step, then another, down the narrowing corridor. Her body tilted slightly to the left, not from imbalance, but to avoid a pipe that wasn’t there before. At the fifteenth pace, the pause came, but the coin did not follow. She listened for the signal, and before the door, the delayed sound arrived as the coin made contact.

Elena pressed her hands around her torso, holding together her previously damaged rib cage that wasn’t healing. She held herself together as she kept walking forward one step at a time. The guard ahead of her slowed as Elena followed without guidance. Her lips parted. “Try me.” Her voice returned only to offer surrender. Whether the guard heard her or her words mattered remained unknown. They took her into a new room where her cuffs weren’t fastened, leaving her hands free for the first time.

Two sets of footsteps approached. One voice spoke in strict and formal Russian, not to her, but to someone else in the room with her. A new door opened with resistance, the hinge groaning differently from the others. Her skin recoiled, but her hands remained at her sides. They had not wanted her to fight or confess but to obey. The tile was perfect, and the air was dead as the guards positioned her in the center, rested their hands briefly on each shoulder.

Then the hood lifted. Light struck her eyes with such force she staggered. Fluorescent arrays burned directly overhead, close and exposed. She blinked but did not raise a hand to shield herself. Her muscles locked as white light filled the room, burning across every wall before the blast took shape. The chamber extended outward, square and clinical, bounded by unmarked doors and glass partitions. She had no name for this place. Her feet remained on the tile.

Before her stood three officials in civilian clothes, arranged  judges without robes. Two sat behind the table, and the third remained standing with arms folded behind their back. On the screen above them, the likeness of Elena Morales resided beside a line of foreign symbols.

Her mouth stayed closed. The trial began. Elena reached for the lighter by instinct, but the pocket was empty, sewn flat against her thigh. No one had read a charge, asked a question, or allowed a defense. The chamber’s soundproofing swallowed every movement around her, while the glass returned no reflection and no visible camera gave her somewhere to look. The lights burned steadily as sweat gathered under her collar, and somewhere in the wall, an observation lens gave one small servo tick.

The marble floor reflected light without warmth, and above her rose-tiered seats, gray figures sat a few in black, a few in green. At the far end, a screen played her face in flicker and freeze, Maidan footage without sound, her younger self lit by firelight, mouth open mid-sentence, beside a list of charges and the name they still used for her country.
“Morales, Elena María.”
“Foreign terrorist.”
“Combatant without uniform.”
“Trafficker of subversion.”

The voices came from above, female, calm, and deliberate, with no urgency and no trace of feeling. They sounded rehearsed into the architecture itself, prepared long before Elena entered the chamber. The prosecutors remained hidden, and the sentence continued from somewhere overhead, as if judgment had started without waiting for her.

“...violated the sovereignty of the Russian Federation through unauthorized paramilitary intervention, the dissemination of destabilizing narratives, and the orchestration of cross-border insurgencies...”
“...in accordance with the emergency legal provisions established by Presidential Decree 301, the accused is hereby sentenced to death by fire, to be carried out at the discretion of the state...”
“...immediate and public transmission authorized.”
“...appeal is denied.”
“...effective immediately.”

The trial offered no ceremony. The projector fan kept turning, pushing white light across her face on loop. Beneath the table, Elena rubbed against her index finger as if the lighter wheel were still there. She was never asked to testify. Instead, a prerecorded confession played for the room using Elena’s faked voice.

The lights stayed on. A gloved hand crossed her vision and folded down the hood, slow and precise, until black fabric took the room again. She had wanted to say more, one more final use of her voice, even if only to deny these acquisitions. But names belonged to people someone meant to remember, and whoever put the hood back on was not erasing her. They were filing her away.

Tile passed underfoot, then concrete, the seam registering beneath her heel as she tracked the shift and tried to gauge direction. The route bent through a longer arc. The air cooled. Somewhere near her left ear, a vent released one short hiss before sealing again. They moved faster now, without the earlier ceremony, past the coin.

The door opened with the same hinge sound she had memorized, and the guards said nothing. She put herself onto the cot. This was where they would come for her. The voice on the screen had promised fire without appeal. No man behind a bench to look in the eye. Charges translated into a sentence. No lighter, no pocket weight, no small flame to test the air. Only the filtered-air valve clicked above her as the sentence forced her forward, elbows to knees, breathing hard. And her name, somewhere in the system, had already been cleared for removal.

She remained standing. The air scoured her throat with the same filtered, manufactured dryness. It lacked the chemical burn of bleach or the coppery tang of rusted vents; the room offered only recycled oxygen and the small regulator tick counting behind the wall. She reached forward and reached the edge of the bench. Identical height but different grain on the surface. She didn’t need to search for scratches. The layout had changed, but the purpose had not. Her room had changed.
The next time they came for her the same way they always did, two guards, with one on each side.

The argument deepened. Leather scraped, then a shoulder struck dull against concrete. One of them barked something. The other answered too close, too fast. They had forgotten her. Then came a hard blow, a burst of movement, and a gunshot so near it flattened the air inside the hood. After that, only the cooling tick of the pistol and the failed hum of the corridor nodes, something new and unclassified.
The corridor swallowed every sound her body should have made. She struck the floor without report, the walls carried no alarm, and even the static that had followed her before was gone.

She crawled first to one side, calibrating her speed to halt at any audio spike. Her shoulder met the wall. She followed forward, findinga corner where two planes of stone came together in a narrow edge. Rising hurt more than she expected. Her back on the wall. One hand stayed braced while the other pulled the hood against the corner and dragged it down. Cloth bit her skin. The first attempt failed. The second slipped. On the third, something sharper, a chipped edge, a metal or broken bracket, and began sawing.

The hood did not come free at once but fought her all the way down, catching in her hair, scraping across her nose, tightening before what became so much of her new world loosened. She kept working at the hood, blind and half ashamed, breathing through old sweat and canvas, certain each second that someone would laugh, or seize her, or let her go on because humiliation was the point. Nothing came. At last, the cloth tore, then shifted, then peeled back enough for light to begin its slow assault. Elena did not rip the hood off in triumph. She dragged the sack down inch by inch, winning sight the hard way, until the world beyond the fabric was reality again.

The Moscow concrete ran both ways, chipped and damp beneath newer cable stapled hard against the walls. She turned to her cell door, open behind her, with an observing eye, hung over the door’s window in front of her. The eye unit faced inward. A narrow articulated housing, no larger than a hand, was fixed on the opening to her cell. Elena reached up, brushing the still-warm inactive casing before her hand came back at once. More of them lined the corridor. An eye watched over every slit on every prisoner’s door.

Dead cameras watched from the corners above her. Receiver nodes clung to the seams where old plaster met newer brackets. Someone had rewired the old prison recently, as if one empire had entered another’s carcass and taught the stone a second obedience.

With all of the monitors, nothing answered, and no one came. She went forward. A body lay halfway down the hall in a Russian kit with a exit wound from a gunshot. The Russian guard collapsed in place, and the Chinese guard vanished. On the Russian guard, a biometric band flickered on the dead guard’s wrist. Elena knelt and inspected the unthinkable. The red band pulsed once, then again, strong and tracking, actively alerting the monitors to the guard’s final moments. A Chinese system on a Russian body in a Russian prison.

The gun and keys came off the corpse, the weapon heavier than she expected. The corridor matched the map she had built in the dark. She took the first turn too early. The direction could not have been right, as Elena turned back and let her living hypothesis chart her course. A quick stop, a step back, a correction. The stairwell to freedom she imagined in the blueprint of her mind was close. Two entrances opened ahead, with separate offices before the last door. Russian script on one side, and Mandarin on the other, with two vertical paths, separate and parallel. She took the Russian door and tried the keys.

It wasn’t a control room, but a repurposed maintenance closet. Aging monitors hung from mismatched brackets above a dented kettle, a ring board for keys, and a plastic tray of cheap lighters. Elena pocketed one without looking away from the screens. Overturned chairs and scattered logs marked a frantic evacuation. She crossed to the screens. The uneven feeds painted an obvious picture. Russian positions were of maintenance, laundry, the mess, restrooms, and showers, with degraded, dark cameras. The Chinese views were cleaner, wider, and steadier, including views of the Russian guard positions. One was the master, the other the slave.

The room’s primary desk held several screens, most of which were tied into the building’s security grid. The largest screen was different. A public channel for a brightly colored drone-racing circuit, saturated for the bunker’s stale light. Across the bottom, bold Mandarin characters translated into a universal headline she recognized before the text finished rendering: WHERE IS TALON?

Elena’s hand stopped over the breaker. Eight legs on stone, a rear joint favoring weight, the spider moved just how Elena anticipated after her second shot. Her victory wasn’t heroic; that victory was a daily occurrence in her childhood. The third shot confirmed Andrew was the pilot.

The broadcast was asking the question people actually cared about. Not where was Elena, but where was the pilot? Elena looked from the headline to the dead security grid and left the breaker untouched. Andrew had seen her too.

The displays held her focus. One screen showed the bridge lowering into place. Civilians gathered on the far side, unformed and violent in the floodlights, executing prison guards as they advanced. Another screen showed Chinese machine gunners already positioned on the other side of the bridge, hiding and ready for their final approach. The film revealed the trap. A third caught movement inside the outer perimeter; more attackers were coming, more gates were giving way. The prison itself was under Moscowite assault.

She pivoted to the switches with no map and no time, finding handwritten labels on some circuits and blank wear where others had faded. Her hand moved down the panel, flipping switches one after another while she checked the screens between each motion. The plan presented itself as she executed. Then the bridge lights flickered, then strobed in sequence in the universal language of SOS, enough to warn anyone watching not to cross into the kill zone. That was enough.

After that, Elena left the custodial command center, only noticing the smell of bleach after exiting the room. The blasts came before she reached the stairwell, a chain of concussive force rolling through the walls. On another level, there was the fainter sound of running. The reverberation rose through the shaft in two separate currents.

She started down. The stairwell curved as she descended. Another stair turned beside it, beyond a dividing wall. The Cyrillic alphabet was on one side, while Mandarin was on the other. But only one stairwell included observation slits, allowing observation of the other. There were more prisoners than those held in cells.

At the first landing, a locked gate cut across the Russian stairwell. The first key failed, the second did not turn, but the third key caught. The lock released with a hard mechanical click as Elena pulled the gate wide. Footsteps approached, heading toward her from the open hallway.

A sprung prisoner came through fast, face-to-face with Elena for a second, eyes wide and unbelieving, before he rushed past her toward the upper levels. They both had the same instinct, fear, before shared survival instincts evolved in an instant. The prison break was on. At first a trickle, then a drip, and soon there would be a flood.

She continued downward. The double helix repeated itself as she descended through curved turns, the same geometry at every level, each gate opening the same way as the one above. Bodies packed the corridor, pressed on rusted railings, elbows throwing desperate strikes, a hundred voices bouncing up the concrete shaft. Voices rose from the Chinese side now, sharper and nearer. Civilians were coming up that spiral with the momentum of a herd. Another crossing gate gave way under her hands. Someone from below shouted, but not one person in the crowd stopped.

The two crowds pressed against each other, bodies jammed in the stairwell, prisoners on one side and their saviors on the other. Her footing broke for half a step before the collision. Bodies pressed through the crossing from both directions. Prisoners forcing their way out, civilians forcing their way in. The gates Elena opened with the keys created access between the levels but brought confusion to the Mandarin- and Russian-marked stairwell, which they must never have known before.

An attacker armed with a rifle stumbled, went down, and vanished beneath the escaping prisoners. The two controlled paths broke into one human mass, and the stairwell, built for separation and command, was given back to fear, force, and the old physics of living bodies in motion.

Either way, Elena stayed on her feet. A man hit her from the left and drove her into the inner rail. Another prisoner’s body slid past on the right, squeezing between the crowd freed from a trap. The stairwell had no room left for choice. Only the force of the crowd remained as the wall between the two spiral stairs disappeared behind bodies and noise.

A shoulder slammed hard into Elena. She lifted entirely off the steps as the mass of bodies carried her downward. The crowd’s kinetic force dragged Elena forward. The mass poured downward and outward together. Some prisoners were still trying to climb over the heads of the crowd pressing in front of them.

Emergency medicine had taken the lift power for the overflow ward, so Anika carried the plywood up six flights herself. Every landing of the Kyiv hospital had become a different kind of corridor: wounded soldiers on stretchers, civilians sleeping upright against walls, nurses stepping over cable runs while orderlies pushed empty oxygen racks back toward the lower floors. The stairwell smelled of iodine, plaster dust, wet wool, and generator smoke drawn through cracked vents. No one asked why Anika had carried a sheet of construction board through the hospital by herself. There wasn’t anyone left in Kyiv to tell Anika no.

Consolation was not in Anika’s plan. Anika entered Andrew’s room and crossed to his window, pressed the sheet of plywood against the lower pane, and drove screws she brought with her into the frame. Andrew’s body didn’t move as she did her work.

By the time Rupert arrived in Andrew’s room, Anika’s knife lay flat on the table beside Andrew’s hospital bed. Above the door’s latch, Anika placed a mirror shard to see who was entering Andrew’s room before they did. Rupert walked in and stopped beside the end of the hospital bed.
“We don’t know what he will remember,” Rupert said.
“Not the truth,” Anika replied.

Rupert took a seat in the chair in the room’s corner. Fluids moved down their narrow lines into Andrew’s arms next to him, unspooling through ports and into the veins of a man who had not moved in hours. Rupert’s chair creaked under him as his hands wringed, as he watched the monitor.
She took one step forward, the toe of her boot brushing tile as she adjusted her white earbuds while her other hand closed around the knife on the table.

At the scrape of steel, Andrew’s eyes opened as if each layer of vision had to peel itself free from some deeper frame. The lights did not seem to reach him immediately. They went to the side of the room, then up toward the ceiling, then through the air itself as a kind of slow, blurring return to the room around him. His head turned toward the sound of the machines, toward the glow of the screen, toward the drip tower, and then toward Anika.
“Elena,” Andrew spoke.
Andrew’s mouth formed Elena’s name before Anika’s white earbud caught the sound. Strength sat in the voice, not fragility.
Rupert cleared his throat but kept his eyes low. “No, Andrew,” he said. “Your friend Elena isn’t here.”

Andrew pushed himself upright against the unfamiliar hospital bed and reached out before his eyes fully opened. His hands searched the empty space beside him with the same urgency as a rider reaching for a saddle that should have been hanging within arm’s reach. He looked down the length of his body toward his bare feet. Across the room, Anika stood motionless with the knife still in her hand.
“Elena isn’t here,” Rupert continued. “Andrew, something terrible has happened.”
Andrew’s eyes rolled back to the ceiling, then snapped back down onto Rupert.

“She was captured days ago and taken to Moscow by the enemy, and put on trial.” Rupert’s shoulders sank as he continued. “She was in Kyiv, of course,” Rupert said. “But she left the morning after her broadcast.”
Andrew turned his head, slow and controlled. Now the look he cast at Rupert was even more chilling. “When exactly did this happen, and where is my team?”
Rupert flinched his head before answering, “I risked everything,” he said. Rupert lunged toward the hospital bed, toward Andrew, trying to physically close the inexorable gap.

Anika spoke before Rupert could close the distance. “I am Anika,” she said. “Rupert did not act alone.” Andrew focused on her, with a predator’s fixed attention. “I sent her away,” she continued. “I am the reason Elena came here, and the reason for her capture.”

His hand took the line in his arm, and he pulled the IV free, letting the plastic tube fall as fluid arced down the inside of his wrist. Anika watched him stand in the thin hospital gown, legs steady beneath the pallor, one hand bracing briefly against the rail. Her next move would depend on him.
Rupert stood unsure of what to do with his hands. “Andrew,” he began. “You’ve been under partial coma for seventy-two hours. You might not remember everything yet.”
Andrew ignored him and did not look away from Anika.
Rupert tried again. “Elena’s been on their broadcasts. Her trial is a global news event, Andrew.”
“Do they think she is a criminal?” Andrew said.
“Yes,” Rupert answered. “They are keeping her in Moscow, but Andrew, the final broadcast airs today.”
“What does final mean for Elena, Rupert?” he said.
Anika turned the knife once in her hand, watching the exchange.
“We ran out of time,” Rupert said.

Andrew’s attention sharpened, though his face remained calm. The muscles along his face did not twitch, nor did the corners of his mouth shift. Anika took a half-step forward as the monitors kept ticking, neither calm nor warning.

“The Russians assaulted Kyiv on all fronts, you know this,” Rupert continued. “She was sent away so she could survive. Our best assessment suspects her guide betrayed her to the enemy. I’m sorry, Andrew.”
Andrew remained motionless as Rupert’s hand tightened on the bed rail.
