“Elena volunteered for this,” Rupert kept trying. “Her footage moved public support for the allies across the world. There have been protests in a hundred cities. You liberated Kyiv, Andrew. We are on track again. We’ve regained the initiative.”
Andrew turned away from him. His chin rested on his left shoulder, where he saw Anika, as if recalibrating where relevance now resided. “Did you design the city’s defense?” he asked her.
Rupert stopped talking. Andrew’s question landed, exact and sharpened. Anika straightened her stance before answering, though she betrayed no outward hesitation. Her voice held no apology, only fact.
“I did,” she said. “The concentric corridors, the underground fallback structure, the decoy generators planted along the former metro tunnels. I led the defense of the city until your arrival.”
The monitor ticked faster beside him. “I saw the shape of the defense from the sky and thought Elena was the designer.”

Anika shifted her weight back to her heels. His approval landed lightly; his exact measure of her did not. Her bombed-out corridors, the collapsed tenements, the severed bridges had revealed their purpose from the air.
Rupert attempted to reclaim the room, his voice reaching for purpose. “We believed in her, and we still do.”
Andrew didn’t stop looking at Anika. “So now I’m here. What does he give you that I don’t?”

She did not reach for title or protocol, only for clarity, drawn forward not by obligation. Rupert’s role in the war was undeniable; his mind had held back collapse for longer than anyone expected, but her thoughts did not turn to his accolades. Rupert was not Slava’s legacy, or Logan’s paint on his face, or Elena’s strength of will. They no longer needed a strategist. The time had come for execution.
“That depends, Andrew,” she said. “Are you capable of piloting the system again?”

Andrew’s eyebrows lifted by a fraction. Neither Andrew nor Anika acknowledged Rupert after that. He reached for the edge of the hospital bed and rested his hand against the reinforced rail. Rupert’s older hands lingered on the reinforced rail. Then he turned and placed one hand gently on Anika’s shoulder. The touch held no weight and asked for no response. Outside the room, Jean Pierre and Felix approached the door, weapons within reach, ready for direction.
Rupert looked once at Andrew, then at Anika, and understood the room had moved without him.

Rupert put on his jacket with military precision, fastening each closure in the same practiced order. Anika watched the decorations settle, logistics command, coalition planning, intelligence liaison, the London Cross, all polished and official. The jacket recorded a successful career in rooms where battles became arrows on glass, but none of its honors belonged to mud, evacuation lines, or wards where people died. He straightened his collar first, then the knot of his tie, then smoothed both sides of his jacket until the visitor badge lay flat. Rupert crossed between Jean Pierre and Felix, while Anika watched the medals pass.

The boarded window faced east, and by the morning after Rupert left, light moved through liberated Kyiv without the furtive quality the siege had taught the city to wear. Below engines kept the square awake. Before Andrew’s breakfast, Anika marked the morning route with black ticks beside the roadside charges. By the time he woke, she was there.

Beneath the hospital, outside, new recruits crossed pavement in loose formations, engines idled, voices rose and broke against one another, and someone had tied a resistance flag to the antenna of a burned-out truck. The wreck became a landmark now decorated with flowers.

Work came to Andrew’s room because Anika was there. A folding table had been dragged to the window and converted into a command post of maps, batteries, radios, ration cards, hospital routes, minefield notations, and casualty sheets. Couriers came and went. Anika initialed what mattered, rejected what slowed them, and kept one eye on Andrew.

The radio somewhere below carried accordion music through the concrete in faint rhythmic bursts, cheerful enough to sound almost offensive. When the room settled, Anika turned. She pulled the folding chair from the table, set it beside the bed, and sat.

“Elena reported to a commander I knew who died saving her. I took them to Rupert in Lublin. The enemy killed two of the American volunteers. After the battle, I sent Elena away before the enemy’s counter-attack began.”
Andrew returned her truth with his own. “I was the spider in the square with you,” he said.
“Elena suspected you were,” Anika said. “I told her volunteers come from every side, some for profit, some for games played from home, and some for both.”
“Your name has been on my commission board,” he said.
Anika let his words settle first. “So you understand,” she said. “Most people need more lies than that to live with themselves.”
“Do you?” he asked.
“No,” Anika replied.
His gaze moved across the room. He chose his words the way he flew. “So she told you about me,” Andrew said.
Anika took a single step forward, keeping one hand visible at her side, the other hand behind her back. “She did,” she said.
“I went to her parents,” Andrew said. “I told them I'd bring her home.”
Anika matched his reverence. “Would you consider one more contract for her?”
Andrew’s eyebrows twitched from their middle up again.
“A personal one,” she added. “Not for Ukraine and not for defense. Revenge for what Moscow will do to our friend.”
“Your friend,” Andrew said.
Anika tilted her head to him. “Yes, and my job was to protect her,” she replied.
Andrew’s eyes stayed on her. “Who gave her to them?”
Anika did not answer quickly, but she did. “The guide’s name is Ryba. He was another one of Rupert’s.”
Andrew looked past her toward his pulse display.
“Do you have a picture of him?” he asked.

Anika kept her eyes on Andrew, watching for grief or calculation in his face. When the broadcast started, she would be with Andrew. Once he saw what would happen next, Anika would stand between him and his drone system, unless she was confident in the direction he would fly.

Anika brushed her hands against her thighs and stood. Pale grit still marked one cuff from the morning walk before breakfast, and the old backpack had not returned with her. She checked the passage behind them once, then faced the room again before the broadcast began.
A knock struck the door. For a long moment, the pilot and the solider stared at each other. Then came the flap of a curtain, and Jean Pierre’s firm voice.
“Anika, bring him. The others are all watching together. Elena’s trial is live.”
Anika looked back at Andrew one last time. “You need to decide what kind of friend you are,” she said. “Remember, we share an enemy now.”

Together they left the room for a warmer, louder hall, a corridor of mismatched voices and war-born exhaustion in the overcrowded wing. Fluorescents buzzed overhead in erratic sequence. Blackout drapes sealed across the tall western windows. In the lobby, an old tube TV hung on a bracket above the intake desk, broadcasting a clear signal to the dozens gathered.

Elena was front and center under a black hood cinched tightly around her neck. The signal was clear. Velvet drapes hung behind her, flanked by men in uniforms crowded with medals. A judge sat above them all. Behind Elena was a wall of Russian tricolor and double-headed eagles. They had dressed her in Soviet prison attire.

Anika read the captions on screen before the volume reached the white earbud. The white earbud carried the tones in a thin, flattened pulse while nurses, doctors, patients, and soldiers watched the television. What the white earbud didn’t miss was Ridge, Cam, and Lorna greeting Andrew, who was walking the hallways for the first time.

On the TV, a wide shot now showed a young female prosecutor. Her voice carried rehearsed care. The charges were a manifesto of foreign interference, coordination with NATO irregulars, and attempted destabilization of sovereign Russian territory. The attending officers in their top brass signaled a state-level offense that would be made an example of.
“The accused will now have justice carried forth as sentenced by the high court in adherence to the laws of the state.”

Felix breathed loudly, gutturally, “Shut this off.” Jean Pierre moved to him and set a hand on Felix’s shoulder. Felix did not shout after that. His mouth opened once, then closed around nothing, and the first sound that came from him was smaller than his body had any right to make. Jean Pierre moved before anyone else did, one hand finding the back of Felix’s neck, the other closing over the shotgun before Felix’s grip could turn useless or dangerous.
Most of the room remained still without making a comment. Nurses, soldiers, and patients all paid heed to the predator amongst them.
Ridge whispered, “This is looking bad.”
“Oh, take her hood off. Let us see her,” Cam said.
Lorna leaned slightly forward, her voice lower than the rest. “Cam, this is not the time to be heard.”
Andrew moved closer to the TV as the timber was carried into the courtroom. Laborers hefted a rough crate and set Elena’s wooden chair at the center of the timber stack.

Anika kept her eyes on Andrew. His mouth stayed closed, his hands stayed flat, and the white earbud gave her nothing useful from the room behind him. Crying broke across too many corners of the room for the earbud to separate one grief from another. Anika removed the white earbud and quickly pulled a small battery replacement from a pouch, exchanged the old battery for a new one, and placed the earbud back again. She only saw Cam’s face go white when the torch was placed on the timber.
“So what is your plan?” Andrew asked.
Anika answered.
“A raiding doctrine,” she said. “Let us see how far we can go.”

The drone count on his display had reached nine hundred and climbing. Anika’s briefing, logged at T-minus-four hours, was still running at 1.5x while Andrew worked ahead of her voice. The swarm was more than his system now: NATO warehouse drones dragged from storage, commercial fleets stripped and rewired, the mesh expanding past anything he had mapped alone. By the time Andrew had indexed the first warehouse cluster, Anika’s requisition order had already overrun his private map.

The order went out across Allied-held territories. Civilians brought theirs in, off-the-shelf quadcopters, customized attack drones, and recon toys with cracked lenses. Couriers from shuttered megacorps were pulled off racks and reborn as eyes. Andrew’s system was the ideal recipient, ancient technology in some aspects, but vastly easier to maintain and modify.

Each new arrival carried a pulse and a promise. The data flow grew exponentially. As the drone mesh drew, more soldiers arrived by the hundreds and civilian volunteers by the thousands. Their approach tracked across the mesh. Not just the fighters in digital camouflage or the ex-special forces marked by gait and stance, but whole lines of non-military personnel. Men with wives, wives with children, grandmothers holding relic crosses to their chests. Anika spoke about them plainly, and the mesh logged each one.
“Why would we prevent them from shielding us?” Anika asked.

They marched together as a moving mass with Andrew riding at the center inside a fortified carrier, engine-hot and plated in fresh armor. Mechanics had unbolted the old leather chair from the transport plane that morning and hard-mounted the seat frame into the belly of the truck before the column rolled east. The new floor bolts squealed whenever the carrier hit broken pavement. Andrew stayed planted in the chair while the army moved around him.

Around him moved the composite host, irregulars in flannel and leather, retirees with bolt-action rifles, convicts in recon vests, monks with bandages strapped beneath body armor. And to their flanks, disciplined formations of the Allied Legions, NATO’s final expeditionary wave. Above them all, every drone available hummed with linked precision. The pod remained static, but the data input flooded his cortex.

Andrew’s killer drones still formed the primary attack wing. Their formations could fly lethal patterns on breath-speed cues. But now he had liquidity. With hundreds more eyes in the sky, he wasn’t tethered to strike vectors. He could watch roads, relay maps, filter signals, and flag distress calls. Responsibilities broadened as the mesh became more than a weapon.
Sensor silk and gel-woven harnesses anchored his motionless frame, each interface reading his stillness as intent.

The Desna River marked the next barrier. The mesh flagged the twisted trestle remains of collapsed bridges. They were north of Kyiv now, heading into Russian lands not invaded since the 1940s. There were no ferry boats, and no time to make pontoons. On Anika’s command, the advancing nation forded the river.

The feed showed mothers with children on their backs, teenagers with plastic rifles, stretchers pulled by mule-drones, and medics counting blankets. Some waded, others swam. Children were lifted above their shoulders, handed down lines. Sleds were floated with empty fuel drums. Shivering bodies huddled into warmth before moving again.

The pattern and movement density emerged where the line slowed, cold cramps, and small bodies created choke points. The mesh recorded all of their advances. Already, families had stopped in the cold, women folding over children while the rear of the line kept moving.
Anika's voice crackled through a drone unit placed inside the command trailer. Andrew used that drone’s microphone to communicate from meters away, his voice arriving without his body.
“Attrition from the river, Anika. Mostly elderly and young,” Andrew said through the hovering unit.
Anika answered the drone rather than the body strapped inside the carrier.
“Good,” Anika said.

Overall, the formation stretched for five kilometers forward and backward with the trailer as the nucleus. From the river to the forests, to the hills behind them where volunteers continued to join the mobilization, each pulse was an observable heartbeat, not a metaphor but a signal his drones could measure.

They moved without banners and only with resolve. Carried first by a scout platoon, then picked up by a wheeled convoy, a song emerged from the marchers on the other side of the river crossing. A low chord, more of a rhythm, passed mouth to mouth.
Anika’s voice cut through the upper channel, entering the feed where his commands usually lived.
“Andrew, confirm western flank integrity,” Anika said.

Andrew balanced hundreds of reference points while her request blinked in the command queue. The melody stopped at his next command mid-formation. Fourteen drones blinked infrared across the treeline. The perimeter beyond the march held clear in every direction.
Again, Anika addressed him. “Andrew. Confirm now.” The command cursor pulsed once beside her channel.

Closer to the rear of the march, Andrew tracked a child dragging a pack twice her size, one strap cutting into her coat. Next to her, a boy with a cast pushed a wagon while an old man used the handle to stay upright. A child with a heavy pack, an old man struggling to keep up.
“Andrew.”
His lips moved, giving her back the one word she needed. “Clear.”

They advanced past towns and railheads, river crossings logged as weak points, intersections flagged by drone pass-throughs. Routes were swept daily, remapped nightly. Convoys entered from the southern ridgelines, civilians from the border west of Sumy, logistics pushed through Chernihiv freight lines. They passed through town centers without stopping. When people waved, he recognized them as civilians.

Noor adjusted his lines, wiped his face, and her lips moved in his peripheral vision while the system feedback ran too loud in his earpiece. She adjusted the drip rate on his primary line, wiped his brow with a thermal pad, and connected another IV for a flush before the stimulant cycle. She leaned in to check his ocular dilation but said nothing else. The mesh didn’t leave room for conversation, though her voice registered on the non-critical comms channel, volume low, frequency tagged as non-tactical.
A command drone hovered above the console internal to the carrier. Noor’s hands replaced the hydration sleeve while he tremored as the mesh adjusted sync.

Cam worked behind the primary station wall, scanning the routing buffer for bleed. His stylus moved in tight loops over a half-dead tablet, sweat pooling beneath his collar. Ridge stood by the side rack sorting battery modules by heat dispersion rating, rotating through tasks, quiet and constant, one cable over his shoulder, one eye on fuel cell integrity. He looked at Andrew once or twice but didn’t linger.

When a drone caught his profile, Andrew saw Noor at the dish array, tapping alternating rhythms across a coil keyboard, bruised focus, dark circles, exhaustion. Noor moved without pause, wiping his brow, adjusting the IV line, not looking at his face. They were still here.

Thousands of inputs cycled through the mesh, but this view held him: his own body in the chair, restrained, mapped from above. He was the reason they’d stayed together, and now he was the thing being held together. They could have walked away after Kyiv, after transport clearance to London, after the first meeting with Rupert, but they didn’t. They stayed when the war stopped being theory and pressed skin-close, remaining inside the steel belly of a moving warhead.

The edge of the mesh came alive. Drones fed new terrain in tight sequence, low fields, hill ridges, armored trails thrown against the horizon. Andrew advanced three forward scouts beyond protocol and absorbed the field. Enemy structures were spread across the treeline: reinforced berms layered in sand canvas, watchfires shielded behind rock, drone swarms orbiting high ground in controlled intervals. Above the haze, towers held position, guarded but not yet engaged. He did not check for battlefield consensus; Anika’s override request sat unanswered.

He locked one channel to elevation, another to movement logic. Commands came without pause, just as they had when he worked alone. The forward screen reloaded as he carved a corridor through the defense. He tagged every entry funnel, predicted the fallback curve, and mapped the cover-to-contact ratio by instinct. If the enemy advanced now, they would lose air support. If they held position, he would drain them across blind terrain. He overlaid orders before saying them, ranged strike logic and shield collapse paths while trap algorithms pulsed and cleared.

Andrew tracked the enemy perimeter, vision mapped in concentric threat layers. Northern hills were screened by low-range drone curtains. Beyond them: tower clusters, antenna gates, glint patterns from glassed optics. He leaned forward, transmitting flyover paths via neural link. Flanking routes, soft entries, wind vectors. Every second built toward the first strike.
“Andrew,” someone said. Then again, louder. “Andrew.”

He didn’t respond. Her audio pinged his receiver, but targeting held the channel closed until the third alert. And then Elena, first a childhood memory, then burning, and gone. Ryba, the enemy who did that to her, was just ahead of him.

Then Anika collapsed the mesh, every relay cut at once, and the neural feed ruptured through him. Blood came from his nose and ears at once. The sky was static and white cloth and screaming metal. His limbs kicked against phantoms. Andrew's Talons kicked against the pod's foot grid. The restraints groaned under the force of his spasms. For a second, he was trying to breathe underwater, and then he wasn’t.

The compulsive movement tore a wet cough out of him, and dark vomit splashed across the cracked leather seat and down the split armrest beneath the damaged red wing decal. Noor braced him upright while Cam disconnected the thread on Anika’s order instead of Andrew’s. Ridge was already pulling towels across the chair and shouting vitals over the noise inside the carrier. The sky vanished from the room. Andrew dragged air back into his lungs and coughed again.
“You pulled me out,” he rasped.
Anika didn’t flinch. “You’re not dead.”
“You gave no answer.”
“You don’t get to pull me.”
“I already did.”
For a moment, the only sound was the drip of IV fluid, the fan in the wall, and the distant thrum of another convoy docking.
“I said your name three times,” she said. “That was one more than I owed you.”
He coughed, heaving. “You need me in the sky.”
Her eyes didn’t move. “Don’t make me choose between your grief and my army.”
Blood still smeared the corner of Andrew’s mouth as his hands trembled.
“Suit back up,” she said, already turning. “Be useful, don’t be a problem.”

Inside the mesh returned all at once, heat, motion, altitude, alerts, while the drones above him stirred, and woke as hounds. They loitered after the unplugging, twitching for a signal in formation above the trees. When Andrew came back online, they surged. He gritted against the pain but reached for control fast enough to mask his headache from the system. The enemy had seen him unplugged once already. Next time, they wouldn’t hesitate.

Andrew deployed a 360-degree scout and decoy perimeter. The northernmost were swatted down by short-range air defenses woven into a multi-tier line, first mobile net batteries, and flak-turrets disguised as farm equipment, then spider drones perched and ready on power towers for extra height before their attack jumps.

As Andrew worked through the next attack plan, Noor cycled stabilizers into his IV while Cam adjusted the overlays, and Ridge followed Felix through the carrier, trying to stay useful. The old leather chair creaked softly beneath Andrew as the fresh carrier bolts settled into place. Back in the saddle again, he tunneled beneath the enemy defenses, and killed the power with a commercial scout carrying a shaped charge smaller than a car battery. The enemy grid dropped out at once. Even the spider drones blinked, faltered, and collapsed where they stood.
In the tent behind him, Anika spoke to Jean Pierre and Felix, the relay drone catching every word.
“We’re choosing not to move,” Felix said.
Anika’s voice came calm as ice. “That’s the idea.”
Noor changed the IV and placed one hand on Andrew. “He will need rest soon,” she said.

Andrew didn’t argue. Blood already ran from his nose, the tether fraying him inside out. Felix and Jean Pierre quietly laid fallback protocols. Assault detachments would advance ahead of the main body, unfolding from within the civilian ranks once the order dropped. Their role would be to drive outward until contact, baiting the enemy to engage. Behind that screen, the levy would shape fallback corridors, channeling any retreat through pre-sighted kill zones laced with mines and overlapping arcs. Once ready, the assault detachments would fall back into overwatch.

Three empty IV bags sat on the deck beside the pod, a fourth half-spent. The teardrop advanced mile by mile, less a shape than thousands of bodies narrowing into one road. Villages folded open, and rail depots burned low. Overhead, the swarm never stopped, and there were constant missile flashes over distant ridgelines, sudden pillars of smoke from empty hamlets.
His lips moved before he meant them to. “They left the door open,” he said. They advanced into trenches that should’ve been occupied. Gun nests sat empty. Rail lines were cold.
The trenches ahead were unmanned, and for now, the roads stayed clear.

The swarm still took casualties. Each drone downed sent a ripple through Andrew’s body, snapping behind the eyes. He spoke only in bleed rates and fire zones. Noor checked his face when he said nothing else. His last non-tactical word had been logged eighteen hours ago.
“And when the last machine falls?” Jean Pierre asked.

Andrew remained in the old leather chair from his childhood, keeping his shoulders drawn forward while the fever worked through him. The carrier rolled east beneath the floor bolts, and every hard mile pulled another small creak from the seat frame. Noor stayed beside him, wiping his brow in steady intervals.
Anika commanded Andrew’s team. "One hour," she said. "Allow him to get some sleep after that."

Felix redirected drone crates by hand, barking quiet instructions while the medics repacked field kits in case of emergency evacuation. Noor adjusted the final IV line, then reached into the side compartment for the stabilizer vial they had been saving for later.

The hum of the control core deepened slightly, but Andrew’s ocular focus remained locked on the dead feed. The first counterattack came hard, just as they had planned, with strike teams from the east, coordinated flanking, and thick surface-to-air battles against the counter-swarm. Anika rotated Andrew out six minutes before contact. He plugged back in. The combat log showed forty-seven engagements in the six hours he’d been offline, all resolved. From above, he mapped the burn fields, noted ammunition dispersal, and calculated casualty profiles.

When he reconnected, the enemy’s fallback positions had tightened by four hundred meters. They had used his six hours. Andrew slept again after only twenty-four hours, his dominance of the upper lanes absolute, but the third cycle wasn’t needed. By the time he reconnected to the core, Anika’s assault detachments had already collapsed back from the forward rim and picked the targets. Andrew commanded a full-spectrum sweep for signatures of resistance, for any cluster of heat that suggested unit cohesion. The trenches ahead sat abandoned, and for a while, the roads remained clear.
The chair was getting louder. Andrew sank a quarter-inch deeper into the worn leather seat while the frame creaked beneath him.

Anika’s sleep schedule held, and his drones scattered wide across the northern approach as perimeter scouts fed back the shape of the horizon. Across villages etched in soot and paint, the same mural returned, again and again. Elena’s face rising from fire rendered in thread, plaster, and scorched vinyl. Elena’s face, reproduced in every material at hand. The deeper they advanced, the more often her face was there.

Through the drone audio, the march was singing. The same song, passed forward through thousands of voices, independent of any orchestration. Andrew held the feed and listened. "What are they singing?" he said.
Anika eventually answered. "A poet's last instructions," she said. "Where to bury him and what to do after."
“Can you translate this for me?” Andrew replied.
“No need to translate this, Andrew. This song is in the blood of our land,” Anika said before sharing the words with Andrew.
The wide Dnipro roars and bellows,
The angry wind howls and groans,
The tall oaks bend and sway and crack,
The dark forest moans.
The old willow weeps and whispers,
Bent down to the ground,
The mountain sends a murmur back,
Echoing the sound.
The moon hides behind the clouds,
As though it were in grief,
The wide Dnipro roars and bellows,
Like a wailing thief.
But no one knows, no one hears.
How the heart of Ukraine weeps
Its tears pour into the river.
As it mourns and sleeps.

The song ended outside the tent before the last voices fully died. Andrew remained in the chair with the sync lines taped along his neck and wrists. The drone net hovered over the forward road, feeding him weather, heat signatures, and the dark geometry of Moscow beyond the eastern line.

Anika stood beside the table after finishing the translation as the tent flap opened with a hard snap of canvas rings. Felix entered first, then Jean Pierre, both road-stained and armed. Between them walked a prisoner with his hands bound in front by wire.
Felix shoved him forward onto the floorboards. “Found him moving behind the third column.”
Jean Pierre closed the flap behind them. “He says he was not following us.”
“I wasn’t,” the prisoner said. “I was going to Moscow for work.”
Anika’s hand moved to the pistol at her belt. “What work?”
The prisoner lifted his face. Dirt had dried along the cut beside his mouth, and the old coat still held most of its buttons.
“I am a Warden,” he said.
Anika walked closer. “What kind of prison?” she asked.
His bound hands tightened around each other. “Foster programs and relocation.”
“Children,” Anika said.
Felix looked at Jean Pierre. “Parasite.”
Anika drew the pistol and raised it without haste. The muzzle settled between the prisoner’s eyes.
The prisoner’s words shortened. “Wait, I have heard rumors.”
Anika’s clicked back the hammer.
“These are Moscow rumors,” the prisoner said.
Andrew’s drone nearest the tent shifted lower outside the canvas. The relay speaker clicked once as the machine moved closer.
Anika did not look away from the prisoner. “Talk.”
“Big changes are coming from the top,” he said. “There was a prison break. The gates to Moscow are closing, and they are paying a premium for new guards.”
Jean Pierre’s rifle sling clicked once against the tent pole. “Practical parasite.”
“That is not enough,” Anika said. Her pistol rose higher, angling toward his temple.
“I have heard more,” the prisoner said.
Andrew’s right hand opened against the chair grip. “What else?”
The prisoner turned toward Andrew’s voice. “She lives.”
Anika’s pistol stopped against his skin.
Andrew’s nearest drone slipped beneath the tent flap, rotors whispering over the floorboards.
The prisoner looked into the fence. “I met her in Lublin. Your side caught me, but let me go after 30 days. There must be a big policy because you clearly don’t know anything about prisons.”
Anika cocked the pistol. The click carried cleanly through the tent.
“They lied,” the prisoner said quickly. “The prison break is why they had to burn the second girl.”
The drone held position a few feet away from his face. Andrew sat motionless in the chair, but the machine’s camera adjusted, narrowing on the prisoner’s eyes as Anika’s pistol dropped.
The prisoner continued. “Elena Morales started the prison break in Moscow.”
Andrew’s voice came through the drone speaker now only inches away from the Warden. “Tell me everything she told you.”

The chill of the grocery store offered no comfort, only the fluorescent hum of failing bulbs and the heavy scent of spoiled dairy. Ryba entered confidently. Ryba’s gaze dropped to the authentic Talons on his feet, the pristine tread once coveted in a Lublin bunker as the shoes of a king, and knelt to adjust the laces. His frozen fingers fumbled against the eyelets and failed to grip the aglets, slipping for the third time before he left the lace dragging and stood.

A puddle of spilled milk spread across the cracked linoleum of aisle three. Ryba carried the white stain forward on the dark soles of his shoes, never once looking down. Near the end of the aisle, an older Russian man on his knees scrubbed the floor with slow, repetitive exhaustion. The worker shifted slightly out of the way as Ryba approached. Ryba did not offer eye contact, nor did he acknowledge the man's muttered slur; he simply adjusted his path and walked by, not once glancing at the exhausted janitor scrubbing the floor beside him.

At the front register, Ryba reached across the counter and took a pack of cigarettes. He tapped the box twice against the glass with precise, practiced habit, one of the only things his hands still executed perfectly. Behind the clerk, hastily pasted Mandarin signage dominated the older, fading Cyrillic text.

Ryba reached into his coat and slid a stack of Chinese yuan across the counter. The clerk looked at the foreign currency, his hands locking over the register. The queue of locals behind Ryba went entirely still. Ryba did not argue or panic. His lips stayed closed. He had the money now for what he wanted. Fixing teeth wasn’t his priority. His hand remained perfectly flat on the counter. Money overrode everything here. Finally, a manager moved forward, scooped up the yuan, and slid the cigarettes across the glass.

Stepping back out the doors, the freezing Moscow wind hit with matter-of-fact brutality. Ryba shifted, pluming white against the gray light, but he did not shiver or react to the cold. He moved around the puddle and drifted away from the center of the avenue, tucking himself along the rusted edges of the street, surviving as he always had: by queuing where others queued, shifting when told to shift, and participating in the collapse of the world by default.

He turned onto Tverskaya Street, the wide radial avenue that once served as the beating heart of the capital. Now, it was an artery choked with the debris of a quiet surrender. The monumental neoclassical facades and the stern, black cube of the Lenin Institute loomed in the gray light, but their massive scale had been humiliated by the occupation. Heavy banners bearing Mandarin characters were bolted directly over the fading Cyrillic of the state banks. It was the architecture of absorption—one empire entering the carcass of another and teaching the stone a second obedience.

Ryba kept near the storefronts along the avenue, slipping into a line of civilians at a makeshift military checkpoint. He queued where the others queued, shifting his weight exactly when told to shift, participating in the occupation by default. He observed the checkpoint with clinical detachment. Chinese machine gunners stood on elevated platforms, treating the shivering Russian citizens below them with open, relaxed hostility.

Ahead of Ryba, an older woman carrying a heavy, oil-stained bundle stumbled, her knees hitting the freezing mud. The bundle slipped from her grip, scattering dented tins across the frozen concrete. Ryba bypassed the spill, his matte gray Talons keeping a steady pace as he looked the other way.

But as he walked, the physical reality of his betrayal began to exact its toll. The blister on his heel rubbed raw against the stiff, pristine heel cup of Reese’s authentic Talons. The factory-clean tread offered no comfort on the blood-slicked mud, slipping slightly with every third step. His hand plunged into his coat, seeking the textured friction of the yuan.

Near the entrance to a requisitioned state bank, a Russian man in a pristine, tailored wool overcoat stood before two Chinese military police. The man was speaking in low, urgent Mandarin, his hands gesturing frantically to a stack of transit manifests. He produced a thick, sealed envelope from his breast pocket, sliding it across the rusted hood of a transport vehicle in a seamless, transaction-like interaction. The officers took the envelope without breaking their relaxed hostility. The businessman adjusted his collar and kept walking, giving him a wider berth on the frozen concrete.

Ryba’s hitched, catching sharply in his throat. The rhythm of the city began to stutter, the flow of foot traffic shifting into a sluggish, unpredictable crawl. The low, heavy idle of a Chinese military transport truck vibrated through Ryba’s pristine Talons. A block away, a rusted barricade collapsed under the weight of a passing tread, but the concussive boom reached Ryba's ears a full second after the dust plumed into the sky.

Ryba looked up at the rusted ironwork of a Soviet streetlamp. Clamped to the decaying metal was a newly installed, matte-black Chinese camera drone. He looked further down the avenue. Dozens of them lined the cornices of the Art Nouveau hotels, an unblinking lattice of surveillance staring down at the frozen street.
The vehicle was parked at the intersection. A perimeter of Chinese guards stood relaxed in the freezing mud, their weapons resting lazily against their vests. They were loading the young.
A line of Russian men and women, their faces pale and eyes hollow, was being systematically herded up the steel ramp into the back of the canvas-covered truck.

Then, the compliance fractured. An older man broke from the sidewalk, his hands grasping frantically at the coat of a young boy ascending the ramp. A mother followed, her raw, breathless shout tearing through the freezing air.

A guard advanced, driving the heavy stock of his rifle into the older man's ribs with a sickening, hollow thud. The mother was shoved backward, her knees hitting the blood-slicked ice. The line resumed its quiet march.

Ryba watched the blood pool in the slush as a clear line of sight framed the truck’s ramp. The freezing wind pressed against his face, demanding a reaction. The weeping mother and the dark mouth of the transport truck logged into Ryba’s periphery. The escalation unfolded. The exact space where a man might intervene lay bare, where a sudden shout or a thrown stone might give the parents a second chance. He simply shifted his weight, adjusted his heavy coat against the chill, and turned down a different street. His survival did not require him to agree with the empire. By choosing not to act, he participated perfectly.

He continued down the avenue until the sharp, unnatural glow of a storefront television cut through the gray light. It was mounted behind a thick pane of condensation-streaked glass, broadcasting a continuous, muted feed to the empty pavement.

Ryba did not merely glance as he walked past. He stopped. On the screen, a woman stood in the dock of a vast courtroom, her arms rigid at her sides, a black hood pulled tight over her head. Behind her, three judges sat before a wall of Russian tricolor and double-headed eagles. A ticker of Mandarin subtitles scrolled rapidly beneath the state logos, flashing the words: terrorist, foreign agent, and execution. The broadcast then cut abruptly to a courtyard of rubble and ash, flames rising higher and higher around the hooded woman’s frame.

Then, the screen blinked. A burst of static, and the courtroom again. The loop restarted. Ryba stood perfectly still on the freezing concrete, his hands buried deep in the pockets of his heavy coat. The first loop finished. Then the second. On the third, he looked away.

He had the yuan in his coat pocket, the paper edge cutting a faint line into his palm. He was a man entirely capable of witnessing the horrific, global consequences of his own transaction, and he actively chose not to integrate them. After the third loop concluded and the flames reset once more to the quiet courtroom, Ryba shifted his weight in his stolen Talons, turned his back to the glass, and continued walking.
He put the intersection behind him, letting the gray of the surrounding apartment blocks swallow his retreat. He tracked the edges, his heavy coat pulled tight against the biting wind.

The heavy wooden door of the tavern shut behind him, cutting off the biting wind but offering no true warmth. The air inside was thick with the scent of spilled vodka, stale tobacco, and the faint, unmistakable copper tang of dried blood. Ryba moved to the scarred wooden counter, his pristine Talons stepping quietly across the floorboards. The room was populated not by civilians, but by the discarded remnants of the front lines, dozens of former soldiers sitting in a quiet, static presence. To his left, a man with a pinned-up, empty sleeve looked into a half-empty glass, while another sat perfectly rigid, his posture locked by the phantom weight of a missing leg, highlighting a highly specific, unromanticized suffering.

Ryba's eyes moved past the veteran's pinned-up empty sleeve without slowing, checking the chalk-written price of vodka on the board behind him. He reached into his heavy coat and withdrew a crisp Chinese yuan note, sliding it flat across the damp wood of the bar.

Chairs stayed tucked beneath tables, voices remained low, and every hand stayed visible. The bartender’s gaze dropped to the yuan, then moved across the men watching from the tables. His hand shook as he reached out and took the bills.

Ryba took his purchase, sliding it into his pocket. He did not break the stare of the men watching him, nor did he offer any justification. He buttoned his coat precisely over a splatter of fresh blood on the floorboards without breaking his stride or glancing down.

Outside, the freezing air had deadened his nerves, and his fingertips were completely numb, the paper offering no warmth. The violence of the street spilled past the mouth of the alleyway in jagged, brutal fragments. A few meters behind Ryba, two Moscowvite revolutionaries dragged a figure over the freezing, blood-slicked concrete. Ryba immediately recognized the tailored wool overcoat from the bank, now torn open and soaked in the freezing mud. The businessman’s hands were bound tightly behind his back with heavy electrical wire, and a stained burlap sack had been yanked down over his head.

The violence arrived without ceremony. Ryba watched him dragged toward the stampede, the gaps between shoulders, and slipped deeper into the crowd. He turned into a narrow alley where the wind dropped away, but the lane ended against a sealed service gate.

A flicker of harsh, unnatural light caught the edge of his vision. Across the narrow passage, illuminated by a damaged, sparking junction box, was a mural painted roughly onto the decaying concrete. The icon of Elena’s face, rendered in cheap, slashing lines of black paint. She walked out from the stone with her demanding eyes.

Ryba stopped, and his eyes caught as he looked down at his stolen Talons, his hands starting to tremble. He had sold her to the empire for a stack of foreign currency, but in the transaction, he had accidentally forged a god. Her face was everywhere. The woman he betrayed now commanded the very forces tearing the city apart around him. He could never spend his money; he was a hunted man in a cage of his own making.

The icon’s weight demanded a reckoning, a collapse, a confession. Ryba provided none. He simply looked at the mural, turned away, and deliberately did not explain himself. The shoes of a king. He knelt in the freezing slush, his hands shaking slightly, and attempted one final time to pull the pristine laces tight. He smiled at nothing, at the street, at the cold, and his teeth showed in the reflection of the ice, jagged and unrepaired.

The soundscape of the collapsing city drowned out the quiet failure of his hands. The waspish whine of a surveillance drone persisted overhead, outlasting its own flight path. In the distance, the muffled hum of daily life echoed through the Moscow air.
